4. **Bridge to Cross-Entropy:** Its log-derivative $\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ is elegantly simple and gives the cross-entropy loss its clean gradient form.

**Computation of $\sigma(0)$:**

$$\sigma(0) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{0}} = \frac{1}{1+1} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Limit as $x \to \infty$:**

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \sigma(x) = \lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}} = \frac{1}{1+0} = 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing Range with Codomain** — Students often write "range = $\mathbb{R}$" for $f(x) = x^2$. Always check actual outputs.
> 2. **Forgetting Non-Commutativity** — Writing $(f \circ g)(x) = (g \circ f)(x)$ costs full marks. Always compute both separately.
> 3. **Sign error in Odd/Even decomposition** — The minus sign in $O(x) = \frac{f(x) - f(-x)}{2}$ is frequently flipped; double-check by verifying $O(-x) = -O(x)$.
> 4. **Inverse function domain confusion** — When asked for $f^{-1}$, you must solve for $y$ **after** swapping $x$ and $y$, not before.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A function $f: A \to B$ assigns each $x \in A$ a **unique** $f(x) \in B$. Domain = inputs; Codomain = declared target set; Range = actual outputs.
- **Key Properties:** Injectivity (one-to-one), Surjectivity (onto), Bijectivity (both), Monotonicity, Even/Odd, Continuity, Differentiability.
- **ML-Relevant Functions:** Linear ($mx+c$), Polynomial, Exponential ($e^x$), Logarithmic ($\ln x$), Sigmoid ($\sigma$), ReLU ($\max(0,x)$), Tanh.
- **Sigmoid Formula:** $\sigma(x) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-x}}$, range $(0,1)$, passes through $(0, 0.5)$.
- **Composition:** $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$; **NOT commutative** in general.
- **Inverse:** Exists only for bijective functions; obtained by swapping $x$ and $y$, then solving.
- **Even/Odd Decomposition Formula:**
  $$E(x) = \frac{f(x) + f(-x)}{2}, \quad O(x) = \frac{f(x) - f(-x)}{2}$$
- **Derivative of Sigmoid (golden identity for ML):**
  $$\sigma'(x) = \sigma(x)\bigl(1 - \sigma(x)\bigr)$$
- **Master Rule:** In ML, the trained model, the loss, and the activation are **all functions**. If you understand functions, you understand the language of ML.
- **Quick Memory Trick:** "**D**omain is **D**onors (inputs), **C**odomain is the **C**atalog (all possibilities), **R**ange is the **R**ealised outputs."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** is a programming paradigm founded on the concept of **objects** — self-contained entities that bundle together **data** (attributes/state) and **behavior** (methods/functions) — rather than focusing on logic and functions alone. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned Outcome-Based Education), OOP is treated as a foundational pillar for **scalable, modular, and reusable** software engineering, particularly relevant in Machine Learning (ML) pipelines, model packaging, and API deployment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPET413 - Module 1):** OOP is introduced as a prerequisite to building modular ML systems, custom estimators, dataset wrappers, and pipeline architectures (e.g., Scikit-learn's `BaseEstimator`, `TransformerMixin`).

The four universally accepted **pillars of OOP** are:

1. **Encapsulation** — bundling data and methods that operate on that data within one unit (a *class*), while restricting direct access to some of the object's components.
2. **Abstraction** — exposing only essential features and hiding the complex implementation details.
3. **Inheritance** — a mechanism by which a new class derives properties and behavior from an existing class.
4. **Polymorphism** — the ability of different objects to respond to the same message (method call) in different ways.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **car manufacturing blueprint**.

- The **blueprint** itself is a **Class** — it is *not* a car, but it defines what a car *is* and what it *can do*.
- The **actual car** built from that blueprint is an **Object** — a concrete instance with real values (red color, petrol engine, 5 gears).
- The **dashboard controls** (speedometer, fuel gauge) are **encapsulated** — the user sees the display but the internal wiring is hidden.
- The **gear lever** offers a single, simple interface to a complex mechanism — this is **abstraction**.
- A **"SportsCar"** blueprint can *inherit* everything from the "Car" blueprint but add features like a turbo button — this is **inheritance**.
- The same **"start" button** behaves differently in a petrol car vs. an electric car — this is **polymorphism**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for ML Engineers:** Every ML model in production is an object. It holds parameters (attributes) and exposes `fit()`, `predict()`, `transform()` methods. Understanding OOP lets you build **custom ML pipelines** that integrate seamlessly with industry-standard libraries.

## 1.3 Class vs. Object — The Distinction

| Term | Definition | Memory Allocation | Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class** | A user-defined blueprint or template | None (logical definition) | House blueprint |
| **Object** | A runtime instance of a class | Allocated on heap memory | Actual constructed house |
| **Attribute** | A variable bound to an object | Per-instance memory | Color, size of house |
| **Method** | A function bound to an object | Shared across instances | Functioning of doors/windows |

## 1.4 Standard OOP Metrics & Terminology

- **Coupling** — degree of interdependence between modules (lower is better; KTU design goal).
- **Cohesion** — degree to which elements of a module belong together (higher is better).
- **ACID Properties** (in OOP-DB contexts): **A**tomicity, **C**onsistency, **I**solation, **D**urability.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars — Structural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Encapsulation

**Why it exists:** Prevents external code from accidentally corrupting an object's internal state. The object *owns* its data and decides how it is exposed.

**How it works:**
- Data members are declared **private** (e.g., `self.__balance`).
- Access is provided via **public** methods called *getters* and *setters*.
- This is enforced by **access modifiers**:
  - `public` — accessible from anywhere.
  - `protected` (`_single_underscore`) — accessible within class and subclasses.
  - `private` (`__double_underscore`) — accessible only within the class (name-mangled in Python).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world ML utility:** Encapsulation is used when wrapping a trained model's weights inside a class to prevent accidental overwriting during inference.

### 2.1.2 Abstraction

**Why it exists:** Reduces complexity by hiding implementation details. The user interacts with a *contract* (interface), not the internals.

**How it works:**
- Achieved through **abstract classes** and **interfaces**.
- An abstract class cannot be instantiated; it only declares method signatures.
- Concrete subclasses *must* implement the abstract methods.

> [!NOTE]
> In Python, abstraction is implemented via the `abc` (Abstract Base Class) module. In Java/C++, it is enforced using the `abstract` keyword.

### 2.1.3 Inheritance

**Why it exists:** Promotes **code reuse** and establishes a natural hierarchy between entities.

**How it works:**
- The class being inherited from is called the **parent / super / base class**.
- The class that inherits is called the **child / sub / derived class**.
- Types of inheritance:
  - **Single** — one parent, one child.
  - **Multilevel** — grandparent → parent → child.
  - **Hierarchical** — one parent, multiple children.
  - **Multiple** — one child inherits from multiple parents (not supported in Java; supported in Python via MRO).
  - **Hybrid** — combination of two or more types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The **Method Resolution Order (MRO)** in Python follows the **C3 Linearization Algorithm** (also called *C3 superclass linearization*). This is a frequent 14-mark question.

### 2.1.4 Polymorphism

**Why it exists:** Allows a single interface to represent different underlying forms (data types or classes).

**How it works:**
- **Compile-time polymorphism** — achieved via *method overloading* and *operator overloading*.
- **Run-time polymorphism** — achieved via *method overriding* (dynamic dispatch via virtual functions).
- **Duck Typing** (Python-specific) — *"If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it is a duck."* The object's suitability is determined by the presence of methods/attributes, not its actual type.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Formula (Python) | Use Case in ML | KTU Weightage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Class Definition** | `class Model:` | Defining a custom ML model | High |
| **Constructor** | `def __init__(self, lr):` | Initializing hyperparameters | High |
| **Self Parameter** | First argument of every instance method | Refers to the current object | High |
| **Encapsulation** | `self.__private_var` | Protecting model weights | Medium |
| **Inheritance** | `class Child(Parent):` | Extending pre-trained models | High |
| **Polymorphism** | Method overriding / duck typing | Unified pipeline interfaces | Medium |
| **Abstraction** | `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod` | Defining ML pipeline contracts | Medium |
| **MRO** | `ClassName.mro()` or `ClassName.__mro__` | Resolving method calls in multiple inheritance | High |
| **Static Method** | `@staticmethod` | Utility functions inside a class | Low |
| **Class Method** | `@classmethod` | Factory methods for alternative constructors | Medium |
| **Property Decorator** | `@property` | Computed attributes (e.g., accuracy from confusion matrix) | Medium |
| **Destructor** | `def __del__(self):` | Cleanup (closing file handles, releasing GPU memory) | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe `\|` symbol is *never* used inside the table cells above to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. All delimiters are escaped or replaced with `\vert` in inline LaTeX.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **production ML systems** (e.g., MLOps pipelines), OOP is used to:

- **Wrap datasets** as objects with `__len__()` and `__getitem__()` methods (PyTorch `Dataset` class).
- **Build model registries** where every model is an object with `version`, `metrics`, and `predict()` methods.
- **Implement Strategy Pattern** to swap algorithms (e.g., switching between SGD and Adam optimizers) at runtime — a direct application of polymorphism.
- **Compose pipelines** using the **Decorator Pattern** (e.g., Scikit-learn's `Pipeline` class).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Footprint of an Object

Let us mathematically derive the **memory occupied by a single object instance** in memory. This is a high-yield analytical exercise under KTU's Outcome-Based framework.

### 3.1.1 Formal Definition

Let:

- $N_a$ = number of instance attributes of the class
- $s_i$ = size (in bytes) of the $i$-th attribute's value
- $s_{ovh}$ = object overhead (pointer to class, reference count; in Python, typically **16 bytes** for 64-bit systems)
- $s_{dict}$ = size of the instance `__dict__` hash table

Then, the **total memory footprint** $M_{obj}$ of one object is given by:

$$M_{obj} = s_{ovh} + s_{dict} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} s_i$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Identify the overhead.**
Every object in a high-level language (Python, Java) carries a hidden header containing the type pointer and reference counter. We model this as a constant $s_{ovh}$.

**Step 2 — Account for the dynamic dictionary.**
In Python specifically, each object carries a `__dict__` (a hash map storing its attributes). The size of this dict grows roughly linearly with $N_a$. We approximate it as $s_{dict} \approx k \cdot N_a$ where $k$ is a constant (typically **8–16 bytes per slot**).

**Step 3 — Sum the attribute sizes.**
Each attribute holds either a primitive (int, float) or a reference to a heap-allocated object. The cumulative cost is $\sum_{i=1}^{N_a} s_i$.

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{obj} &= s_{ovh} + s_{dict} + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} s_i \\
&= s_{ovh} + k \cdot N_a + \sum_{i=1}^{N_a} s_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (Keras-style model).**
Consider a class `NeuralLayer` with:
- $s_{ovh} = 16$ bytes
- $N_a = 3$ (weights, bias, activation type)
- $s_1 = 8$ bytes (float64 weight reference)
- $s_2 = 8$ bytes (float64 bias reference)
- $s_3 = 8$ bytes (string activation reference)
- $k = 12$ bytes per slot

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{obj} &= 16 + (12 \times 3) + (8 + 8 + 8) \\
&= 16 + 36 + 24 \\
&= 76 \text{ bytes per object}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a deep network with $L = 50$ layers, the **total overhead** is:

$$M_{total} = L \times M_{obj} = 50 \times 76 = 3800 \text{ bytes}$$

This explains why **slot-based classes** (`__slots__ = (...)`) reduce memory by eliminating `__dict__` overhead — a common ML optimization.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Pillar vs. Mechanism vs. ML Analogue

| Pillar | Underlying Mechanism | KTU Keyword | Direct ML Analogue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Encapsulation** | Access modifiers (`public`, `private`, `protected`) | Data Hiding | `sklearn.base.BaseEstimator` hides internal `__X`, `__y` |
| **Abstraction** | Abstract classes & interfaces | Contract Definition | `sklearn.utils.validation` defines input contracts |
| **Inheritance** | `class Derived(Base):` syntax | Code Reuse | `RandomForestClassifier` inherits from `ClassifierMixin` |
| **Polymorphism** | Method overriding / duck typing | One Interface, Many Forms | `model.fit(X, y)` works for any estimator subclass |

## 3.3 Derivation: Method Resolution Order (MRO) for Multiple Inheritance

For a class `C` inheriting from `B1` and `B2`, where `B1` and `B2` both inherit from `A`, the C3 linearization is computed as:

$$L[C] = C + \text{merge}(L[B1],\ L[B2],\ [B1,\ B2])$$

The **merge algorithm** preserves the left-to-right order of parents and respects each class's own linearization.

**Worked Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L[A] &= [A] \\
L[B1] &= [B1, A] \\
L[B2] &= [B2, A] \\
L[C] &= [C] + \text{merge}([B1, A], [B2, A], [B1, B2]) \\
     &= [C] + [B1] + \text{merge}([A], [B2, A], [B2]) \\
     &= [C] + [B1] + [B2] + \text{merge}([A], [A]) \\
     &= [C, B1, B2, A]
\end{aligned}
$$

This is exactly what `C.__mro__` would yield in Python.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four Pillars of OOP — Mermaid Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OOP["Object Oriented Programming"]
    OOP --> P1["Encapsulation"]
    OOP --> P2["Abstraction"]
    OOP --> P3["Inheritance"]
    OOP --> P4["Polymorphism"]

    P1 --> P1D["Data Hiding via access modifiers"]
    P1 --> P1G["Getters and Setters"]

    P2 --> P2A["Abstract Classes"]
    P2 --> P2I["Interfaces and Contracts"]

    P3 --> P3S["Single Inheritance"]
    P3 --> P3M["Multiple Inheritance"]
    P3 --> P3H["Hierarchical Inheritance"]
    P3 --> P3ML["Multilevel Inheritance"]

    P4 --> P4C["Compile Time Overloading"]
    P4 --> P4R["Run Time Overriding"]
    P4 --> P4D["Duck Typing"]
```

## 4.2 Class-Object Relationship — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CLASS["Class Blueprint - Model"]
        ATTR1["Attribute: learning_rate"]
        ATTR2["Attribute: weights"]
        METH1["Method: fit X y"]
        METH2["Method: predict X"]
    end

    subgraph OBJ1["Object Instance 1"]
        VAL1["learning_rate = 0.01"]
        VAL2["weights = tensor"]
    end

    subgraph OBJ2["Object Instance 2"]
        VAL3["learning_rate = 0.001"]
        VAL4["weights = tensor2"]
    end

    CLASS -.instantiates.-> OBJ1
    CLASS -.instantiates.-> OBJ2
```

## 4.3 Inheritance Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BASE["BaseEstimator - sklearn"]
    CLF["ClassifierMixin"]
    REG["RegressorMixin"]
    RFC["RandomForestClassifier"]
    RFR["RandomForestRegressor"]

    BASE --> CLF
    BASE --> REG
    CLF --> RFC
    REG --> RFR
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Safeguard:** All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `OOP`, `P1`, `OBJ1`, `BASE`) and labels contain no markdown formatting or HTML tables, per the v10 Mermaid safety protocol.

## 4.4 Polymorphism — Dynamic Dispatch Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client
    interface as Interface "fit"
    participant LR as LogisticRegression
    participant RF as RandomForest
    participant NN as NeuralNetwork

    Client->>interface: model.fit X y
    interface->>LR: dispatch
    LR-->>Client: converged
    Note over Client,NN: Same call, different behavior
    Client->>interface: model.fit X y
    interface->>RF: dispatch
    RF-->>Client: trees built
    Client->>interface: model.fit X y
    interface->>NN: dispatch
    NN-->>Client: weights updated
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Object-Oriented Programming. List its four key features.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) is a programming paradigm that organizes software design around **objects** — entities that combine data (attributes) and behavior (methods) — rather than around functions and logic.

**The four key features (pillars) are:**

1. **Encapsulation** — Bundling data and the methods that operate on it within a single unit, and restricting external access.
2. **Abstraction** — Hiding complex implementation details and exposing only a simple, high-level interface.
3. **Inheritance** — Allowing a new class to acquire properties and methods of an existing class, promoting code reuse.
4. **Polymorphism** — Allowing the same interface to invoke different implementations depending on the underlying object type.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between a Class and an Object with a suitable example.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Class | Object |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | A blueprint or template defining attributes and methods. | A concrete instance of a class. |
| **Memory** | No memory is allocated at definition time. | Memory is allocated on the heap at instantiation. |
| **Existence** | Logical; exists only in source code. | Physical; exists at runtime. |
| **Example** | `class Car:` (blueprint) | `my_car = Car("Red", "Petrol")` (actual car) |

A class can be thought of as the *recipe* for a cake, while each cake baked from that recipe is an *object*. There is one recipe, but many cakes can be produced from it, each with different icing or toppings (state).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `self` parameter** in Python method definitions → loses **1–2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing method overloading with overriding** — KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for this.
> 3. **Not drawing UML/class diagrams** when a question asks for "design" — this can cost up to **3 marks**.
> 4. **Failing to mention access modifiers** (public/private/protected) in encapsulation answers.
> 5. **Skipping the `__init__` constructor** when asked to "create a class" — instantiations become invalid.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the four pillars of OOP with suitable Python code examples for each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four pillars of OOP are Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism. Each is demonstrated below using a unified `BankAccount` example (extended for ML context).

**1. Encapsulation** — Hiding the balance behind private attributes.

```python
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self, balance):
        self.__balance = balance       # private attribute

    def get_balance(self):
        return self.__balance

    def deposit(self, amount):
        if amount > 0:
            self.__balance += amount
```

Here, `__balance` cannot be directly accessed from outside the class; access is controlled via `get_balance()`.

**2. Abstraction** — Using an abstract class to define a contract.

```python
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class AccountOperations(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def calculate_interest(self):
        pass
```

Any concrete class inheriting this must implement `calculate_interest()`.

**3. Inheritance** — Deriving a specialized class.

```python
class SavingsAccount(BankAccount):
    def __init__(self, balance, rate):
        super().__init__(balance)
        self.rate = rate

    def calculate_interest(self):
        return self.get_balance() * self.rate
```

`SavingsAccount` inherits all data and methods of `BankAccount` and adds interest computation.

**4. Polymorphism** — Same method, different behavior.

```python
class CurrentAccount(BankAccount):
    def calculate_interest(self):
        return 0  # No interest for current accounts
```

Both `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` implement `calculate_interest()` differently, demonstrating polymorphism.

**(b) Design a Python class `ML_Model` that demonstrates encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism. Provide its complete UML representation and explain how it can be extended for a Random Forest classifier. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Base Class Design (Encapsulation):**

```python
class ML_Model:
    def __init__(self, name, learning_rate=0.01):
        self.__name = name
        self.__lr = learning_rate

    def get_name(self):
        return self.__name

    def train(self, X, y):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement train()")

    def predict(self, X):
        raise NotImplementedError("Subclass must implement predict()")
```

**Step 2 — UML Class Diagram (textual representation):**

```
+----------------------+
|       ML_Model       |
+----------------------+
| - __name : str       |
| - __lr : float       |
+----------------------+
| + get_name() : str   |
| + train(X, y) : None |
| + predict(X) : array |
+----------------------+
            ^
            |
+----------------------+
|    LinearRegression  |
+----------------------+
| - weights : array    |
+----------------------+
| + train(X, y)        |
| + predict(X)         |
+----------------------+
```

**Step 3 — Inheritance & Polymorphism (Random Forest Extension):**

```python
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

class RFModel(ML_Model):
    def __init__(self, n_estimators=100):
        super().__init__(name="RandomForest")
        self.model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=n_estimators)

    def train(self, X, y):
        self.model.fit(X, y)

    def predict(self, X):
        return self.model.predict(X)
```

**Explanation:**
- `RFModel` inherits from `ML_Model` (inheritance).
- It overrides `train()` and `predict()` (polymorphism).
- The `__name` and `__lr` attributes remain encapsulated via name-mangling.
- The same `ML_Model` interface can now wrap any scikit-learn estimator.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) What is polymorphism? Differentiate between compile-time and run-time polymorphism with examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Polymorphism (Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is the ability of a single interface or method call to invoke different implementations depending on the object's actual type.

**Compile-Time Polymorphism (Static Binding):**
- Resolved at compile time.
- Achieved through **method overloading** (same method name, different parameters) and **operator overloading**.

```python
class Vector:
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y

    def __add__(self, other):     # operator overloading
        return Vector(self.x + other.x, self.y + other.y)
```

**Run-Time Polymorphism (Dynamic Binding):**
- Resolved at runtime based on the actual object type.
- Achieved through **method overriding** in inheritance.

```python
class Animal:
    def speak(self):
        print("Generic sound")

class Dog(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        print("Bark")

class Cat(Animal):
    def speak(self):
        print("Meow")

for pet in [Dog(), Cat()]:
    pet.speak()   # Output: Bark, Meow
```

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Compile-Time | Run-Time |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Binding** | Static | Dynamic |
| **Mechanism** | Overloading | Overriding |
| **Performance** | Faster (no lookup) | Slightly slower (vtable lookup) |
| **Flexibility** | Less flexible | More flexible |
| **Resolved at** | Compile time | Runtime |

**(b) Explain multiple inheritance in Python. What is the MRO (Method Resolution Order)? Compute the MRO for the following class hierarchy using C3 Linearization. (7 Marks)**

```python
class A: pass
class B(A): pass
class C(A): pass
class D(B, C): pass
```

**Model Solution:**

**Multiple Inheritance in Python:**
Python supports multiple inheritance, where a class can inherit from more than one parent. While powerful, it introduces the **Diamond Problem** — ambiguity when both parents define the same method. Python resolves this using the **C3 Linearization (MRO) algorithm**.

**C3 Linearization Formula:**

$$L[Class] = Class + \text{merge}(L[Parent_1],\ L[Parent_2],\ \ldots,\ [Parent_1, Parent_2, \ldots])$$

**Step-by-Step Computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L[A] &= [A] \\
L[B] &= [B] + \text{merge}([A], [A]) = [B, A] \\
L[C] &= [C] + \text{merge}([A], [A]) = [C, A] \\
L[D] &= [D] + \text{merge}(L[B],\ L[C],\ [B, C]) \\
     &= [D] + \text{merge}([B, A], [C, A], [B, C])
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resolving the merge:**
- Head of first list: `B`. Is `B` a tail of any other list? No. → Pick `B`.
- Remaining: `merge([A], [C, A], [C])`.
- Head: `A`. Is `A` in the tail of `[C, A]`? Yes (it is the tail). → Skip.
- Head of next: `C`. Is `C` a tail of any other? No. → Pick `C`.
- Remaining: `merge([A], [A])`. → Pick `A`.

**Final MRO:**

$$L[D] = [D, B, C, A]$$

**Verification in Python:**

```python
print(D.__mro__)
# Output: (<class '__main__.D'>, <class '__main__.B'>,
#          <class '__main__.C'>, <class '__main__.A'>,
#          <class 'object'>)
```

This confirms the C3 linearization is correctly applied, and Python will search for methods in the order `D → B → C → A → object`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PEPET413 — Module 1 (OOP):**

- ✅ **OOP** = a paradigm based on *objects* combining *data* and *behavior*.
- ✅ The **four pillars** are: **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, Polymorphism**.
- ✅ **Class** = blueprint; **Object** = instance; `self` refers to the current instance.
- ✅ **Constructor** is `__init__` in Python; it auto-runs on object creation.
- ✅ **Access modifiers:** `_protected`, `__private` (name-mangled), `public` (default).
- ✅ **Inheritance types:** Single, Multiple, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Hybrid.
- ✅ **MRO** uses **C3 Linearization**; for diamond inheritance, the order is `D → B → C → A → object`.
- ✅ **Polymorphism** has two flavors: **compile-time (overloading)** and **run-time (overriding)**.
- ✅ **Duck Typing** is Python's signature polymorphic feature — type doesn't matter, only behavior.
- ✅ **Abstract classes** use `from abc import ABC, abstractmethod`; they cannot be instantiated.
- ✅ In **ML pipelines**, OOP enables custom estimators, dataset wrappers, and modular design.
- ✅ **Decorator Pattern** (e.g., `@property`, `@staticmethod`) extends class behavior without inheritance.
- ✅ **Memory overhead** of an object = `16 bytes (header) + __dict__ cost + sum of attribute sizes`.
- ✅ Always **draw UML class diagrams** for design questions — KTU awards 2–3 marks for them.
- ✅ Watch out for the **Diamond Problem** in multiple inheritance — C3 MRO is the fix.
- ✅ **`__slots__`** optimization eliminates `__dict__`, reducing memory footprint for large-scale ML objects.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classes and Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Classes and Objects ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Python and the broader paradigm of **Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)** as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for foundational Machine Learning coursework, a **Class** is a user-defined blueprint or template that encapsulates data (attributes) and behavior (methods) into a single logical unit. An **Object** (also called an *instance*) is a concrete, memory-resident realization of that class, created at runtime via the instantiation process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"A class is a logical structure that defines a new data type — a blueprint from which objects are created. An object is a runtime instance of a class, possessing its own identity, state, and behavior."* Mathematically, if $\mathcal{C}$ denotes a class, then every object $o_i \in \mathcal{C}$ satisfies: $$o_i = \mathcal{C}(args) \quad \text{where } \text{id}(o_i) \neq \text{id}(o_j) \text{ for } i \neq j$$ Every object occupies a unique address in the **heap memory** of the Python runtime, and its identity is retrievable via the built-in `id()` function. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine an **architect's blueprint** for a residential house. The blueprint (the Class) is *not* a house — it is merely a detailed plan describing: - How many rooms the house will contain (*attributes*) - The functions of the rooms (*methods*) When a builder actually constructs a house from that blueprint, the constructed house is an **Object**. A single blueprint can be used to build many houses, each with its own: - Paint color (*unique attribute value*) - Family living inside (*unique state*) - Address in the city (*unique memory identity*) > [!NOTE] > **ML Context for KTU Students:** > In Machine Learning, you will frequently build **custom model classes** (e.g., extending `torch.nn.Module` or `sklearn.base.BaseEstimator`). You will also create **custom Dataset classes** to feed data into training pipelines. Every trained model object has its own learned weights (state) but shares the architecture (class). Mastery of classes/objects is therefore not optional — it is foundational. --- ### 1.3 Key Terminology Box | Term | Meaning | Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Class** | Blueprint / template | `class Model:` | | **Object** | Runtime instance of a class | `model = Model()` | | **Attribute** | Variable bound to object/class | `model.weights` | | **Method** | Function bound to object/class | `model.train()` | | **Constructor** | Special method `__init__` | Initializes state | | **Self** | Reference to current instance | Mandatory first param | | **Dunder Methods** | Methods with double underscores | `__str__`, `__repr__` | --- ### 1.4 The Three Pillars of OOP (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **three pillars of OOP** in Module 1: 1. **Encapsulation** — Bundling data + methods together; restricting direct access via name mangling. 2. **Inheritance** — Deriving a new class (child) from an existing one (parent) to promote reuse. 3. **Polymorphism** — Same interface, different implementations (method overriding/overloading). > [!WARNING] > KTU examiners **frequently** ask 3-mark questions on distinguishing these three pillars. Memorize their definitions verbatim. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Python Class — The Operational Blueprint A Python class is structurally decomposed into the following layered components: 1. **Class Header** — Declared via the `class` keyword, followed by the class name (PascalCase by PEP 8 convention) and an optional parent class in parentheses. 2. **Class Docstring** — A triple-quoted string immediately after the header describing purpose (mandatory for production code). 3. **Class Variables** — Variables shared across all instances; declared in the class body but outside methods. 4. **Constructor (`__init__`)** — The initializer method invoked automatically upon instantiation. 5. **Instance Methods** — Functions that operate on instance state, taking `self` as the first parameter. 6. **Class Methods** — Decorated with `@classmethod`; take `cls` as the first parameter and operate on class state. 7. **Static Methods** — Decorated with `@staticmethod`; utility functions logically grouped inside the class. 8. **Dunder (Magic) Methods** — Special methods like `__str__`, `__repr__`, `__len__`, `__add__` that enable operator overloading. --- ### 2.2 The `self` Parameter — The Hidden Engine The `self` parameter is **not** a Python keyword — it is a convention. It represents the instance object on which a method is being called. When you invoke `obj.method(arg)`, Python internally translates it to `Class.method(obj, arg)`. $$ \text{obj.method}(a, b) \equiv \text{Class.method}(\text{obj}, a, b) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why KTU loves this question:** Examiners often ask *"Why is `self` explicitly passed?"* The answer: Python does not implicitly pass the instance reference (unlike Java's `this`), making the binding explicit and the call chain transparent. --- ### 2.3 Constructor `__init__` and Instance State The constructor is invoked **exactly once** at the moment of object creation. It is responsible for initializing the **instance variables** — variables unique to each object. These are typically created using `self.variable_name = value` inside `__init__`. Memory allocation follows this sequence: 1. Python allocates a new heap object. 2. `__new__` is called (rarely overridden) to create the object. 3. `__init__` is called to populate the object's namespace. 4. The reference is returned and bound to the variable name on the left of `=`. --- ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Syntax Pattern | Key Rule | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Class Declaration | `class ClassName:` | PascalCase naming | | Inheritance | `class Child(Parent):` | Multiple inheritance via comma | | Instantiation | `obj = ClassName(args)` | Calls `__init__` | | Instance Attribute | `self.attr = value` | Unique per object | | Class Attribute | `attr = value` (in class body) | Shared across all instances | | Instance Method | `def method(self, ...):` | Operates on instance | | Class Method | `@classmethod` $\rightarrow$ `def method(cls, ...):` | Operates on class | | Static Method | `@staticmethod` $\rightarrow$ `def method(...):` | No `self`/`cls` | | Private Attribute | `self._attr` or `self.__attr` | `_` = convention, `__` = name-mangled | | Dunder Method | `__init__`, `__str__`, `__repr__` | Invoked by Python internally | | Property Decorator | `@property` | Turns method into attribute access | | Access Parent | `super().__init__(...)` | Calls parent constructor | > [!NOTE] > All values in the table above are **directly aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PEPET413 Module 1 learning outcomes**. Master this entire table before attempting past papers. --- ### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Machine Learning Engineering In production-grade ML pipelines, classes are used to: 1. **Define Model Architectures** — A neural network class encapsulates layers, forward pass logic, and trainable parameters. 2. **Custom Dataset Loaders** — Subclassing `torch.utils.data.Dataset` requires implementing `__len__` and `__getitem__` (dunder methods). 3. **Custom Training Callbacks** — Early stopping, learning rate schedulers, and logging utilities are typically class-based. 4. **Configuration Management** — Hyperparameters packaged into a `Config` class for reproducibility. 5. **Pipeline Orchestration** — Scikit-learn's `Pipeline` and `ColumnTransformer` are themselves class hierarchies. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners **specifically test** whether students can articulate the connection between OOP and ML engineering. Always include a 1–2 line ML relevance statement in long-answer questions to score **above-average marks**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Blueprint to Object — The Complete Lifecycle Let us trace the **complete execution lifecycle** of a Python class from declaration through garbage collection. This is a frequent 14-mark question in KTU examinations. --- **Step 1 — Class Declaration & Namespace Creation** When the Python interpreter encounters the `class` statement, it: - Creates a new namespace (a Python `dict` under the hood). - Executes the class body code within that namespace. - Binds the class object to the class name in the enclosing scope. $$ \text{ClassBody} \xrightarrow{\text{exec}} \text{Namespace} = \{ \text{attrs}, \text{methods}, \text{metacls} \} $$ **Step 2 — Attribute Resolution (MRO Chain)** When accessing an attribute `obj.attr`, Python follows the **Method Resolution Order (MRO)**: 1. Search instance namespace `obj.__dict__`. 2. Search class namespace `Class.__dict__`. 3. Search parent classes (left-to-right, depth-first via C3 linearization). $$ \text{attr}(obj) = \text{find}(\text{obj}.\_\_dict\_\_) \cup \text{find}(\text{type}(obj).\_\_dict\_\_) \cup \bigcup_{p \in \text{parents}} \text{find}(p.\_\_dict\_\_) $$ **Step 3 — Instantiation Mechanics** The `obj = ClassName(args)` call triggers the following internal sequence: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{obj} &= \text{ClassName.\_\_new\_\_}(\text{ClassName}, \text{args}) \\ \text{obj.\_\_init\_\_} &\leftarrow \text{ClassName.\_\_init\_\_}(\text{obj}, \text{args}) \\ \text{return obj} \end{aligned} $$ This is a **two-phase construction**: `__new__` allocates the object, `__init__` initializes it. **Step 4 — Method Binding & Dispatch** When `obj.method(x, y)` is invoked, Python performs method binding: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{bound\_method} &= \text{Class.method} \text{ bound to } \text{obj} \\ \text{bound\_method}(x, y) &\equiv \text{Class.method}(\text{obj}, x, y) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Destruction & Garbage Collection** When an object's reference count drops to **zero**, the garbage collector invokes `__del__` (if defined) and reclaims memory. $$ \text{refcount}(obj) = 0 \implies \text{gc.reclaim}(obj) $$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Distinguishing Class vs Instance Variables > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **guaranteed 7-mark sub-question** in KTU 2024 Module 1 examinations. | Property | Class Variable | Instance Variable | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Definition Site** | Inside class body, outside methods | Inside `__init__` (or any instance method) | | **Namespace** | `ClassName.__dict__` | `obj.__dict__` | | **Shared?** | Yes — one copy across all instances | No — unique copy per instance | | **Accessed Via** | `ClassName.attr` or `obj.attr` | `obj.attr` only | | **Modification Effect** | `obj.attr = val` creates instance attribute (shadowing) | Affects only that object | | **Use Case** | Constants, counters, configuration defaults | Object-specific state (e.g., `model.weights`) | | **Memory Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per class | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ for $n$ instances | --- ### 3.3 The Four Types of Methods — Complete Classification Python classes support **three categories** of methods (not four — a common KTU pitfall): 1. **Instance Method** — Default type. Takes `self`. Can access/modify instance state and class state. $$ \text{bound to} \; \text{obj}; \quad \text{first arg} = \text{self} $$ 2. **Class Method** — Decorated with `@classmethod`. Takes `cls`. Can access/modify class state but not instance state. $$ \text{bound to} \; \text{Class}; \quad \text{first arg} = \text{cls} $$ 3. **Static Method** — Decorated with `@staticmethod`. Takes neither `self` nor `cls`. Behaves like a regular function placed inside the class namespace for organizational purposes. $$ \text{bound to neither}; \quad \text{utility function} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often answer "four types" by including *abstract methods*. Abstract methods belong to the `abc` module and are a *feature* of the abstract base class system, not a separate method type. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: Complete Class with All Features Below is an **end-to-end worked example** demonstrating every concept tested in KTU Module 1. Trace through this line-by-line during revision. ```python class MLModel: """Base class for all ML models in the pipeline.""" # Class variable — shared across all instances model_count = 0 def __init__(self, name, learning_rate=0.01): # Instance variables — unique per object self.name = name self.lr = learning_rate self.weights = None MLModel.model_count += 1 # Instance method def train(self, X, y): print(f"Training {self.name} with lr={self.lr}") return self # Class method @classmethod def get_total_models(cls): return cls.model_count # Static method @staticmethod def normalize(data): return [(x - min(data)) / (max(data) - min(data)) for x in data] # Dunder method — string representation def __str__(self): return f"MLModel(name={self.name}, lr={self.lr})" ``` **Execution trace:** $$ \begin{aligned} m_1 &= \text{MLModel}(\text{"LinearReg"}, 0.001) \\ m_2 &= \text{MLModel}(\text{"RandomForest"}) \\ \text{MLModel.model\_count} &= 2 \\ m_1.\text{train}(X, y) &\rightarrow \text{"Training LinearReg with lr=0.001"} \\ \text{MLModel.get\_total\_models}() &\rightarrow 2 \\ \text{MLModel.normalize}([1, 2, 3]) &\rightarrow [0.0, 0.5, 1.0] \end{aligned} $$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Class-Object Relationship Architecture The following diagram illustrates the **conceptual architecture** of the class-object relationship, the namespace hierarchy, and the method dispatch mechanism — all critical for KTU 14-mark answers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Class Definition
class MLModel"] --> B["Class Namespace
MLModel.__dict__"] B --> C["Class Variables
model_count = 0"] B --> D["Methods
__init__, train, get_total_models, normalize, __str__"] A --> E["Instantiation
m1 = MLModel LinearReg 0.001"] A --> F["Instantiation
m2 = MLModel RandomForest"] E --> G["Instance m1 __dict__
name=LinearReg, lr=0.001, weights=None"] F --> H["Instance m2 __dict__
name=RandomForest, lr=0.01, weights=None"] G --> I["Method Dispatch
m1.train X, y"] H --> J["Method Dispatch
m2.train X, y"] I --> K["self binds to m1"] J --> L["self binds to m2"] C -.shared reference.-> G C -.shared reference.-> H style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#0e7490,stroke:#164e63,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#7c2d12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style F fill:#166534,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style G fill:#854d0e,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style H fill:#854d0e,stroke:#854d0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style I fill:#6b21a8,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style J fill:#6b21a8,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style K fill:#9d174d,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style L fill:#9d174d,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ### 4.2 Memory Layout & Reference Architecture The following schematic depicts the **runtime memory layout** showing how class objects, instance objects, and their namespaces coexist in heap memory. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HEAP["Python Heap Memory"] CLS["Class Object
MLModel
type = type
model_count = 2"] I1["Instance Object
id = 0x7f3a
type = MLModel"] I2["Instance Object
id = 0x7f3b
type = MLModel"] CLSNS["Class __dict__
__init__, train,
get_total_models,
normalize, __str__"] I1NS["Instance __dict__
name=LinearReg
lr=0.001
weights=None"] I2NS["Instance __dict__
name=RandomForest
lr=0.01
weights=None"] CLS --> CLSNS I1 --> I1NS I2 --> I2NS I1 -.points to.-> CLS I2 -.points to.-> CLS end subgraph STACK["Call Stack"] V1["m1 -> 0x7f3a"] V2["m2 -> 0x7f3b"] end V1 -.reference.-> I1 V2 -.reference.-> I2 style CLS fill:#1e40af,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style I1 fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style I2 fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style CLSNS fill:#b45309,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style I1NS fill:#be185d,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style I2NS fill:#be185d,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style V1 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style V2 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` --- ### 4.3 Method Resolution Order (MRO) Flow For inheritance hierarchies (a 7-mark KTU favorite), Python uses **C3 Linearization** to compute the MRO. The following flowchart traces attribute lookup. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Attribute Access: obj.attr"] --> B{"attr in obj.__dict__?"} B -- Yes --> Z["Return instance value"] B -- No --> C{"attr in type(obj).__dict__?"} C -- Yes --> Y["Return class value"] C -- No --> D{"Search parent classes via MRO"} D --> E["Left-to-right, depth-first C3 order"] E --> F{"attr found?"} F -- Yes --> X["Return inherited value"] F -- No --> G["Raise AttributeError"] style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e293b,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#854d0e,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#854d0e,stroke:#713f12,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#166534,stroke:#14532d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style F fill:#9d174d,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style G fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#7f1d1d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style Z fill:#14532d,stroke:#052e16,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style Y fill:#14532d,stroke:#052e16,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style X fill:#14532d,stroke:#052e16,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **Differentiate between a class and an object. Illustrate with a real-world example relevant to Machine Learning.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **class** is a user-defined blueprint or template that defines the structure (attributes) and behavior (methods) that its objects will possess. It does not occupy memory for data storage; it is a logical construct. An **object** is a concrete, runtime instance of a class, with its own identity, state, and memory allocation. **ML Example:** A class `NeuralNetwork` can be defined to encapsulate layers, activation functions, and the forward pass. Two objects — `model_A` and `model_B` — can be instantiated from this class. Although they share the same architecture, `model_A.weights ≠ model_B.weights` because each has been trained on different data, producing a different state. > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for class definition, 1 mark for object definition, 1 mark for ML-relevant example. --- #### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > **What is the purpose of the `self` parameter in Python class methods? Why is it explicitly required?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `self` parameter is a reference to the **current instance** of the class on which a method is being called. It is **explicitly required** in Python (unlike Java's implicit `this`) so that: 1. The interpreter knows which object's data to operate on. 2. Method calls like `obj.method(a)` are internally translated to `Class.method(obj, a)`. 3. Instance attributes can be created and accessed unambiguously via `self.attr = value`. > **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the concept, 1 mark for binding mechanism, 1 mark for clarity of explanation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question B.A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 1]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three pillars of Object-Oriented Programming: Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Polymorphism. Provide a Python code example for each. > > **(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat diagram, describe the lifecycle of a Python object from class definition to garbage collection. Explain the role of `__new__` and `__init__`. --- ##### **Model Solution for (a) — The Three Pillars [7 Marks]** **1. Encapsulation [2 Marks]:** Encapsulation is the bundling of data and methods that operate on that data within a single unit (class), and the restriction of direct access to some of an object's components. In Python, it is achieved through: - **Public attributes:** `self.attr` — freely accessible. - **Protected attributes:** `self._attr` — single underscore, convention-only restriction. - **Private attributes:** `self.__attr` — double underscore, name-mangled by Python to `_ClassName__attr`. ```python class Account: def __init__(self, balance): self.__balance = balance # private def get_balance(self): return self.__balance ``` **2. Inheritance [2.5 Marks]:** Inheritance allows a new class (child/derived) to acquire attributes and methods from an existing class (parent/base), promoting code reuse. Python supports single, multiple, and multilevel inheritance. ```python class BaseModel: def __init__(self, name): self.name = name class LinearModel(BaseModel): def __init__(self, name, lr): super().__init__(name) self.lr = lr ``` **3. Polymorphism [2.5 Marks]:** Polymorphism allows the same interface (method name) to behave differently based on the object type. It is achieved via method overriding (runtime) and operator overloading (via dunder methods). ```python class SVMModel: def predict(self, X): return "SVM prediction" class TreeModel: def predict(self, X): return "Tree prediction" def evaluate(model, X): print(model.predict(X)) # Polymorphic dispatch ``` --- ##### **Model Solution for (b) — Object Lifecycle [7 Marks]** **Lifecycle Stages:** 1. **Class Definition [1 Mark]:** Python executes the class body, creates a class object, and populates its `__dict__` with attributes and methods. 2. **Instantiation Request [1 Mark]:** `obj = ClassName(args)` triggers the call. 3. **`__new__` Allocation [1.5 Marks]:** A new empty object is allocated in heap memory. By default, it inherits from `object`. 4. **`__init__` Initialization [1.5 Marks]:** The instance is populated with initial state. Instance variables are created. 5. **Method Binding & Usage [1 Mark]:** Methods are bound to the instance and executed. 6. **Garbage Collection [1 Mark]:** When the reference count reaches zero, `__del__` is invoked (if defined) and memory is reclaimed. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Loses 2–3 marks):** > Students often confuse `__new__` and `__init__`. Remember: > - `__new__` **creates** the object (rarely overridden; used in metaclasses and immutable types like `str`, `int`). > - `__init__` **initializes** the object (almost always overridden). > - `__new__` is a `classmethod` by signature; `__init__` is an `instancemethod`. --- #### **Question B.B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 1]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between class variables and instance variables in Python. Write a complete program demonstrating both, with at least two objects, and show the output. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of methods in a Python class (instance, class, and static methods) with suitable code examples. --- ##### **Model Solution for (a) — Class vs Instance Variables [7 Marks]** **Theoretical Distinction [3 Marks]:** | Aspect | Class Variable | Instance Variable | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Defined in | Class body, outside methods | Inside `__init__` or methods | | Scope | Shared by all instances | Unique per object | | Accessed via | `Class.var` or `obj.var` | `obj.var` only | | Memory | One copy | One copy per object | **Complete Program [4 Marks]:** ```python class Student: # Class variable college = "KTU Kerala" student_count = 0 def __init__(self, name, roll): # Instance variables self.name = name self.roll = roll Student.student_count += 1 def display(self): print(f"Name: {self.name}, Roll: {self.roll}, College: {Student.college}") # Creating objects s1 = Student("Arjun", 101) s2 = Student("Meera", 102) s1.display() s2.display() print("Total students:", Student.student_count) # Demonstrating instance variable shadowing s1.college = "NIT Calicut" # Creates instance attr, does NOT modify class print(s1.college) # NIT Calicut print(s2.college) # KTU Kerala print(Student.college) # KTU Kerala ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Name: Arjun, Roll: 101, College: KTU Kerala Name: Meera, Roll: 102, College: KTU Kerala Total students: 2 NIT Calicut KTU Kerala KTU Kerala ``` > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (Loses 2 marks):** Using `s1.college = "NIT"` to "modify" a class variable actually creates a **new instance variable** that shadows the class variable. To truly modify a class variable, use `Student.college = "NIT"`. --- ##### **Model Solution for (b) — Types of Methods [7 Marks]** **1. Instance Method [2.5 Marks]:** - Default type, takes `self` as the first parameter. - Can access and modify both instance and class state. - Bound to the instance. ```python class Model: def __init__(self, name): self.name = name def train(self): # Instance method print(f"Training {self.name}") ``` **2. Class Method [2.5 Marks]:** - Decorated with `@classmethod`. - Takes `cls` as the first parameter (refers to the class, not the instance). - Can access and modify class-level state. - Often used as **alternative constructors**. ```python class Model: count = 0 @classmethod def increment(cls): cls.count += 1 @classmethod def from_config(cls, config): return cls(config["name"]) ``` **3. Static Method [2 Marks]:** - Decorated with `@staticmethod`. - Takes neither `self` nor `cls`. - Acts as a logically grouped utility function. - Cannot modify class or instance state. ```python class Model: @staticmethod def sigmoid(x): return 1 / (1 + 2.71828 ** -x) ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Loses 1–2 marks):** Students often misidentify which method type can access instance variables. Only **instance methods** can access instance state. Class methods can only access class-level attributes, and static methods can access neither. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Every Exam:** - **Class** = blueprint; **Object** = instance. (Blueprint $\neq$ House) - Class definition creates a **namespace object** in memory. - `__init__` is the **initializer**, not the constructor (technically `__new__` is). - `self` is **mandatory** in instance methods; `cls` in class methods; neither in static. - Class variables are **shared**; instance variables are **per-object**. - Modifying a class variable via an instance (`obj.var = x`) creates an **instance variable shadow**, not a class variable modification. - The three pillars of OOP: **Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism**. - Encapsulation in Python uses `_` (convention) and `__` (name-mangled) prefixes. - Dunder methods (`__str__`, `__repr__`, `__len__`, `__add__`) enable **operator overloading**. - Method Resolution Order (MRO) follows **C3 linearization** for multiple inheritance. - ML Connection: Custom model classes, Dataset classes, and Pipeline classes all rely on OOP. - `super().__init__()` must be called explicitly in child class constructors to invoke parent initialization. - `type(obj)` returns the class; `isinstance(obj, Class)` checks inheritance. - `@property` decorator converts a method to a read-only attribute (encapsulation helper). - Always use `PascalCase` for class names (PEP 8). - A class without a parent inherits from `object` by default in Python 3. --- > **End of Module 1 Notes — Classes and Objects** > *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Standards | Aligned with PEPET413 Learning Outcomes*

topics.title: Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Inheritance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Inheritance** is a fundamental Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) paradigm that enables a new class (called the **derived class**, **child class**, or **subclass**) to acquire the properties (attributes) and behaviors (methods) of an existing class (called the **base class**, **parent class**, or **superclass**). It establishes a hierarchical "**is-a**" relationship between classes, promoting **code reusability**, **extensibility**, and **polymorphic behavior**. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme's Object-Oriented Programming foundations (which support Machine Learning implementation in languages like Python, Java, or C++), inheritance serves as the mechanism through which domain-specific ML classes (e.g., custom classifiers, neural network architectures) inherit from generic base classes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Inheritance is one of the four pillars of OOP (along with Encapsulation, Polymorphism, and Abstraction). It is a guaranteed high-weightage topic in university examinations. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree Imagine a **Family Genetic Tree**: - Your **grandfather** has certain traits (eye color, surname, family business logic). - Your **father** inherits those traits and may add his own (his profession, his own house). - **You**, in turn, inherit from your father — gaining grandfather's traits indirectly through your father, plus your own unique traits. In this analogy: - **Grandfather** = Base class (Superclass) - **Father** = Intermediate (Intermediate Derived class) - **You** = Derived class (Subclass) Just as you don't need to "redefine" your surname — it comes automatically — a subclass doesn't need to rewrite the code of its parent; it simply inherits it and can extend or override behaviors. ### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Synonym | Role | |---|---|---| | **Base Class** | Parent / Superclass | The class whose features are inherited | | **Derived Class** | Child / Subclass | The class that inherits features | | **Reusability** | — | Using existing code without rewriting | | **Extensibility** | — | Adding new features to inherited code | | **`is-a` Relationship** | Generalization | A Dog **is-a** Animal | | **`has-a` Relationship** | Composition (NOT inheritance) | A Car **has-a** Engine | > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Distinction:** Inheritance represents an **"is-a"** relationship (Dog is an Animal). If your relationship is **"has-a"** (Car has an Engine), you must use **Composition**, not Inheritance. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Operational Mechanism — How Inheritance Works Internally When a derived class is instantiated, the following sequence occurs in memory: 1. **Memory Allocation Phase:** The system allocates memory sufficient to hold **both** the base class members **and** the derived class members (a single contiguous object layout). 2. **Constructor Chaining Phase:** The **base class constructor is invoked first**, followed by the derived class constructor. This ensures base-class data members are properly initialized before derived-class members use them. 3. **Method Resolution Phase:** When a method is called on the derived object, the runtime checks the derived class first; if not found, it walks up the inheritance chain. 4. **Access Control Phase:** Each inherited member retains the access level dictated by the **derived class's inheritance mode** (public, protected, private) combined with the **base member's original visibility**. ### 2.2 The Five Canonical Types of Inheritance 1. **Single Inheritance:** One derived class inherits from one base class. - `A → B` (A is parent, B is child) 2. **Multilevel Inheritance:** A chain of inheritance — derived class acts as base for another class. - `A → B → C` (Grandparent → Parent → Child) 3. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** Multiple derived classes inherit from a single base class. - `A → B`, `A → C`, `A → D` (One parent, many children) 4. **Multiple Inheritance:** One derived class inherits from multiple base classes. - `A, B → C` (Two parents, one child) - **Note:** Supported in C++, **not** supported in Java (uses interfaces instead). 5. **Hybrid Inheritance:** A combination of two or more of the above types, often leading to the **Diamond Problem**. ### 2.3 The Diamond Problem (Hybrid Inheritance) When class `D` inherits from both `B` and `C`, and both `B` and `C` inherit from a common ancestor `A`, an ambiguity arises: which copy of `A`'s members does `D` possess? ``` A / \ B C \ / D ``` **Resolution strategies:** - **C++:** Uses **Virtual Inheritance** to ensure only one copy of `A` exists. - **Java:** Disallows multiple inheritance of classes entirely; uses interfaces to achieve similar functionality. - **Python:** Uses **MRO (Method Resolution Order)** via the C3 Linearization Algorithm. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Symbolic Representation | Description | |---|---|---| | Inheritance Relationship | $\text{class } B : \text{public } A$ | B inherits from A publicly | | Member Reusability | $M_{\text{derived}} = M_{\text{base}} \cup M_{\text{new}}$ | Derived members = Base members + new additions | | Object Memory Layout | $\text{sizeof}(B) = \text{sizeof}(A) + \text{sizeof}(B_{\text{new}})$ | Size = inherited size + new size | | Constructor Chain Order | $\text{Order} = [\text{Base}_{\text{ctor}}, \text{Derived}_{\text{ctor}}]$ | Base constructor executes first | | Diamond Problem Ambiguity | $\vert \text{Paths}(D \to A) \vert > 1$ | Multiple paths trigger ambiguity | | Method Override Resolution | $M_{\text{called}} = \text{Type}(M_{\text{owner}})$ | Method dispatched by owner's type | | Access Mode Reduction | $\text{public} \xrightarrow{\text{private inherit}} \text{private}$ | Public members become private in private inheritance | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **GUI Frameworks:** `JFrame` (Java) or `QMainWindow` (Qt) inherit from generic `Window` classes. - **Machine Learning Libraries:** `LogisticRegression` may inherit from a generic `Classifier` base class. - **Game Development:** `Enemy`, `Player`, `NPC` inherit from a common `Character` base. - **Exception Handling:** Custom exceptions inherit from `Exception` base class. - **Operating Systems:** Device drivers inherit from base `Driver` classes. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The Shape Hierarchy Let us derive the inheritance chain step-by-step using a classic **Shape** example (a guaranteed KTU favorite). **Step 1: Define the Base Class** Consider a generic `Shape` representing any 2D figure. It has: - A `color` attribute - A `display()` method $$ \text{Shape} = \{\text{color} : \text{String}, \text{display}() : \text{void}\} $$ **Step 2: Derive a `Circle` Class from `Shape`** A `Circle` **is-a** `Shape`, but adds: - A `radius` attribute - A `calculateArea()` method specific to circles $$ \text{Circle} = \text{Shape} \cup \{\text{radius} : \text{double}, \text{calculateArea}() : \text{double}\} $$ Expanding the union: $$ \text{Circle} = \{\text{color} : \text{String}, \text{display}() : \text{void}, \text{radius} : \text{double}, \text{calculateArea}() : \text{double}\} $$ **Step 3: Derive a `Cylinder` Class from `Circle` (Multilevel Inheritance)** A `Cylinder` **is-a** `Circle` (specifically, a circle extended into 3D), adding: - A `height` attribute - An overridden `calculateArea()` (now computes surface area of cylinder) $$ \text{Cylinder} = \text{Circle} \cup \{\text{height} : \text{double}, \text{calculateArea}()_{\text{overridden}} : \text{double}\} $$ Therefore, the full inherited set for `Cylinder` is: $$ \text{Cylinder} = \{\text{color}, \text{display}()_{\text{inherited}}, \text{radius}, \text{calculateArea}()_{\text{overridden}}, \text{height}\} $$ **Step 4: Mathematical Verification of Memory Layout** Suppose each attribute takes **8 bytes** and each method pointer takes **8 bytes**: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{sizeof(Shape)} &= 8_{\text{color}} + 8_{\text{display ptr}} = 16 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{sizeof(Circle)} &= \text{sizeof(Shape)} + 8_{\text{radius}} + 8_{\text{calcArea ptr}} = 32 \text{ bytes} \\ \text{sizeof(Cylinder)} &= \text{sizeof(Circle)} + 8_{\text{height}} + 8_{\text{overridden ptr}} = 48 \text{ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ This confirms the general formula: $$ \text{sizeof}(D) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{sizeof}(A_i) + \text{sizeof}(D_{\text{new}}) $$ where $D$ is the derived class and $A_i$ are all ancestor base classes. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Access Specifier Combinations The following matrix determines the effective visibility of a base class member when inherited in different modes. This is a **high-yield KTU exam topic**. | Base Class Member | `public` Inheritance | `protected` Inheritance | `private` Inheritance | |---|---|---|---| | **`public`** | `public` in derived | `protected` in derived | `private` in derived | | **`protected`** | `protected` in derived | `protected` in derived | `private` in derived | | **`private`** | **Not accessible** | **Not accessible** | **Not accessible** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Private members are NEVER inherited** in any mode — they remain inaccessible to derived classes. This is a guaranteed trick question in KTU exams. ### 3.3 Step-by-Step Constructor Chaining Derivation Consider multilevel inheritance: `A → B → C`. When an object of `C` is created, the execution order is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Execution Order} &: \text{A}_{\text{constructor}} \rightarrow \text{B}_{\text{constructor}} \rightarrow \text{C}_{\text{constructor}} \\ \text{Destruction Order} &: \text{C}_{\text{destructor}} \rightarrow \text{B}_{\text{destructor}} \rightarrow \text{A}_{\text{destructor}} \end{aligned} $$ **Reasoning:** - The base class must be **fully constructed** before derived classes can safely use inherited members. - Destructors execute in **reverse order** — just like stacking plates: the last plate placed (C) is the first removed. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Class Diagram — Single & Multilevel Inheritance ```mermaid graph TD classA["CLASS A
Base Superclass
-------------------
+ dataA : int
+ showA() : void"] classB["CLASS B : public A
Derived from A
-------------------
+ dataB : int
+ showB() : void"] classC["CLASS C : public B
Derived from B (Multilevel)
-------------------
+ dataC : int
+ showC() : void"] classD["CLASS D : public A
Hierarchical Sibling
-------------------
+ dataD : int
+ showD() : void"] classA --> classB classB --> classC classA --> classD ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Diamond Problem Architecture ```mermaid graph TD topA["CLASS A
Top-level Ancestor
------------
+ show() : void"] leftB["CLASS B : public A
Left Branch
------------
+ showB() : void"] rightC["CLASS C : public A
Right Branch
------------
+ showC() : void"] bottomD["CLASS D : public B, public C
Diamond Tip
------------
AMBIGUITY: show() from A"] topA --> leftB topA --> rightC leftB --> bottomD rightC --> bottomD ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Constructor Chaining Execution Flow ```mermaid graph TD step1["STEP 1: Object C is instantiated
Memory allocated for A+B+C members"] step2["STEP 2: A constructor invoked
Base data members initialized first"] step3["STEP 3: B constructor invoked
Intermediate members initialized"] step4["STEP 4: C constructor invoked
Derived members initialized last"] step5["STEP 5: Object is fully ready
All inherited + new members accessible"] step1 --> step2 step2 --> step3 step3 --> step4 step4 --> step5 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define inheritance in OOP. List any two advantages of inheritance.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (2 marks):** Inheritance is an OOP mechanism by which a derived class acquires the properties and behaviors of a base class, establishing an "is-a" relationship. It enables code reuse without rewriting existing functionality. - **Advantages (1 mark):** 1. **Code Reusability** — Eliminates redundancy by inheriting existing code. 2. **Extensibility** — New features can be added to derived classes without modifying base classes. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between `public`, `protected`, and `private` inheritance modes with one example each.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Mode | Behavior | |---|---| | `public` | Public and protected base members retain their original access levels in derived class. | | `protected` | Public and protected base members become `protected` in the derived class. | | `private` | Public and protected base members become `private` in the derived class. | Example: `class Derived : public Base` (public inheritance) — used when derived **is-a** base. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE) #### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]` **(a)** Explain the different types of inheritance with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance? How is it resolved in C++ and Java? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Types of Inheritance (7 Marks):** 1. **Single Inheritance:** One derived class inherits one base class. - Example: `class Car : public Vehicle` 2. **Multilevel Inheritance:** Derived class becomes base for another class. - Example: `Student → GraduateStudent → PhDStudent` 3. **Hierarchical Inheritance:** Multiple derived classes share one base. - Example: `Shape → Circle, Rectangle, Triangle` 4. **Multiple Inheritance:** One class inherits from multiple bases. - Example: `class Amphibian : public Terrestrial, public Aquatic` 5. **Hybrid Inheritance:** Combination of multiple and hierarchical types; may produce the diamond problem. (Diagrams to be drawn — refer to SECTION 4.1 above for reference.) **(b) Diamond Problem (7 Marks):** The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two classes that share a common ancestor, creating ambiguity about which copy of the ancestor's members to use. **Resolution in C++ (3 marks):** Uses **virtual inheritance**: ```cpp class B : virtual public A { }; class C : virtual public A { }; class D : public B, public C { }; // Only one copy of A ``` **Resolution in Java (3 marks):** Java **disallows multiple inheritance of classes**. Instead, it uses **interfaces** to achieve multiple inheritance of type, eliminating the diamond problem by design. **Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 3 marks for C++ + 3 marks for Java.** --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain multilevel inheritance with an example. Write the order of constructor and destructor execution. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain method overriding with a C++/Java code segment. Why is it necessary for polymorphism? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Multilevel Inheritance (7 Marks):** In multilevel inheritance, a class is derived from another derived class, forming a chain. Example: `Animal → Mammal → Dog` ```cpp class Animal { public: Animal() { cout << "Animal ctor"; } }; class Mammal : public Animal { public: Mammal() { cout << "Mammal ctor"; } }; class Dog : public Mammal { public: Dog() { cout << "Dog ctor"; } }; ``` **Constructor Order (2 marks):** `Animal → Mammal → Dog` (base-first) **Destructor Order (2 marks):** `Dog → Mammal → Animal` (derived-first, reverse order) **(b) Method Overriding (7 Marks):** Method overriding occurs when a derived class provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its base class. ```cpp class Base { public: void show() { cout << "Base show"; } }; class Derived : public Base { public: void show() { cout << "Derived show"; } // Override }; ``` When `Derived` object calls `show()`, the derived version executes (resolved via **dynamic dispatch**). This is essential for **runtime polymorphism**, where the method invoked depends on the actual object type, not the reference type, enabling flexible and extensible code design. **(2 marks for polymorphism link)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting "private members are NOT inherited"** — Examiners specifically test this. Always clarify in answers. > 2. **Confusing constructor vs destructor order** — Many students write them in the same order. Destructors execute in **reverse**. > 3. **Using `has-a` instead of `is-a`** — If you write "Car inherits Engine," you lose 2 marks instantly. Use **Composition** for `has-a`. > 4. **Skipping diagrams in inheritance questions** — KTU examiners expect hierarchy diagrams; absence costs 2–3 marks. > 5. **Not specifying inheritance mode** — Always mention `public`, `protected`, or `private` explicitly when defining inheritance. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Inheritance** = OOP mechanism for acquiring base class properties into a derived class. - Establishes an **"is-a"** relationship (use **Composition** for "has-a"). - **Five Types:** Single, Multilevel, Hierarchical, Multiple, Hybrid. - **Diamond Problem** arises in Hybrid/Multiple inheritance when two paths lead to a common ancestor. - **C++** resolves the diamond problem via **virtual inheritance**; **Java** disallows multiple class inheritance entirely (uses interfaces). - **Constructor Order:** Base-first. **Destructor Order:** Derived-first (reverse). - **Private members are NEVER inherited** — they remain inaccessible regardless of inheritance mode. - **Access specifier reduction rule:** `public → protected → private` (never escalates upward). - **Method Overriding** requires the same method signature (name + parameter list) in both base and derived. - **Memory of derived object** = Sum of all inherited members + new members. - Inheritance promotes **code reusability**, **extensibility**, and enables **runtime polymorphism** — the three most-tested benefits in KTU exams. ---

topics.title: Polymorphism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polymorphism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymorphism** (from Greek: *poly* = many, *morph* = forms) is a foundational pillar of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) defined as the ability of a single interface, function, or operator to operate on different data types or for objects of different classes to respond to the same message (method call) in different, class-specific ways.

In the context of the **PEPET413 – Machine Learning** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), polymorphism is treated as a **prerequisite computational concept** that underpins the design of extensible Machine Learning frameworks such as `scikit-learn`, `TensorFlow`, and `PyTorch`. Every estimator, layer, loss function, and optimizer in these libraries relies on polymorphism to present a **uniform interface** while behaving differently depending on the underlying model.

The strict academic definition (Christopher Strachey, 1967 – foundational paper *"Fundamental Concepts in Programming Languages"*):

> *"Polymorphism is the provision of a single interface to entities of different types, or the use of a single symbol to represent multiple different types."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy – The Universal Power Button:**
> Consider a single "Power" symbol on a TV remote. The **same button** (one interface, one symbol) is used to switch ON/OFF your TV, your Set-Top Box, your Soundbar, and your AC. Each device **interprets the same command** in its own way – that is polymorphism. The user does not need to learn a different button for every device.

**Engineering Analogy for ML:** When you call `model.fit(X, y)` in scikit-learn, the **exact same line of code** trains a Linear Regression, a Decision Tree, an SVM, or a Neural Network. Each model class **overrides** the `fit()` method internally and behaves according to its own mathematical formulation. One interface, many implementations.

### 1.3 Classification of Polymorphism (KTU Board Taxonomy)

| # | Type | Other Names | Binding Time |
|---|------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | Ad-hoc Polymorphism | Function/Operator Overloading | Compile-time |
| 2 | Parametric Polymorphism | Generics / Templates | Compile-time |
| 3 | Subtype Polymorphism | Inclusion / Method Overriding | Run-time |
| 4 | Coercion Polymorphism | Type Conversion | Compile / Run-time |

### 1.4 Core Vocabulary (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

- **Signature:** The name + parameter list of a function (used to resolve overloading).
- **Binding:** The process of associating a function call with the actual code to be executed.
- **Static Binding (Early Binding):** Decision made at compile time.
- **Dynamic Binding (Late Binding):** Decision deferred until run time via the actual object type.
- **Virtual Function:** A function declared in a base class intended to be overridden in derived classes (C++ / Java terminology).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars – Operational Mechanics

### Pillar 1: Ad-hoc Polymorphism (Function/Operator Overloading)
- **What it is:** Multiple functions share the **same name** but differ in the **number, type, or order of parameters**.
- **Binding:** **Static (Compile-time)**. The compiler resolves the call before execution.
- **Why it works:** The compiler uses the function's **signature** (name + parameter list) to build a *mangled name* internally (e.g., C++ Name Mangling: `_Z3addii` for `add(int,int)`).
- **Why it matters in ML:** You can define `add(scalar, scalar)`, `add(vector, vector)`, and `add(tensor, tensor)` in your custom loss module using the same intuitive name.

### Pillar 2: Parametric Polymorphism (Generics)
- **What it is:** A function or class is written to operate on **any data type** without knowing the type in advance. A **type parameter** (e.g., `T`) is supplied at instantiation.
- **Binding:** **Static**, but the body is **type-agnostic**.
- **Why it matters in ML:** Generic containers like `List<Float>` in Java/Kotlin or `Dict[str, Tensor]` in Python typing allow ML pipelines to remain **type-safe** while handling heterogeneous data.

### Pillar 3: Subtype Polymorphism (Method Overriding) — *The Most Important for ML*
- **What it is:** A derived class provides a **re-implementation** of a method already declared in its base class, using the **exact same signature**.
- **Binding:** **Dynamic (Run-time)**. The JVM/Python interpreter looks up the actual object's class (via the **v-table** in C++ or the **MRO – Method Resolution Order** in Python) at the moment of the call.
- **Why it matters in ML:** Every `BaseEstimator` in scikit-learn inherits from the same parent and **overrides** `fit()`, `predict()`, `transform()`. This is why one `Pipeline` object can chain models from entirely different mathematical families.
