A Type II error means failing to diagnose an actually sick person. **(b)** [7 Marks] The average waiting time at a bank was reported to be 12 minutes. To test this, a sample of 36 customers was taken and the mean waiting time was found to be 11 minutes with a standard deviation of 3 minutes. At a 5% significance level, test whether the average waiting time has decreased. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: State Hypotheses** $$H_0: \mu = 12 \text{ minutes (no change)}$$ $$H_1: \mu < 12 \text{ minutes (decreased)}$$ This is a **left-tailed test**. **Step 2: Significance Level** $$\alpha = 0.05$$ **Step 3: Test Selection** - n = 36 (large), so we can use the **Z-test**. - Sample standard deviation s = 3 is used as an estimate. **Step 4: Compute Test Statistic** $$\begin{aligned} Z_{calc} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{s / \sqrt{n}} \\ Z_{calc} &= \dfrac{11 - 12}{3 / \sqrt{36}} \\ Z_{calc} &= \dfrac{-1}{3 / 6} \\ Z_{calc} &= \dfrac{-1}{0.5} \\ Z_{calc} &= -2.0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Critical Value** For left-tailed test at α = 0.05: $$Z_{crit} = -1.645$$ **Step 6: Decision** Since $Z_{calc} = -2.0 < Z_{crit} = -1.645$, the test statistic falls in the rejection region. **Therefore, we reject $H_0$.** **Step 7: Conclusion** At the 5% significance level, there is sufficient evidence to conclude that the average waiting time at the bank has **decreased** significantly from 12 minutes. **Verification using p-value:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{p-value} &= P(Z \leq -2.0) \\ &= 0.0228 \end{aligned}$$ Since p-value (0.0228) < α (0.05), this confirms our rejection of $H_0$. --- **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the concept of p-value in hypothesis testing. How is it used to make decisions? Discuss its advantages over the critical value approach. **Model Solution:** **Definition of p-value:** The **p-value** (probability value) is the probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as, or more extreme than, the one observed in the sample, **assuming the null hypothesis $H_0$ is true**. **Mathematical Representation (Two-tailed):** $$\text{p-value} = 2 \times P(Z \geq \vert Z_{calc} \vert)$$ **Mathematical Representation (One-tailed right):** $$\text{p-value} = P(Z \geq Z_{calc})$$ **Decision Rule:** - If **p-value ≤ α** → Reject $H_0$ - If **p-value > α** → Fail to reject $H_0$ **Example**: If p-value = 0.03 and α = 0.05, since 0.03 < 0.05, we reject $H_0$. **Advantages of p-value Approach over Critical Value Approach:** | Aspect | p-value Approach | Critical Value Approach | |---|---|---| | **Information Content** | Provides exact probability of observed data under $H_0$ | Only tells if statistic is in rejection region | | **Flexibility** | Same p-value works for any α without recomputation | Need new critical value for each α | | **Interpretation** | Quantifies strength of evidence (smaller p = stronger evidence) | Binary yes/no decision only | | **Reporting in Research** | Universally accepted in scientific publications | Less commonly reported alone | **Limitations of p-value:** - Does NOT measure the size or importance of an effect. - Does NOT give the probability that $H_0$ is true. - Highly sensitive to sample size (large samples can yield tiny p-values for trivial effects). **(b)** [7 Marks] A random sample of 16 observations from a normal population gave a sample mean of 78 and a sample standard deviation of 10. Test at a 5% significance level whether the population mean is different from 75. **Model Solution:** **Step 1: State Hypotheses** $$H_0: \mu = 75$$ $$H_1: \mu \neq 75$$ This is a **two-tailed test**. **Step 2: Significance Level** $$\alpha = 0.05$$ **Step 3: Test Selection** - n = 16 (< 30) - σ unknown (only sample s = 10 is given) - Therefore, use the **t-test** with df = n − 1 = 15. **Step 4: Compute Test Statistic** $$\begin{aligned} t_{calc} &= \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{s / \sqrt{n}} \\ t_{calc} &= \dfrac{78 - 75}{10 / \sqrt{16}} \\ t_{calc} &= \dfrac{3}{10 / 4} \\ t_{calc} &= \dfrac{3}{2.5} \\ t_{calc} &= 1.2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5: Critical Value** For two-tailed test at α = 0.05 with df = 15: $$t_{crit} = \pm 2.131 \text{ (from t-table)}$$ **Step 6: Decision** Since $\vert t_{calc} \vert = 1.2 < t_{crit} = 2.131$, the test statistic does **NOT** fall in the rejection region. **Therefore, we fail to reject $H_0$.** **Step 7: Conclusion** At the 5% significance level, there is **insufficient evidence** to conclude that the population mean is different from 75. The observed difference (sample mean 78 vs hypothesized 75) could reasonably be attributed to random sampling variation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**: > 1. **Wrong Test Selection**: Students frequently use the Z-test when σ is unknown and n < 30. Always check the conditions first — use t-test when σ is unknown. > 2. **Direction Confusion**: For a left-tailed test, students often compare |Z| with Z_crit instead of comparing Z_calc with -Z_crit. Always respect the sign. > 3. **Degrees of Freedom Error**: Forgetting that t-test uses df = n − 1, not n. > 4. **"Accept H₀" vs "Fail to Reject H₀"**: Examiners **strictly deduct marks** if you write "we accept H₀." The correct phrase is "we fail to reject H₀." > 5. **p-value Misinterpretation**: Never write "p-value is the probability that H₀ is true." This is a conceptual error worth 2 marks. > 6. **Two-tailed Split**: For two-tailed tests, forgetting to divide α by 2 when finding critical values. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Hypothesis**: A testable statement about a population parameter. - **Null Hypothesis (H₀)**: The default/assumption statement — always contains equality. - **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)**: The contradicting statement — what we want evidence for. - **Significance Level (α)**: Maximum tolerable Type I error rate; typically **0.05**. - **Type I Error (α)**: Rejecting a true $H_0$ — False Positive. - **Type II Error (β)**: Failing to reject a false $H_0$ — False Negative. - **Power = 1 − β**: Probability of correctly detecting a real effect. - **Z-test formula**: $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ — used when σ is known or n ≥ 30. - **t-test formula**: $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ — used when σ is unknown and n < 30. - **Degrees of Freedom for t-test**: df = n − 1. - **One-tailed test**: Rejection region on ONE side only — use when direction is pre-specified. - **Two-tailed test**: Rejection region on BOTH sides — use when no directional hypothesis. - **Critical values for α = 0.05**: Right Z = 1.645, Two-tailed Z = ±1.96, Right t depends on df. - **Decision rule (Critical)**: Reject $H_0$ if test statistic falls in rejection region. - **Decision rule (p-value)**: Reject $H_0$ if p-value ≤ α. - **"Fail to reject" ≠ "Accept"**: We never *prove* $H_0$; we only fail to reject it. - **Smaller p-value = stronger evidence against $H_0$**. - **Increasing sample size reduces β** (Type II error), thereby increasing power. - **Lowering α reduces Type I error but increases Type II error** (trade-off). - **ML Connection**: Hypothesis testing is used in A/B testing, model comparison, feature significance testing, and cross-validation performance comparison. - **Real-world applications**: Quality control, clinical trials, manufacturing tolerances, software benchmarking. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra**: State hypotheses clearly → Choose correct test → Compute statistic accurately → Apply correct critical value (respecting tails) → Conclude using "reject" or "fail to reject" — never "accept." This six-step pattern guarantees full marks on any KTU hypothesis testing question.

topics.title: Confidence Intervals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Confidence Intervals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Confidence Interval (CI):** A *confidence interval* is a range of values, derived from a sample statistic, that is likely to contain the value of an unknown population parameter with a specified level of confidence. It is formally expressed as a point estimate ± a margin of error, governed by a confidence coefficient $(1 - \alpha)$.

Mathematically, for a population parameter $\theta$ and a sample-based estimator $\hat{\theta}$:

$$CI_{(1-\alpha)} \;=\; \left[\, \hat{\theta} \;-\; E,\;\; \hat{\theta} \;+\; E \,\right]$$

where:
- $\hat{\theta}$ = **point estimate** of the true parameter
- $E$ = **margin of error** (half-width of the interval)
- $(1 - \alpha)$ = **confidence level** (e.g., 0.95 for 95% CI)
- $\alpha$ = **significance level** (probability that the interval fails to contain $\theta$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the Machine Learning context, Confidence Intervals are foundational to *statistical inference for model evaluation* — they quantify uncertainty in estimates of accuracy, error rates, regression coefficients, and generalization bounds.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Spearfishing" Interpretation

Imagine you are standing on the shore of a lake and want to estimate where the fish (true population mean $\mu$) are swimming. You cannot see them, so you cast a net (collect a sample) and compute the average weight (sample mean $\bar{x}$).

- A **point estimate** is a single guess: *"The fish weigh about 3.2 kg on average."*
- A **confidence interval** is like saying: *"Based on my net, I am 95% confident the true average weight is between 2.9 kg and 3.5 kg."*

The confidence level is **NOT** the probability that $\mu$ lies in any *single* interval you compute (that is either 0% or 100%). Instead, it is the long-run proportion of such intervals that would contain $\mu$ if you repeated the sampling process infinitely often.

> [!TIP]
> **Frequentist Intuition:** If you repeated your experiment 100 times and built 100 different 95% CIs, approximately 95 of those intervals would bracket the true parameter $\theta$.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Glossary Standard)

| Term | Symbol | Plain-English Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Point Estimate | $\hat{\theta}$ | Best single guess for $\theta$ from sample data |
| Margin of Error | $E$ | Maximum likely distance between $\hat{\theta}$ and $\theta$ |
| Confidence Level | $1 - \alpha$ | Probability the procedure captures $\theta$ (e.g., 0.95) |
| Significance Level | $\alpha$ | Probability of *missing* $\theta$ (e.g., 0.05) |
| Critical Value | $z_{\alpha/2}$, $t_{\alpha/2}$ | Quantile from standard/t-distribution |
| Standard Error | $SE(\hat{\theta})$ | Variability of $\hat{\theta}$ across samples |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Confidence Intervals Matter in Machine Learning

In ML, we never have access to the *true* data distribution. We rely on finite samples to:
1. **Quantify uncertainty** in model accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score.
2. **Compare classifiers** — decide whether Algorithm A is *significantly* better than Algorithm B.
3. **Set generalization bounds** — Hoeffding's inequality and VC-theory rely on confidence-based reasoning.
4. **Conduct A/B testing** for deployed ML systems.
5. **Estimate regression coefficients** with associated uncertainty (linear regression inference).

---

## 2.2 The Four Canonical CI Scenarios

### 🔹 Case 1: CI for Population Mean ($\mu$) — Variance $\sigma^2$ KNOWN

$$CI \;=\; \bar{x} \; \pm \; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

- **Use:** Large samples ($n \geq 30$) **OR** when $\sigma$ is given.
- **Distribution:** Standard Normal ($Z$).

### 🔹 Case 2: CI for Population Mean ($\mu$) — Variance $\sigma^2$ UNKNOWN

$$CI \;=\; \bar{x} \; \pm \; t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

- **Use:** Small samples ($n < 30$), $\sigma$ estimated by sample std. dev. $s$.
- **Distribution:** Student's $t$ with $n - 1$ degrees of freedom.

### 🔹 Case 3: CI for Population Proportion ($p$)

$$CI \;=\; \hat{p} \; \pm \; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$$

- **Use:** Binary classification, success/failure experiments.
- **Validity condition:** $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$.

### 🔹 Case 4: CI for Population Variance ($\sigma^2$)

$$\left[\, \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\, n-1}},\;\; \frac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\, n-1}} \,\right]$$

- **Distribution:** Chi-square ($\chi^2$) with $n - 1$ d.f.

---

## 2.3 Margin of Error & Sample Size Determination

The margin of error:

$$E \;=\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

Solving for required sample size given a desired margin $E$:

$$n \;=\; \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$$

For proportions, replace $\sigma$ with $\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}$:

$$n \;=\; \left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2}}{E}\right)^{2} \cdot \hat{p}(1-\hat{p})$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Distribution | Required Conditions |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Mean ($\mu$), $\sigma$ known | $\bar{x} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $Z$ (Normal) | $n \geq 30$ or $\sigma$ given |
| 2 | Mean ($\mu$), $\sigma$ unknown | $\bar{x} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ | $t$ (Student) | $n < 30$, $s$ from sample |
| 3 | Proportion ($p$) | $\hat{p} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$ | $Z$ (Normal) | $n\hat{p} \geq 5$, $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ |
| 4 | Variance ($\sigma^2$) | $\left[\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{\alpha/2,\,n-1}},\;\dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2,\,n-1}}\right]$ | $\chi^2$ | Normal population |
| 5 | Difference of Means | $(\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2) \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,\nu} \cdot SE_{pooled}$ | $t$ | Independent samples |
| 6 | Sample Size | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma}{E}\right)^{2}$ | — | Specified $E$ and confidence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse the **two tails** of the $\chi^2$ distribution. The *left* tail uses $\chi^2_{1-\alpha/2}$ (lower) in the *denominator*, and the *right* tail uses $\chi^2_{\alpha/2}$ (upper) in the *denominator*. Memorize the inversion pattern!

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of CIs |
| :--- | :--- |
| **ML Model Evaluation** | Bound true accuracy of a classifier on a test set |
| **Medical Diagnosis AI** | Estimate sensitivity/specificity with uncertainty |
| **A/B Testing** | Compare two recommendation systems' click-through rates |
| **Manufacturing QC** | Tolerances on production parameters |
| **Financial Forecasting** | Uncertainty bands on predicted returns |
| **NLP/Speech Systems** | Confidence in word-error-rate (WER) estimates |
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CI for Mean (σ Known) — From First Principles

### Step 1 — Standardize the Sample Mean

By the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**, for a sample of size $n$ drawn from a population with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, the standardized variable:

$$Z \;=\; \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

follows the **Standard Normal Distribution** $N(0, 1)$ as $n \to \infty$.

> **Conversion Logic:** Subtracting the population mean $\mu$ centers the distribution at 0. Dividing by the standard deviation of $\bar{X}$, namely $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, scales it to unit variance.

### Step 2 — Define the Probability Statement

We seek an interval $[a, b]$ such that $P(a \leq Z \leq b) = 1 - \alpha$. By symmetry of the normal distribution around 0, the optimal choice is:

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \;\leq\; Z \;\leq\; z_{\alpha/2}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

where $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the critical value leaving probability $\alpha/2$ in each tail.

### Step 3 — Substitute the Expression for $Z$

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \;\leq\; \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; z_{\alpha/2}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

### Step 4 — Algebraically Isolate $\mu$

Multiply all parts by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$:

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; \bar{X} - \mu \;\leq\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Subtract $\bar{X}$ from all parts:

$$P\!\left(-\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; -\mu \;\leq\; -\bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Multiply by $-1$ (which *flips* the inequality signs):

$$P\!\left(\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; \mu \;\leq\; \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

### Step 5 — Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\;CI_{(1-\alpha)} \;=\; \bar{X} \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\;}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The width of the CI is $2 z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / \sqrt{n}$. To *halve* the width, you must *quadruple* the sample size — a critical trade-off in experiment design.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A random sample of $n = 36$ observations from a population yields $\bar{x} = 75$, with known $\sigma = 12$. Construct a **95% confidence interval** for the population mean $\mu$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**
- $\bar{x} = 75$, $\sigma = 12$, $n = 36$, confidence level $= 0.95 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0.05$.

**Step 2 — Find the critical value.**
For a 95% CI, $\alpha/2 = 0.025$. From the standard normal table:
$$z_{0.025} = 1.96$$

**Step 3 — Compute the standard error.**

$$SE \;=\; \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;=\; \frac{12}{\sqrt{36}} \;=\; \frac{12}{6} \;=\; 2$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The standard error measures how much $\bar{X}$ varies across hypothetical repeated samples.

**Step 4 — Compute the margin of error.**

$$E \;=\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot SE \;=\; 1.96 \times 2 \;=\; 3.92$$

**Step 5 — Form the interval.**

$$CI \;=\; [75 - 3.92,\;\; 75 + 3.92] \;=\; [71.08,\;\; 78.92]$$

**Interpretation:** We are 95% confident that the true population mean $\mu$ lies between **71.08** and **78.92**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on CI Width

| Parameter | Increase Effect on Width | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $\uparrow$ Confidence level ($1-\alpha$) | Wider | More certainty → larger interval |
| $\uparrow$ Sample size ($n$) | Narrower (by factor $\sqrt{n}$) | More data → sharper estimate |
| $\uparrow$ Population std. dev. ($\sigma$) | Wider | Noisier data → more uncertainty |
| $\uparrow$ Significance level ($\alpha$) | Narrower | Accept more risk for sharper CI |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — CI for a Proportion

**Problem:** A classifier correctly classifies 87 out of 100 test images. Construct a 99% CI for the true classification accuracy $p$.

**Step 1 — Compute the point estimate.**

$$\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{87}{100} \;=\; 0.87$$

**Step 2 — Verify conditions.**

$$n\hat{p} = 100(0.87) = 87 \geq 5 \quad \checkmark$$
$$n(1-\hat{p}) = 100(0.13) = 13 \geq 5 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Find the critical value.**

For 99% confidence, $\alpha/2 = 0.005$. From the $Z$-table:
$$z_{0.005} = 2.576$$

**Step 4 — Compute the standard error.**

$$SE \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.87 \times 0.13}{100}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.001131} \;\approx\; 0.0336$$

**Step 5 — Compute the margin of error.**

$$E \;=\; 2.576 \times 0.0336 \;\approx\; 0.0866$$

**Step 6 — Form the interval.**

$$CI \;=\; [0.87 - 0.0866,\;\; 0.87 + 0.0866] \;=\; [0.7834,\;\; 0.9566]$$

> **ML Interpretation:** The classifier's true accuracy is 99% likely to lie between **78.34%** and **95.66%**. Notice that with only 100 test samples, even a 99% CI has substantial width — a critical concern when reporting ML model performance!
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Confidence Interval Construction

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the decision logic for selecting and computing the correct CI in any problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Identify Target Parameter"] --> B{"What are we estimating?"}
    B -- Mean mu --> C{"Is sigma known?"}
    B -- Proportion p --> D["Use Z formula with phat"]
    B -- Variance sigma2 --> E["Use Chi-square formula"]
    C -- Yes --> F["Use Z CI: xbar +- z * sigma / sqrt n"]
    C -- No --> G["Use t CI: xbar +- t n-1 * s / sqrt n"]
    D --> H["Verify n*phat >= 5 and n*1-phat >= 5"]
    H --> I["Compute SE and margin of error"]
    E --> J["Compute chi-square bounds and invert"]
    F --> K["Report CI: lower and upper bound"]
    G --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["Interpret: 1-alpha confidence in theta"]
```

## 4.2 Probability Coverage Visualization (Z-Distribution)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ZDistribution["Standard Normal Z Distribution"]
        L["Left Tail: alpha/2"] --- M["Central Region: 1 - alpha"] --- R["Right Tail: alpha/2"]
    end
    M --- CV1["Critical Value: -z alpha/2"]
    M --- CV2["Critical Value: +z alpha/2"]
    CV1 --- EQ["Captures mu with probability 1 - alpha"]
    CV2 --- EQ
```

## 4.3 CI Decomposition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Confidence Interval Output"] --> B["Point Estimate Center"]
    A --> C["Margin of Error Wing"]
    B --> D["xbar or phat"]
    C --> E["Critical Value z or t"]
    C --> F["Standard Error Sigma over sqrt n"]
    E --> G["Quantile Function Lookup"]
    F --> H["Sample Standard Deviation s"]
    F --> I["Sample Size n"]
```

## 4.4 Sample Size Trade-off Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Desired Margin E"] --> B["Solve n = z^2 sigma^2 / E^2"]
    C["Desired Confidence 1-alpha"] --> B
    D["Population Variability sigma"] --> B
    B --> E["Required Sample Size n"]
    E --> F{"Resources Available?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Proceed with Sampling"]
    F -- No --> H["Relax Margin or Confidence"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Drawing such flowcharts in the exam paper earns partial credit even if the final numerical answer is wrong — it demonstrates *methodological understanding* of CI selection.
---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a 95% confidence interval for a population mean. State the assumptions required for its validity.**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> A 95% confidence interval for the population mean $\mu$ is an interval constructed from sample data such that, in the long run, 95% of such intervals will contain the true value of $\mu$.
>
> **(1 mark)** — Mathematical form: $\bar{x} \pm z_{0.025} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ (when $\sigma$ is known) or $\bar{x} \pm t_{0.025,\,n-1} \cdot \dfrac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$ (when $\sigma$ is unknown).
>
> **(1 mark)** — Assumptions:
> - Sample is *random* and *independent*.
> - Population is *normally distributed* OR $n \geq 30$ (CLT applies).
> - $\sigma$ is known (Z case) OR $s$ is used as an unbiased estimator of $\sigma$ (t case).
>
> **(1 mark)** — Correct interpretation: The confidence is on the *procedure*, not on a single computed interval.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between a point estimate and an interval estimate. Why is an interval estimate preferred in machine learning model evaluation?**

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **(1 mark)** — *Point estimate*: a single numerical value computed from the sample that serves as the best guess of the population parameter (e.g., $\bar{x}$ for $\mu$, $\hat{p}$ for $p$).
>
> **(1 mark)** — *Interval estimate*: a range of values $[\hat{\theta} - E,\; \hat{\theta} + E]$ that, with a stated confidence level, is expected to contain the true parameter.
>
> **(1 mark)** — *Why preferred in ML:* A point estimate of accuracy (say, 0.91) gives no information about *reliability*. A 95% CI like $[0.88, 0.94]$ conveys the **uncertainty** due to finite test-set size, allowing meaningful comparison between models and sound decision-making.

---

## 📝 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for the population mean $\mu$ when the population standard deviation $\sigma$ is *known*. State clearly the assumptions and the role of the Central Limit Theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine learning regression model is evaluated on 25 independent test cases. The sample mean of absolute prediction errors is $\bar{x} = 4.2$ with sample standard deviation $s = 1.1$. Construct a **98% confidence interval** for the true mean absolute error $\mu$. Given $t_{0.01,\, 24} = 2.492$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Setup and CLT Invocation (2 marks):**
Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be a random sample of size $n$ from a population with mean $\mu$ and known standard deviation $\sigma$. By the **Central Limit Theorem**, for sufficiently large $n$ (typically $n \geq 30$), the sample mean $\bar{X}$ is approximately normally distributed:

$$\bar{X} \;\sim\; N\!\left(\mu,\; \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Standardization (1 mark):**
Standardize $\bar{X}$ to obtain the standard normal variable:

$$Z \;=\; \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \;\sim\; N(0, 1)$$

**Step 3 — Probability Statement (2 marks):**
By definition of the critical value $z_{\alpha/2}$:

$$P(-z_{\alpha/2} \;\leq\; Z \;\leq\; z_{\alpha/2}) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

Substituting:

$$P\!\left(-z_{\alpha/2} \;\leq\; \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; z_{\alpha/2}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Isolation of $\mu$ (1 mark):**
Multiply by $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, subtract $\bar{X}$, multiply by $-1$ (flipping inequalities):

$$P\!\left(\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \;\leq\; \mu \;\leq\; \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) \;=\; 1 - \alpha$$

**Step 5 — Final CI Form & Assumptions (1 mark):**

$$CI_{(1-\alpha)} \;=\; \bar{X} \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Random, independent sample; (ii) $\sigma$ is *known*; (iii) $n \geq 30$ OR population is normal.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Parameter Identification (1 mark):**
- $n = 25$, $\bar{x} = 4.2$, $s = 1.1$, confidence level $= 0.98 \Rightarrow \alpha = 0.02$, $df = n - 1 = 24$.

**Step 2 — Choose Distribution (1 mark):**
Since $\sigma$ is **unknown** and $n < 30$, we use the **Student's $t$-distribution** with $24$ degrees of freedom.

**Step 3 — Critical Value (1 mark):**

$$t_{\alpha/2,\, n-1} \;=\; t_{0.01,\, 24} \;=\; 2.492 \quad \text{(given)}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Error (1 mark):**

$$SE \;=\; \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \;=\; \frac{1.1}{\sqrt{25}} \;=\; \frac{1.1}{5} \;=\; 0.22$$

**Step 5 — Margin of Error (1 mark):**

$$E \;=\; t_{0.01,\, 24} \cdot SE \;=\; 2.492 \times 0.22 \;=\; 0.54824$$

**Step 6 — Confidence Interval (1 mark):**

$$CI_{98\%} \;=\; [4.2 - 0.548,\;\; 4.2 + 0.548] \;=\; [3.652,\;\; 4.748]$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
We are 98% confident that the *true* mean absolute prediction error $\mu$ of the regression model lies between **3.652** and **4.748** units.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the construction of a confidence interval for a population *proportion* $p$. What are the validity conditions? Illustrate with a classifier evaluation example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spam-detection model correctly classifies 78 out of 90 emails. Compute a **95% confidence interval** for the true precision of this model. Use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$. How would the interval change if the test set increased to 900 emails with 780 correct classifications? **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
The CI for a population proportion $p$ estimates the true fraction of successes in the population, based on observed sample fraction $\hat{p}$.

**Step 2 — Formula (2 marks):**

$$CI_{(1-\alpha)} \;=\; \hat{p} \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Validity Conditions (2 marks):**
1. $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ (expected successes)
2. $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ (expected failures)
3. Random and independent observations.
4. Sample size sufficiently large for normal approximation (CLT).

**Step 4 — ML Example (2 marks):**
A face-recognition model is tested on 200 images, correctly recognizing 174. Then $\hat{p} = 174/200 = 0.87$. Both $n\hat{p} = 174$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) = 26$ exceed 5, so the CI is valid. A 95% CI:

$$SE = \sqrt{0.87 \times 0.13 / 200} = 0.0238, \quad E = 1.96 \times 0.0238 = 0.0466$$

$$CI = [0.823, \; 0.917]$$

We are 95% confident the model's true recognition accuracy lies between 82.3% and 91.7%.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Part (i) — $n = 90$, correct $= 78$ (3 marks):**

$$\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{78}{90} \;=\; 0.8667$$

Check: $n\hat{p} = 78 \geq 5$ ✓, $n(1-\hat{p}) = 12 \geq 5$ ✓.

$$SE_1 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.8667 \times 0.1333}{90}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.001284} \;\approx\; 0.0358$$

$$E_1 \;=\; 1.96 \times 0.0358 \;\approx\; 0.0702$$

$$CI_1 \;=\; [0.8667 - 0.0702,\; 0.8667 + 0.0702] \;=\; [0.7965,\; 0.9369]$$

**Part (ii) — $n = 900$, correct $= 780$ (3 marks):**

$$\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{780}{900} \;=\; 0.8667 \;(\text{same estimate})$$

$$SE_2 \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.8667 \times 0.1333}{900}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.0001284} \;\approx\; 0.01133$$

$$E_2 \;=\; 1.96 \times 0.01133 \;\approx\; 0.0222$$

$$CI_2 \;=\; [0.8667 - 0.0222,\; 0.8667 + 0.0222] \;=\; [0.8445,\; 0.8889]$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Increasing $n$ tenfold (from 90 to 900) **shrinks the CI width by a factor of $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$** — from width 0.140 to width 0.044. This demonstrates the $\sqrt{n}$-law of statistical precision and underscores why **larger test sets yield more reliable ML evaluation**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Misinterpreting confidence level:** Writing *"There is a 95% probability that $\mu$ is in this interval"* is **WRONG** for any single computed interval. Correct: *"We are 95% confident..."* (Marks lost: 1 per occurrence.)
> 2. **Confusing $z$ and $t$:** When $\sigma$ is unknown and $n$ is small, students wrongly use $z = 1.96$. Always verify whether $s$ or $\sigma$ is given.
> 3. **Wrong tail in $\chi^2$ CI:** Inverting the numerator-denominator placement for $\chi^2$ bounds swaps upper/lower limits.
> 4. **Skipping the validity check for $p$:** Forgetting to verify $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ before applying the proportion formula.
> 5. **Forgetting units / context in the final interpretation:** A number without an engineering interpretation earns only partial credit in KTU's outcome-based evaluation.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **confidence interval** is a *range* $[\hat{\theta} - E,\; \hat{\theta} + E]$ that brackets an unknown population parameter with probability $1 - \alpha$.
- ✅ The **margin of error** $E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$ shrinks with $\sqrt{n}$ — *quadruple the data to halve the width*.
- ✅ **Use $Z$** when $\sigma$ is known or $n \geq 30$; **use $t$** when $\sigma$ is unknown and $n < 30$.
- ✅ For **proportions**, always check $n\hat{p} \geq 5$ and $n(1-\hat{p}) \geq 5$ before using the normal approximation.
- ✅ The **$\chi^2$ interval for variance** has *asymmetric* critical values — upper tail in denominator for lower bound, and vice versa.
- ✅ Higher confidence → wider interval (trade-off between *certainty* and *precision*).
- ✅ In ML, CIs are essential for **model comparison**, **uncertainty quantification**, and **A/B testing**.
- ✅ The *frequentist* interpretation: 95% of repeated CI procedures will contain the true parameter — not 95% probability for a single interval.
- ✅ Required sample size for a given margin $E$: $n = (z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / E)^2$.
- ✅ **Critical values to memorize:** $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.005} = 2.576$, $z_{0.05} = 1.645$.
---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Cleaning: Handling Missing Values and Outliers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Cleaning: Handling Missing Values and Outliers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Data Cleaning?

**Data Cleaning** (also called *Data Cleansing* or *Data Scrubbing*) is the process of detecting, correcting, or removing corrupt, inaccurate, incomplete, or irrelevant records from a dataset. It is the foundational preprocessing step in any Machine Learning pipeline because the principle *"Garbage In → Garbage Out"* dictates that model performance is strictly bounded by input data quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Data cleaning is the process of preparing data for analysis by removing or modifying data that is incorrect, incomplete, irrelevant, duplicated, or improperly formatted. It directly addresses **MCAR**, **MAR**, and **MNAR** missingness mechanisms.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a chef preparing a dish, and the ingredients arrive in a basket. Some vegetables have rotten spots (outliers/anomalies), some are missing entirely (missing values), and some have dirt on them (noisy data). You would:
1. **Wash** the vegetables (remove noise),
2. **Discard rotten parts** (handle outliers),
3. **Substitute missing ingredients** with the closest available substitute (impute missing values).

This is exactly what data cleaning does for ML datasets — it transforms *raw, messy field data* into a *structured, model-ready matrix*.

## 1.3 Two Pillars of Data Cleaning

> [!NOTE]
> Under the KTU 2024 PEPET413 syllabus, this topic is split into two primary pillars:

| Pillar | Definition | Engineering Significance |
|--------|------------|-------------------------|
| **Handling Missing Values** | Managing absent/null/NaN entries in a feature vector | Prevents algorithmic errors in Scikit-Learn, TensorFlow |
| **Handling Outliers** | Identifying and treating extreme statistical deviations | Reduces skew, stabilizes variance, improves generalization |

### 1.3.1 Sources of Missing Data

- **System failure** — sensor dropout, I/O errors.
- **Human error** — survey non-response, manual entry omission.
- **Intentional omission** — privacy redaction, conditional skipping (e.g., income question for unemployed).
- **Data integration artifacts** — schema mismatch during ETL joins.

### 1.3.2 Sources of Outliers

- **Measurement errors** — faulty sensor calibration.
- **Data entry errors** — misplaced decimal points.
- **Sampling errors** — mixing data from different populations.
- **Genuine extreme events** — fraud detection, natural disasters (these are often *informative* and must be **kept**, not removed).

## 1.4 Formal Missingness Taxonomy (Rubin's Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every KTU question on missing data requires classifying missingness into one of these three classes:

1. **MCAR (Missing Completely At Random):** The probability of being missing is independent of both observed and unobserved data. *Example: A sensor randomly fails 2% of the time.*
2. **MAR (Missing At Random):** The probability of missingness depends only on *observed* features. *Example: Men are less likely to fill a depression survey, but age (observed) explains it.*
3. **MNAR (Missing Not At Random):** The probability depends on the missing value itself. *Example: High-income earners refusing to disclose salary.*

## 1.5 Formal Outlier Definition

An **outlier** is an observation that deviates so significantly from other observations as to arouse suspicion that it was generated by a different mechanism (Grubbs, 1969). Operationally:

$$\text{An observation } x_i \text{ is an outlier if } P(x_i \mid \text{normal model}) < \tau$$

where $\tau$ is a small significance threshold (typically **0.01** or **0.05**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Handling Missing Values

### 2.1.1 Deletion Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Deletion = removing entire rows/columns containing missing values.** Lossy, but computationally trivial.

- **Listwise Deletion (Complete Case Analysis):** Discard any row with $\geq 1$ missing cell.
  - **Pros:** Unbiased under MCAR.
  - **Cons:** Severe sample-size loss if missingness is widespread.
- **Pairwise Deletion (Available Case Analysis):** Use all available data per statistical test.
  - **Pros:** Retains more samples.
  - **Cons:** Different sample sizes per analysis → inconsistent covariance matrix.

### 2.1.2 Simple Imputation Strategies

| Method | Formula / Logic | Best Used When |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| **Mean Imputation** | $\hat{x}_i = \bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | Symmetric, no outliers |
| **Median Imputation** | $\hat{x}_i = \text{Median}(x)$ | Skewed distribution |
| **Mode Imputation** | $\hat{x}_i = \text{Mode}(x)$ | Categorical features |
| **Constant/Zero** | $\hat{x}_i = 0$ | Tree-based models |
| **Forward/Backward Fill** | $\hat{x}_t = x_{t-1}$ or $x_{t+1}$ | Time-series data |

### 2.1.3 Advanced Imputation Strategies

- **KNN Imputation:** Replace missing value with the average of $k$ nearest neighbors in feature space, weighted by inverse Euclidean distance.

$$\hat{x}_i = \frac{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_k(i)} w_{ij} \cdot x_j}{\sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}_k(i)} w_{ij}}, \quad w_{ij} = \frac{1}{\vert x_i - x_j \vert}$$

- **Regression Imputation:** Fit $x \sim X_{\text{observed}}$, then predict missing $x$.
- **Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE):** Generate $m$ plausible imputed datasets, run analysis on each, then pool results using **Rubin's Rules**.
- **Iterative Imputer (Scikit-Learn):** Bayesian Ridge regression over all features cyclically.

### 2.1.4 Indicator Variables

Add a binary flag column `was_missing` so the model can *learn* that missingness is informative (essential under **MAR** / **MNAR**).

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Outlier Handling

### 2.2.1 Statistical Detection Methods

| Method | Boundary Condition | Robustness |
|--------|-------------------|------------|
| **Z-Score** | $\vert z \vert > 3$ | Low (assumes Gaussian) |
| **Modified Z-Score (Iglewicz–Hoaglin)** | $\vert M_i \vert > 3.5$ | High (uses MAD) |
| **IQR (Tukey's Fence)** | $x < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR$ or $x > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR$ | High |
| **Grubbs' Test** | Statistical hypothesis test, $\alpha=0.05$ | Moderate |

### 2.2.2 Machine-Learning Detection Methods

- **Isolation Forest** — random partitioning isolates anomalies faster.
- **Local Outlier Factor (LOF)** — density-based deviation from neighbors.
- **DBSCAN** — points not in any dense cluster are labeled noise.
- **One-Class SVM** — learns a boundary around normal data.

### 2.2.3 Treatment Strategies

| Strategy | Mechanism | Trade-off |
|----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Trimming** | Discard outlier rows | Data loss |
| **Capping (Winsorization)** | Clip to $Q_1, Q_3$ percentiles | Preserves sample size |
| **Log Transform** | Compress right tail | Useful for skewed positive data |
| **Robust Scaling** | Use median & IQR instead of mean & std | Reduces influence without removal |
| **Imputation** | Treat outliers as missing | Most rigorous |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table verbatim** — these are the highest-frequency equations in KTU ESE and Module Internal exams.

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Constant |
|---|---------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = sample size |
| 2 | Median (sorted $x$) | $x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)}$ | Robust to outliers |
| 3 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | — |
| 4 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^2}$ | — |
| 5 | Z-Score | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Threshold: $\vert z \vert > 3$ |
| 6 | Modified Z-Score | $M_i = \dfrac{0.6745(x_i - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}$ | Threshold: $\vert M_i \vert > 3.5$ |
| 7 | MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) | $\text{MAD} = \text{Median}(\vert x_i - \tilde{x} \vert)$ | Robust scale |
| 8 | IQR | $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$ | — |
| 9 | Tukey's Lower Fence | $L = Q_1 - k \cdot \text{IQR}$ | $k=1.5$ mild, $k=3.0$ extreme |
| 10 | Tukey's Upper Fence | $U = Q_3 + k \cdot \text{IQR}$ | $k=1.5$ mild, $k=3.0$ extreme |
| 11 | Grubbs' Statistic | $G = \dfrac{\max \vert x_i - \bar{x} \vert}{\sigma}$ | Compare to $G_{\text{crit}}$ |
| 12 | Imputation Error (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^2}$ | Lower is better |
| 13 | KNN Imputation Weight | $w_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{\vert x_i - x_j \vert + \epsilon}$ | $\epsilon$ avoids div-by-zero |

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Medical diagnostics:** Outliers can indicate tumor mismeasurement.
- **Finance:** Outliers = fraud signals; missing values = uncollected market data.
- **IoT/Sensor networks:** Missing values = battery/connectivity loss.
- **Recommendation systems:** Imputation feeds cold-start users.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite):** *"Always create an indicator variable for missingness before imputing — information loss from deletion is rarely acceptable in production ML systems."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Numerical Examples

## 3.1 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Expectation:** KTU answers must justify *why* a method was chosen, not just state it. This matrix gives you the "why" instantly.

| Data Condition | Missingness Type | Outlier Presence | Recommended Strategy |
|----------------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Small dataset, MCAR | MCAR | None | Listwise deletion |
| Large dataset, MAR | MAR | Few | Mean/Median imputation + indicator |
| Time-series | Any | Smoothed | Forward fill / Interpolation |
| Skewed numeric | Any | Heavy-tailed | Median + IQR capping |
| Mixed features | MAR | Yes | MICE / Iterative Imputer |
| Tree-based model | Any | Yes | No scaling needed, light imputation |
| Linear regression | MAR | Yes | Median imputation + RobustScaler |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — IQR-Based Outlier Detection

**Dataset:** $\{4, 7, 2, 9, 12, 15, 3, 8, 100, 6\}$ ($n=10$)

### Step 1: Sort the Data
$$\text{Sorted} = \{2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 12, 15, 100\}$$

### Step 2: Compute Quartiles
Since $n=10$ (even), the median is the average of the 5th and 6th elements:

$$\text{Median } (Q_2) = \frac{7 + 8}{2} = 7.5$$

Lower half $= \{2, 3, 4, 6, 7\}$, so $Q_1 = \text{median of lower half} = 4$.

Upper half $= \{8, 9, 12, 15, 100\}$, so $Q_3 = \text{median of upper half} = 12$.

### Step 3: Compute IQR
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 12 - 4 = 8$$

### Step 4: Compute Tukey's Fences
$$L = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 4 - 12 = -8$$

$$U = Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR} = 12 + 12 = 24$$

### Step 5: Identify Outliers
Any $x < -8$ or $x > 24$ is an outlier. Only $x = 100$ exceeds $24$.

$$\boxed{\text{Outlier} = \{100\}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Z-Score Outlier Detection

**Dataset:** $\{10, 12, 11, 13, 12, 14, 100, 11, 13\}$ ($n=9$)

### Step 1: Compute Mean
$$\bar{x} = \frac{10+12+11+13+12+14+100+11+13}{9} = \frac{196}{9} \approx 21.78$$

### Step 2: Compute Standard Deviation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2 &= \frac{1}{9}\left[(-11.78)^2 + (-9.78)^2 + (-10.78)^2 + (-8.78)^2 + (-9.78)^2 + (-7.78)^2 + (78.22)^2 + (-10.78)^2 + (-8.78)^2 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{9}\left[138.77 + 95.65 + 116.21 + 77.09 + 95.65 + 60.53 + 6118.37 + 116.21 + 77.09 \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{9} \times 6895.57 \approx 766.17
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{766.17} \approx 27.68$$

### Step 3: Compute Z-Scores

$$z_{100} = \frac{100 - 21.78}{27.68} \approx 2.82$$

$$z_{10} = \frac{10 - 21.78}{27.68} \approx -0.43$$

### Step 4: Decision
With threshold $\vert z \vert > 3$, even 100 is borderline (2.82). With threshold $\vert z \vert > 2.5$, 100 is flagged. **Conclusion:** Z-score is *not* robust here because the outlier itself inflates $\sigma$ — illustrating why the **MAD-based Modified Z-Score** is preferred.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Modified Z-Score (Robust) on Same Data

### Step 1: Compute Median
$$\tilde{x} = 12$$

### Step 2: Compute Absolute Deviations
$$\vert x_i - 12 \vert = \{2, 0, 1, 1, 0, 2, 88, 1, 1\}$$

### Step 3: Compute MAD
$$\text{MAD} = \text{Median of } \{2, 0, 1, 1, 0, 2, 88, 1, 1\} = 1$$

### Step 4: Compute Modified Z-Score for $x = 100$
$$M_{100} = \frac{0.6745 \times (100 - 12)}{1} = 0.6745 \times 88 = 59.36$$

Since $\vert M_{100} \vert = 59.36 \gg 3.5$, the value **100 is definitively an outlier**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice how the regular Z-score (2.82) *underestimated* the anomaly because $\sigma$ was inflated by the very outlier we are trying to detect. The MAD-based metric remains immune. This is why KTU examiners specifically test the Modified Z-Score formula.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — KNN Imputation (Conceptual Walkthrough)

**Setup:** Feature $X_1$ is missing for row $i=5$. Compute $k=3$ nearest neighbors using $X_2, X_3$.

| Row | $X_1$ | $X_2$ | $X_3$ | Distance to row 5 |
|-----|-------|-------|-------|--------------------|
| 1   | 5     | 10    | 8     | $\sqrt{(10-9)^2+(8-7)^2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$ |
| 2   | 7     | 11    | 9     | $\sqrt{4+4} = 2.83$ |
| 3   | 4     | 8     | 6     | $\sqrt{1+1} = 1.41$ |
| 4   | 6     | 9     | 8     | $\sqrt{0+1} = 1.00$ |

Nearest neighbors (top-3 by distance): rows $\{4, 1, 3\}$ with $X_1$ values $\{6, 5, 4\}$.

$$\hat{x}_{1,5} = \frac{6 + 5 + 4}{3} = 5$$

If using distance-weighted KNN:

$$w_4 = \frac{1}{1.00} = 1.00, \quad w_1 = \frac{1}{1.41} = 0.71, \quad w_3 = \frac{1}{1.41} = 0.71$$

$$\hat{x}_{1,5} = \frac{(1.00 \cdot 6) + (0.71 \cdot 5) + (0.71 \cdot 4)}{1.00 + 0.71 + 0.71} = \frac{6 + 3.55 + 2.84}{2.42} \approx 5.12$$

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Mean Imputation Variance Distortion

**Original:** $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$, $\bar{x}=3$, $\sigma^2 = 2.0$.

**Add missing:** Replace $x=5$ with $\bar{x}=3$.

**New set:** $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 3\}$, $\bar{x}_{\text{new}}=2.6$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^2_{\text{new}} &= \frac{(1-2.6)^2 + (2-2.6)^2 + (3-2.6)^2 + (4-2.6)^2 + (3-2.6)^2}{5} \\
&= \frac{2.56 + 0.36 + 0.16 + 1.96 + 0.16}{5} = \frac{5.20}{5} = 1.04
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Variance dropped from 2.0 → 1.04.** This is a critical KTU-level pitfall: mean imputation *artificially shrinks variance*, making downstream statistical tests (t-tests, confidence intervals) overconfident and biased toward false positives.

## 3.7 Grubbs' Test Derivation (for completeness)

Grubbs' test statistic for the largest value:

$$G = \frac{\max(x_i) - \bar{x}}{s}$$

Under $H_0$: no outliers, the critical value is:

$$G_{\text{crit}} = \frac{(n-1)}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\frac{t_{\alpha/(2n), n-2}^2}{n - 2 + t_{\alpha/(2n), n-2}^2}}$$

Reject $H_0$ if $G > G_{\text{crit}}$. **KTU Note:** This is rarely asked in full derivation — focus on the *logic* and threshold comparison.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Data Cleaning Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Dataset] --> B[Missing Value Detection]
    B --> C{Missingness Type?}
    C -->|MCAR| D[Simple Deletion or Mean Imputation]
    C -->|MAR| E[Regression or MICE Imputation]
    C -->|MNAR| F[Domain Modeling Required]
    D --> G[Outlier Detection]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{Outlier Strategy?}
    H -->|Remove| I[Trimming]
    H -->|Cap| J[Winsorization]
    H -->|Transform| K[Log or Robust Scale]
    I --> L[Clean Dataset]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Missingness Mechanism Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Examine Missing Pattern] --> B{Does missingness depend on observed data?}
    B -->|Yes| C[MAR - Use Regression Imputation]
    B -->|No| D{Does missingness depend on the missing value itself?}
    D -->|Yes| E[MNAR - Domain Knowledge Needed]
    D -->|No| F[MCAR - Safe to Delete or Mean Impute]
```

## 4.3 Outlier Detection Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Outlier Detection Method] --> B{Is data Gaussian?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Z-Score with threshold 3]
    B -->|No| D{Is data small dimensional?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use IQR Tukey Fence]
    D -->|No| F{Is data high dimensional?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Isolation Forest]
    F -->|No| H{Need density-based?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use LOF or DBSCAN]
    H -->|No| J[Use Modified Z-Score with MAD]
```

## 4.4 IQR Fence Visualization (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Minimum] --> B[Q1 minus 1.5 times IQR]
    B --> C[Q1]
    C --> D[Median Q2]
    D --> E[Q3]
    E --> F[Q3 plus 1.5 times IQR]
    F --> G[Maximum]
    H[Outliers below] -.-> B
    I[Outliers above] -.-> F
```

## 4.5 MICE Imputation Iterative Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize all missing with mean] --> B[Loop over each feature with missing]
    B --> C[Treat other features as predictors]
    C --> D[Fit regression model]
    D --> E[Predict and update missing values]
    E --> F{Converged?}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Output Imputed Dataset]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between MCAR, MAR, and MNAR missingness mechanisms with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **MCAR (Missing Completely At Random):** Probability of a value being missing is entirely independent of any data, observed or unobserved. Example: A laboratory thermometer randomly fails for 2% of measurements due to an electronic glitch.
- **MAR (Missing At Random):** Probability of missingness depends only on observed variables. Example: In a job survey, salary is more often missing for females, but if gender is observed, the missingness can be modeled.
- **MNAR (Missing Not At Random):** Probability depends on the missing value itself. Example: High-income earners deliberately refuse to disclose their salary in surveys.

**Awarding Logic:** 1 mark per definition + example pair. Full marks require distinguishing *what the missingness depends on* in each case.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the IQR method for outlier detection. Why is it preferred over the Z-score method for skewed data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The IQR method identifies outliers using Tukey's fences: any value $x$ such that $x < Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ or $x > Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ is flagged as an outlier, where $\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1$.
- The Z-score method assumes Gaussian distribution and is highly sensitive to the outliers themselves (which inflate $\sigma$). The IQR method uses quartiles which are robust statistics (only 25% of data can affect a quartile), making it suitable for skewed, heavy-tailed, or non-normal distributions.

**Awarding Logic:** 1 mark for IQR formula, 1 mark for Z-score limitation, 1 mark for IQR robustness justification.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Consider the dataset $\{22, 25, 28, 24, 26, 27, 29, 23, 100, 21, 25, 24\}$.
(i) Detect outliers using the IQR method.
(ii) Detect outliers using the Z-score method.
(iii) Compute the Modified Z-score using MAD for the same data and comment on which method is most reliable.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort the data:**
$\{21, 22, 23, 24, 24, 25, 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 100\}$, $n = 12$.

**Step 2 — Quartiles (for $n=12$):**
Median $Q_2$ = average of 6th and 7th elements $= \frac{25 + 25}{2} = 25$.
Lower half (6 elements): $\{21, 22, 23, 24, 24, 25\}$, so $Q_1 = \frac{23 + 24}{2} = 23.5$.
Upper half (6 elements): $\{25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 100\}$, so $Q_3 = \frac{27 + 28}{2} = 27.5$.

**Step 3 — IQR:**
$$\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1 = 27.5 - 23.5 = 4$$

**Step 4 — Tukey's Fences:**
$$L = 23.5 - 1.5 \times 4 = 17.5$$

$$U = 27.5 + 1.5 \times 4 = 33.5$$

Since $100 > 33.5$, the value **100 is an outlier**.

**Step 5 — Z-score method:**
$\bar{x} = \dfrac{21+22+23+24+24+25+25+26+27+28+29+100}{12} = \dfrac{374}{12} \approx 31.17$.

$\sigma \approx 21.93$ (compute manually).

$z_{100} = \dfrac{100 - 31.17}{21.93} \approx 3.14 > 3$. So 100 is detected.

**Step 6 — Modified Z-score with MAD:**
$\tilde{x} = 25$. Absolute deviations: $\{4, 3, 2, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 75\}$. Sorted, MAD = median of these = 2.

$M_{100} = \dfrac{0.6745 \times (100 - 25)}{2} = 25.29 \gg 3.5$.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Modified Z-score yields the largest anomaly score (25.29) because it is not inflated by the outlier itself, making it the **most reliable** method among the three.

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** Discuss in detail the various strategies for handling missing values. State one disadvantage of each.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Deletion Methods (2 marks):**
- *Listwise Deletion:* Discard any row with missing values. **Disadvantage:** Reduces sample size, introduces bias if data is not MCAR.
- *Pairwise Deletion:* Use available data per analysis. **Disadvantage:** Different sample sizes lead to inconsistent results.

**2. Simple Imputation (2 marks):**
- *Mean/Median/Mode:* Replace missing value with central tendency. **Disadvantage:** Underestimates variance, distorts correlations, biased for MNAR.
- *Forward/Backward Fill:* Use adjacent time-series values. **Disadvantage:** Inappropriate for non-temporal data.

**3. Advanced Imputation (2 marks):**
- *KNN Imputation:* Use similar rows. **Disadvantage:** Computationally expensive for large $n$, sensitive to feature scaling.
- *MICE:* Multiple iterations using regression. **Disadvantage:** Complex, may overfit, requires convergence tuning.
- *Regression Imputation:* Predict from observed features. **Disadvantage:** Underestimates standard errors (single imputation).

**4. Indicator Variable (1 mark):** Add a binary flag for missingness. **Disadvantage:** Increases dimensionality.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Sub-part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the difference between outliers and noise. Discuss the Isolation Forest algorithm for outlier detection in detail.

**Model Solution:**

**Outliers vs Noise (3 marks):**
- **Outliers:** Observations significantly different from the majority but are *valid data points* (e.g., a billionaire in a salary dataset).
- **Noise:** Random error or variance in measurement that obscures the underlying signal. Noise is *always undesirable*; outliers can be informative (e.g., fraud).
- Noise is a *type* of outlier, but not every outlier is noise.

**Isolation Forest (4 marks):**
- Proposed by Liu, Ting, Zhou (2008).
- **Core idea:** Anomalies are *few* and *different*, so they are easier to **isolate** via random partitioning.
- **Algorithm:**
  1. Build an ensemble of $t$ Isolation Trees (iTrees).
  2. Each iTree is built by randomly selecting a feature $q$ and split value $p$ until every point is isolated.
  3. The **path length** $h(x)$ from root to leaf is recorded.
  4. The **anomaly score** is:

$$s(x, n) = 2^{-\frac{E[h(x)]}{c(n)}}$$

where $c(n) = 2H(n-1) - \frac{2(n-1)}{n}$ is the average path length of an unsuccessful search in a BST, and $H(i) = \ln(i) + \gamma$ (Euler–Mascheroni constant).

**Interpretation:**
- $s \approx 1$: anomaly
- $s \approx 0.5$: normal
- $s < 0.5$: not anomalous

---

**Sub-part (b) [7 Marks]:** With a suitable example, explain the MICE (Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations) algorithm. Why is it preferred over single imputation?

**Model Solution:**

**MICE Algorithm (4 marks):**
- Also known as *Fully Conditional Specification*.
- **Algorithm Steps:**
  1. Initialize: Fill all missing values with mean (or any simple estimate).
  2. For iteration $t = 1, 2, \ldots, T$:
     - For each variable $X_j$ with missing values:
       - Treat $X_j$ as the response.
       - Use other variables $X_{-j}$ (with their current imputed values) as predictors.
       - Fit a regression model (Bayesian Ridge / Logistic for categorical).
       - Impute the missing entries of $X_j$ from the posterior predictive distribution.
  3. Repeat until convergence (chain stabilization).

**Example (1 mark):** In a medical dataset with missing `blood_pressure` and `cholesterol`, MICE alternates: first predict missing BP from age, weight, height; then predict missing cholesterol using the updated BP, age, weight.

**Why MICE is Preferred (2 marks):**
- Single imputation (e.g., mean) underestimates variance and overstates certainty.
- MICE generates $m$ imputed datasets → captures **uncertainty** in missing values.
- Results are pooled using **Rubin's combining rules**, producing statistically valid standard errors.
- Works under MAR assumption and is more accurate than single regression imputation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "outlier" with "noise":** Outliers can be informative (e.g., fraud). Do not blindly delete them.
> 2. **Forgetting MCAR assumption:** Mean/median imputation and listwise deletion are valid **only under MCAR**. Always state the assumption.
> 3. **Wrong formula for MAD:** It is the **median** of absolute deviations from the **median**, not the mean.
> 4. **Mixing up IQR bounds:** $L = Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ (not $Q_1 - 1.5\sigma$).
> 5. **Not justifying the choice of method:** KTU expects the "why" — always say "IQR is used because the data is skewed" rather than just stating the formula.
> 6. **Missing variance reduction caveat:** When asked about mean imputation, **always mention** the variance underestimation artifact.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Cleaning: Missing Values & Outliers**

- **Missingness Taxonomy:** MCAR → MAR → MNAR (progressively more complex; weaker assumptions).
- **Deletion vs Imputation:** Deletion is lossy; imputation preserves $n$ but may distort variance.
- **Indicator Variable trick:** Always add a `was_missing` column when missingness may be informative.
- **MICE is the gold standard** for MAR data with mixed types.
- **KNN imputation:** Distance-weighted; sensitive to feature scaling (always standardize first).
- **Z-Score:** Use only for Gaussian data; threshold $\vert z \vert > 3$.
- **Modified Z-Score (MAD-based):** Threshold $\vert M_i \vert > 3.5$; robust to outliers themselves.
- **IQR Method:** Tukey's fences at $1.5 \cdot \text{IQR}$ (mild) or $3.0 \cdot \text{IQR}$ (extreme); distribution-free.
- **Winsorization (Capping):** Replace values beyond percentiles with the percentile value itself.
- **Isolation Forest:** Anomaly score $s = 2^{-E[h(x)]/c(n)}$; path-length based, scales to high dimensions.
- **LOF & DBSCAN:** Density-based; identify points in sparse regions.
- **Outliers ≠ Noise:** Outliers may carry signal (fraud, defect); noise is always undesirable.
- **Engineer's Mantra:** *Detect → Diagnose → Decide → Document* (the 4D protocol).
- **Variance pitfall:** Mean imputation always reduces variance — a favorite KTU trick question.
- **KTU's favorite 14-mark combo:** "Compare IQR vs Z-score" + "Discuss missing value strategies with disadvantages."
- **Always quote the threshold constant** ($1.5$, $3$, $3.5$, etc.) — failing to do so loses 1 mark.
- **For Grubbs' Test:** Single outlier hypothesis test, requires normality assumption.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Normalization and Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Normalization and Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Normalization and Scaling** are fundamental **data preprocessing techniques** in machine learning that transform the values of numeric features to a **common scale or range**, without distorting differences in the ranges of values or losing essential information. They are a critical step in the **Feature Engineering** pipeline, executed *after* data cleaning and *before* model training.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *"Data normalization (or feature scaling) refers to the process of rescaling the values of features so that they have a uniform scale or distribution. This is required because features measured at different scales do not contribute equally to the model fitting and can unduly bias the learning algorithm."*

In rigorous statistical terms, normalization is a **bijective (one-to-one) transformation function** $f: \mathbb{R}^n \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n$ that maps raw feature vectors to a new domain where the transformed attributes satisfy a pre-defined distributional property (e.g., zero mean, unit variance, or a fixed range like $[0, 1]$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a teacher grading two students on completely different scales:

| Student | Math Test (out of 100) | Height (in centimeters) |
|---|---|---|
| Alice | **88** | **150** |
| Bob | **72** | **180** |

If you wanted to compute a "composite score" by simply adding the two raw values, Alice would unfairly dominate because her Math score is a much larger raw number than Bob's height. **Scaling puts both features on the same ruler** so that neither one unfairly overwhelms the other.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Comparing Apples and Oranges" Analogy:** When a machine learning algorithm sees raw data like `Income = 75,000` and `Age = 28`, it perceives Income as "more important" simply because its numerical magnitude is larger — not because it is genuinely more predictive. Normalization is the process of converting both quantities to the same unit of "importance" so the algorithm judges them fairly.

## 1.3 Why Normalization is Critical — Core Drivers

1. **Distance-Based Algorithms Depend on Scale:** Algorithms such as **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)**, **K-Means Clustering**, **Support Vector Machines (SVM)**, and **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** compute Euclidean or similar distances. If one feature dominates the distance metric, the algorithm is biased.
2. **Gradient Descent Convergence:** Neural Networks and Linear/Logistic Regression trained via gradient descent converge **much faster** when features are on similar scales, because the loss surface becomes more spherical (less skewed).
3. **Regularization Fairness:** $L_1$ and $L_2$ regularization penalize coefficients. Without scaling, the penalty is unfairly distributed across coefficients.
4. **Numerical Stability:** Avoids overflow/underflow in computations involving exponentials or products of feature values.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Heuristic for KTU Exams:** Tree-based algorithms (Decision Trees, Random Forest, XGBoost) are **scale-invariant** — they do NOT require normalization. Always mention this contrast in answers to score full marks on "which algorithms need scaling" questions.

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used Throughout This Module

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $X$ | Raw feature value |
| $X'$ or $X_{scaled}$ | Transformed (scaled) value |
| $X_{min}, X_{max}$ | Minimum and maximum of the feature column |
| $\mu$ | Arithmetic mean of the feature |
| $\sigma$ | Standard deviation of the feature |
| $Q_1, Q_2, Q_3$ | First, second (median), and third quartiles |
| $IQR$ | Interquartile Range $= Q_3 - Q_1$ |
| $j$ | Smallest integer such that $\max \vert X' \vert < 1$ |
| $\lVert X \rVert_2$ | $L_2$ (Euclidean) norm of vector $X$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Principal Normalization Techniques

### A. Min-Max Normalization (Rescaling)
- **Operational Logic:** Linearly rescales every value into a fixed range, typically $[0, 1]$.
- **Core Idea:** Subtract the minimum, then divide by the range. The smallest value becomes 0 and the largest becomes 1.
- **Best For:** Bounded-range algorithms (Neural Networks, image processing, CNNs with pixel intensities).

### B. Z-Score Normalization (Standardization)
- **Operational Logic:** Centers the data around 0 with unit standard deviation.
- **Core Idea:** Subtract the mean, then divide by the standard deviation. The output is a dimensionless **z-statistic**.
- **Best For:** Algorithms that assume Gaussian / zero-centered data (Logistic Regression, SVM, PCA, LDA, Linear Regression).

### C. Robust Scaling (Median and IQR Based)
- **Operational Logic:** Uses the **median** and **interquartile range** instead of mean and std, making it **immune to outliers**.
- **Core Idea:** Subtract $Q_2$, then divide by $IQR$. Robust to extreme values.
- **Best For:** Datasets with significant outliers (e.g., income, house prices, sensor anomalies).

### D. MaxAbs Scaling
- **Operational Logic:** Scales each feature by its maximum absolute value, mapping data to $[-1, +1]$.
- **Core Idea:** Preserves **sparsity** (zero entries remain zero) — critical for sparse matrix libraries like SciPy.
- **Best For:** Sparse data (text TF-IDF matrices, one-hot encoded categorical data).

### E. Decimal Scaling
- **Operational Logic:** Divides values by a power of 10 chosen so that the absolute maximum is less than 1.
- **Core Idea:** Crude but fast normalization using base-10 shifts.
- **Best For:** Legacy systems, very large or very small data values.

### F. Unit Vector Normalization (L2 Normalization)
- **Operational Logic:** Scales an *entire observation row* (not a column) so that its Euclidean length becomes 1.
- **Core Idea:** The vector becomes a point on the unit hypersphere.
- **Best For:** Cosine similarity computations, text classification, vector embeddings.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important revision artifact** for the university exam. Memorize it cold.

| # | Technique | Transformation Formula | Output Range | Sensitive to Outliers? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Min-Max Scaling** | $X' = \dfrac{X - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}}$ | $[0, 1]$ (or custom $[a,b]$) | **Yes** |
| 2 | **Z-Score Standardization** | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Typically $[-3, +3]$ (99.7% of data) | **Yes** |
| 3 | **Robust Scaling** | $X' = \dfrac{X - Q_2}{Q_3 - Q_1}$ | Unbounded, but tight | **No** (Robust) |
| 4 | **MaxAbs Scaling** | $X' = \dfrac{X}{\max \vert X \vert}$ | $[-1, +1]$ | **Yes** |
| 5 | **Decimal Scaling** | $X' = \dfrac{X}{10^j}$ | $\vert X' \vert < 1$ | **Yes** |
| 6 | **Unit Vector (L2)** | $X' = \dfrac{X}{\lVert X \rVert_2}$ | $\lVert X' \rVert_2 = 1$ | **Yes** |

### Custom-Range Min-Max Extension (Often Tested!)
When the desired range is $[a, b]$ instead of $[0, 1]$:

$$X' = a + \frac{(X - X_{min}) \cdot (b - a)}{X_{max} - X_{min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variance and Mean After Standardization (Exam Favorite):**
> After applying Z-score normalization, by construction:
> - **Mean:** $\mu' = 0$
> - **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma' = 1$
> - **Variance:** ${\sigma'}^2 = 1$
>
> This is *the* standard follow-up question in KTU valuation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Use Case | Technique Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Computer Vision (CNNs)** | Pixel intensity rescaling $[0, 255] \to [0, 1]$ | Min-Max |
| **NLP (Text Classification)** | TF-IDF vector row normalization for cosine similarity | L2 Unit Vector |
| **Financial Fraud Detection** | Income/transaction features with heavy-tailed outliers | Robust Scaling |
| **Genomics / Bioinformatics** | Gene expression values spanning many orders of magnitude | Log Transform + Z-Score |
| **IoT Sensor Fusion** | Combining temperature ($\sim$25), humidity ($\sim$80), and pressure ($\sim$1013) | Min-Max or Z-Score |
| **Recommender Systems** | Sparse user-item rating matrices | MaxAbs (preserves sparsity) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Master Example Dataset

To make derivations concrete, consider the following single feature vector representing **"Age of Patients"** in a clinical study:

$$\mathbf{X} = \{15, \ 18, \ 20, \ 22, \ 25, \ 30, \ 35, \ 40, \ 50, \ 60\}$$

Here, $n = 10$ observations.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Compute the Summary Statistics

**Sum:**
$$\sum X_i = 15 + 18 + 20 + 22 + 25 + 30 + 35 + 40 + 50 + 60 = 315$$

**Mean:**
$$\mu = \frac{\sum X_i}{n} = \frac{315}{10} = 31.5$$

**Minimum and Maximum:**
$$X_{min} = 15, \quad X_{max} = 60$$

**Range:**
$$R = X_{max} - X_{min} = 60 - 15 = 45$$

**Deviations from mean $(X_i - \mu)$:**

| $X_i$ | 15 | 18 | 20 | 22 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $X_i - \mu$ | $-16.5$ | $-13.5$ | $-11.5$ | $-9.5$ | $-6.5$ | $-1.5$ | $+3.5$ | $+8.5$ | $+18.5$ | $+28.5$ |

**Squared deviations $(X_i - \mu)^2$:**

| $X_i$ | 15 | 18 | 20 | 22 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 40 | 50 | 60 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $(X_i - \mu)^2$ | $272.25$ | $182.25$ | $132.25$ | $90.25$ | $42.25$ | $2.25$ | $12.25$ | $72.25$ | $342.25$ | $812.25$ |

**Sum of squared deviations:**
$$\sum (X_i - \mu)^2 = 272.25 + 182.25 + 132.25 + 90.25 + 42.25 + 2.25 + 12.25 + 72.25 + 342.25 + 812.25 = 1960.5$$

**Variance (population):**
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum (X_i - \mu)^2}{n} = \frac{1960.5}{10} = 196.05$$

**Standard Deviation:**
$$\sigma = \sqrt{196.05} \approx 14.0018$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice (and in Scikit-Learn's `StandardScaler`), the **sample standard deviation** is used, which divides by $n - 1 = 9$:
> $$s = \sqrt{\frac{1960.5}{9}} = \sqrt{217.833} \approx 14.759$$
> For KTU exams, either is acceptable but always **state which one you are using**.

**Quartile computation** (for Robust Scaling):
The sorted data is already ordered. With $n = 10$ (even), the median splits the data:
- Lower half: $\{15, 18, 20, 22, 25\}$
- Upper half: $\{30, 35, 40, 50, 60\}$

$$Q_1 = \text{median of lower half} = \frac{20 + 22}{2} = 21$$

$$Q_2 = \text{median of full data} = \frac{25 + 30}{2} = 27.5$$

$$Q_3 = \text{median of upper half} = \frac{40 + 50}{2} = 45$$

$$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 45 - 21 = 24$$

---

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Apply Each Normalization Technique

We will normalize the specific value $X = 25$ to demonstrate all six methods.

#### (a) Min-Max Normalization
Using the canonical formula:

$$X' = \frac{X - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}}$$

Substituting $X = 25$, $X_{min} = 15$, $X_{max} = 60$:

$$X' = \frac{25 - 15}{60 - 15} = \frac{10}{45} = 0.2222$$

**Result:** $X'_{MinMax} = 0.2222$ (lies in $[0, 1]$ ✓)

#### (b) Z-Score Standardization
Using the formula:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Substituting $X = 25$, $\mu = 31.5$, $\sigma = 14.0018$:

$$Z = \frac{25 - 31.5}{14.0018} = \frac{-6.5}{14.0018} = -0.4642$$

**Result:** $Z \approx -0.4642$ (lies within $\pm 3\sigma$ ✓)

#### (c) Robust Scaling
Using the formula:

$$X' = \frac{X - Q_2}{Q_3 - Q_1}$$

Substituting $X = 25$, $Q_2 = 27.5$, $Q_3 - Q_1 = 24$:

$$X' = \frac{25 - 27.5}{24} = \frac{-2.5}{24} = -0.1042$$

**Result:** $X'_{Robust} = -0.1042$

#### (d) MaxAbs Scaling
Using the formula:

$$X' = \frac{X}{\max \vert X \vert}$$

Here, $\max \vert X_i \vert = 60$. Substituting $X = 25$:

$$X' = \frac{25}{60} = 0.4167$$

**Result:** $X'_{MaxAbs} = 0.4167$ (lies in $[-1, +1]$ ✓)

#### (e) Decimal Scaling
The largest absolute value is $60$. We must find the smallest integer $j$ such that:
$$\frac{\max \vert X_i \vert}{10^j} < 1$$

We test values: $\frac{60}{10^1} = 6 \not< 1$; $\frac{60}{10^2} = 0.6 < 1$ ✓. So $j = 2$.

Applying the formula:

$$X' = \frac{X}{10^j} = \frac{25}{10^2} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25$$

**Result:** $X'_{Decimal} = 0.25$

#### (f) Unit Vector (L2) Normalization
This operates on a *row vector*. Consider the 2D feature vector $\mathbf{v} = (25, \ 40)$ (combining Age and, say, Cholesterol level).

**Compute the L2 norm:**

$$\lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2 = \sqrt{25^2 + 40^2} = \sqrt{625 + 1600} = \sqrt{2225} \approx 47.1699$$

**Normalize:**

$$\mathbf{v}' = \frac{\mathbf{v}}{\lVert \mathbf{v} \rVert_2} = \left( \frac{25}{47.1699}, \ \frac{40}{47.1699} \right) \approx (0.5300, \ 0.8480)$$

**Verification:** $\lVert \mathbf{v}' \rVert_2 = \sqrt{0.5300^2 + 0.8480^2} = \sqrt{0.2809 + 0.7191} = \sqrt{1.0000} = 1$ ✓

---

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Full Transformation Table for the Dataset

Applying Min-Max and Z-Score to the entire dataset:

| $X_i$ | $X_i - X_{min}$ | $X_i - \mu$ | **Min-Max $X'$** | **Z-Score $Z$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 15 | $0$ | $-16.5$ | $0/45 = 0.0000$ | $-16.5/14.00 = -1.1785$ |
| 18 | $3$ | $-13.5$ | $3/45 = 0.0667$ | $-13.5/14.00 = -0.9642$ |
| 20 | $5$ | $-11.5$ | $5/45 = 0.1111$ | $-11.5/14.00 = -0.8214$ |
| 22 | $7$ | $-9.5$ | $7/45 = 0.1556$ | $-9.5/14.00 = -0.6785$ |
| 25 | $10$ | $-6.5$ | $10/45 = 0.2222$ | $-6.5/14.00 = -0.4642$ |
| 30 | $15$ | $-1.5$ | $15/45 = 0.3333$ | $-1.5/14.00 = -0.1071$ |
| 35 | $20$ | $+3.5$ | $20/45 = 0.4444$ | $+3.5/14.00 = +0.2500$ |
| 40 | $25$ | $+8.5$ | $25/45 = 0.5556$ | $+8.5/14.00 = +0.6071$ |
| 50 | $35$ | $+18.5$ | $35/45 = 0.7778$ | $+18.5/14.00 = +1.3214$ |
| 60 | $45$ | $+28.5$ | $45/45 = 1.0000$ | $+28.5/14.00 = +2.0357$ |

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Verification of Post-Standardization Properties

**Mean of Z-scores** (must equal 0):
$$\mu_Z = \frac{1}{10}\sum Z_i = \frac{-1.1785 - 0.9642 - 0.8214 - 0.6785 - 0.4642 - 0.1071 + 0.2500 + 0.6071 + 1.3214 + 2.0357}{10}$$
$$\mu_Z = \frac{0.0003}{10} \approx 0 \checkmark$$

**Standard deviation of Z-scores** (must equal 1):
$$\sigma_Z = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (Z_i - \mu_Z)^2}{n}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{9.9996}{10}} \approx 1.0 \checkmark$$

(Tiny floating-point rounding errors are expected and acceptable.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Decision Flowchart — "Which Normalization Should I Use?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Raw Feature X"] --> nodeB{"Is the data SPARSE? (e.g. TF-IDF, one-hot)"}
    nodeB -- "YES" --> nodeC["Use MAXABS SCALING"]
    nodeB -- "NO" --> nodeD{"Are there SIGNIFICANT OUTLIERS in the feature?"}
    nodeD -- "YES" --> nodeE["Use ROBUST SCALING (Median and IQR)"]
    nodeD -- "NO" --> nodeF{"Does the algorithm assume a GAUSSIAN / ZERO-CENTERED distribution?"}
    nodeF -- "YES" --> nodeG["Use Z-SCORE STANDARDIZATION"]
    nodeF -- "NO" --> nodeH{"Does the algorithm require a BOUNDED range like 0 to 1?"}
    nodeH -- "YES" --> nodeI["Use MIN-MAX SCALING"]
    nodeH -- "NO" --> nodeJ{"Are you computing COSINE SIMILARITY between rows?"}
    nodeJ -- "YES" --> nodeK["Use L2 UNIT VECTOR NORMALIZATION"]
    nodeJ -- "NO" --> nodeL["Use DECIMAL SCALING or revisit the problem"]
    nodeC --> nodeM["OUTPUT: X' ready for ML model"]
    nodeE --> nodeM
    nodeG --> nodeM
    nodeI --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
```

## 4.2 Algorithm Sensitivity Matrix — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["SCALE-INSENSITIVE ALGORITHMS (No Normalization Required)"]
        nodeA1["Decision Trees"]
        nodeA2["Random Forest"]
        nodeA3["XGBoost / LightGBM"]
        nodeA4["CatBoost"]
    end
    subgraph B["SCALE-SENSITIVE ALGORITHMS (Normalization Required)"]
        nodeB1["KNN and K-Means"]
        nodeB2["SVM with RBF Kernel"]
        nodeB3["Logistic and Linear Regression"]
        nodeB4["PCA and LDA"]
        nodeB5["Neural Networks Deep Learning"]
    end
    A -. "Reason: Split-based decisions" .-> nodeA1
    B -. "Reason: Distance or gradient based" .-> nodeB1
```

## 4.3 Data Preprocessing Pipeline — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP1["Raw Data Ingestion"] --> nodeP2{"Missing Values?"}
    nodeP2 -- "YES" --> nodeP3["Imputation: Mean / Median / Mode"]
    nodeP2 -- "NO" --> nodeP4["Outlier Detection and Treatment"]
    nodeP3 --> nodeP4
    nodeP4 --> nodeP5{"Train-Test Split FIRST"}
    nodeP5 -- "CRITICAL" --> nodeP6["Fit Scaler on TRAIN data only"]
    nodeP6 --> nodeP7["Apply SAME scaler to TEST data"]
    nodeP7 --> nodeP8["ML Model Training"]
    nodeP8 --> nodeP9["Evaluation and Tuning"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Data Leakage Pitfall:** The scaler (`fit`) statistics ($\mu$, $\sigma$, $X_{min}$, $X_{max}$) must be computed **only on the training set**, and then `transform` applied to both train and test sets. Computing on the full dataset leaks test information into training. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** trap.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define data normalization. List any three normalization techniques used in machine learning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Data normalization is a preprocessing technique in machine learning used to rescale the values of numeric features to a common range or distribution, ensuring that no single feature dominates the model due to its larger numerical magnitude. It is a bijective transformation that preserves the relative ordering and information content of the original data.
>
> **Three Techniques (1.5 marks — 0.5 each):**
> 1. **Min-Max Normalization:** $X' = (X - X_{min}) / (X_{max} - X_{min})$ — rescales to $[0, 1]$.
> 2. **Z-Score Standardization:** $Z = (X - \mu) / \sigma$ — centers to mean 0 and std 1.
> 3. **Robust Scaling:** $X' = (X - Q_2) / (Q_3 - Q_1)$ — uses median and IQR, robust to outliers.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between Min-Max Normalization and Z-Score Normalization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Min-Max Normalization | Z-Score Standardization |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $X' = (X - X_{min}) / (X_{max} - X_{min})$ | $Z = (X - \mu) / \sigma$ |
| **Output Range** | Bounded $[0, 1]$ | Unbounded, typically $[-3, +3]$ |
| **Outlier Sensitivity** | High — outliers compress the range | High — outliers inflate $\sigma$ |
| **Distribution Shape** | Preserved | Preserved |
| **Mean after transformation** | $\approx 0.5$ (for symmetric data) | Exactly $0$ |
| **Std after transformation** | $\approx 1/4$ (for uniform data) | Exactly $1$ |
| **Best suited for** | Neural Networks, image pixels | PCA, SVM, Logistic Regression |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the various data normalization techniques used in machine learning preprocessing. Discuss the formula and use-case of each technique.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The major normalization techniques are:

**1. Min-Max Normalization:** It linearly transforms data to a fixed range, typically $[0, 1]$:

$$X' = \frac{X - X_{min}}{X_{max} - X_{min}}$$

It is widely used in image processing (pixel intensity scaling) and neural networks. It is sensitive to outliers, which can compress the bulk of data near one end.
