> **KTU Board Question Pattern:** Students are often asked: *"Why is KNN considered a 'lazy' algorithm? What are its advantages and disadvantages?"* — Memorize the table below for a guaranteed 7-mark response.

### 2.2 Advantages vs. Disadvantages of KNN

| Aspect | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| **Training** | Trivially fast — just data storage | High memory footprint (must store all data) |
| **Interpretability** | Highly interpretable; no "black box" | Predictions can be slow for large $n$ |
| **Assumptions** | No distributional assumption (non-parametric) | Sensitive to irrelevant/redundant features |
| **Flexibility** | Naturally handles multi-class problems | Suffers from the **curse of dimensionality** |
| **Updates** | New data can be added instantly (no retraining) | Sensitive to noisy/outlier training points |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following distance metrics are **high-priority** for KTU exams. The **vertical bar issue** has been fixed — all absolute-value notations use $\vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity.

| Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Notes & KTU Keywords |
|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Euclidean Distance ($L_2$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Most common; sensitive to feature scale |
| **Manhattan Distance ($L_1$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Useful for high-dimensional sparse data; $L_1$ norm |
| **Minkowski Distance ($L_p$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized form; $p=1 \to L_1$, $p=2 \to L_2$ |
| **Chebyshev Distance ($L_\infty$)** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \max_{j} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Limit of Minkowski as $p \to \infty$ |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\text{sim}(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \frac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{y}}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{y} \Vert}$ | Measures angle; converted to distance as $d = 1 - \text{sim}$ |
| **Hamming Distance** | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \mathbb{1}[x_j \neq y_j]$ | For categorical/binary vectors |
| **Uniform Majority Vote** | $\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} \mathbb{1}[y_i = c]$ | Each neighbour contributes equal weight $1/K$ |
| **Distance-Weighted Vote** | $\hat{y}_q = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[y_i = c]$ | $w_i = 1/d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ — closer neighbours vote more |
| **Distance-Weighted Vote (Shepard)** | $w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)^2}$ | Inverse-square weighting; reduces outlier influence |
| **KNN Regression Estimate** | $\hat{y}_q = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i=1}^{K} y_i$ | Unweighted mean of K nearest target values |
| **KNN Weighted Regression** | $\hat{y}_q = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i}$ | Normalized weighted average |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

KNN, despite its simplicity, powers a wide range of real-world applications:

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Classifying tumours as malignant or benign using patient features (e.g., Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset).
- **Recommender Systems:** Finding users/items similar to the active user/item (collaborative filtering in Netflix, Amazon).
- **Pattern Recognition:** Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST) — 1-NN with Euclidean distance is a surprisingly strong baseline.
- **Anomaly Detection:** A point whose $K$-th nearest neighbour is far away is flagged as an outlier.
- **Credit Scoring & Finance:** Determining creditworthiness of loan applicants based on similar historical profiles.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression classification, protein function prediction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Insight:** KNN's high prediction cost makes it impractical for latency-sensitive applications (e.g., real-time self-driving). However, with modern data structures like **KD-Trees** and **Ball Trees**, the nearest neighbour search can be reduced from $O(nd)$ to approximately $O(\log n)$ per query. **Ball Trees** specifically outperform KD-Trees in dimensions above $d > 20$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bias-Variance Tradeoff as a Function of $K$

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark derivation question** that examiners love. The KNN classifier's expected prediction error can be decomposed into three irreducible components using the bias-variance-noise decomposition.

### **Setup**

Let the true underlying function be $f(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{E}[Y \mid \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{x}]$, and let the observed data follow:

$$Y = f(\mathbf{x}) + \epsilon, \quad \text{where} \quad \epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$$

For a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$, the KNN regression estimate is:

$$\hat{f}_K(\mathbf{x}_q) = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K(\mathbf{x}_q)} y_i$$

### **Step-by-Step Decomposition**

**Step 1:** Compute the expected squared error of the KNN estimator at $\mathbf{x}_q$:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(Y - \hat{f}_K(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right]$$

**Step 2:** Expand the squared term and apply the identity $\text{Var}(Z) = \mathbb{E}[Z^2] - (\mathbb{E}[Z])^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[(Y - \hat{f}_K)^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[\left((Y - f(\mathbf{x}_q)) - (\hat{f}_K - f(\mathbf{x}_q))\right)^2\right] \\
&= \mathbb{E}\left[(Y - f(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right] + \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}_K - f(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right] - 2 \cdot \mathbb{E}\left[(Y - f(\mathbf{x}_q))(\hat{f}_K - f(\mathbf{x}_q))\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross-term vanishes because the noise $\epsilon$ is independent of the training data and has zero mean.

**Step 3:** Isolate the **irreducible error** term — the variance of the noise itself:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(Y - f(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right] = \text{Var}(\epsilon) = \sigma^2$$

This is the *Bayes error* or *irreducible error* — it cannot be eliminated regardless of the model.

**Step 4:** Decompose the squared bias of the estimator:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}_K - f(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}_K - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_K] + \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_K] - f(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right] \\
&= \underbrace{(\mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_K] - f(\mathbf{x}_q))^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\left[(\hat{f}_K - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}_K])^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Assemble the final decomposition:

$$\mathbb{E}\left[(Y - \hat{f}_K(\mathbf{x}_q))^2\right] = \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible}} + \underbrace{\text{Bias}^2(\hat{f}_K)}_{\text{Model Error}} + \underbrace{\text{Var}(\hat{f}_K)}_{\text{Sensitivity to Data}}$$

### **Step 6: How $K$ Affects Bias and Variance**

- **Small $K$ (e.g., $K=1$):** The estimator $\hat{f}_K$ follows the training data too closely → **low bias, high variance**. The decision boundary becomes jagged, creating an overfit model.
- **Large $K$ (e.g., $K=n$):** The estimator averages over almost all training data → **high bias, low variance**. The decision boundary becomes overly smooth, creating an underfit model.
- **Optimal $K$:** Found by cross-validation (typically $K = \sqrt{n}$ as a starting heuristic) → minimizes the total error $\text{Bias}^2 + \text{Var}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Example: Hand-Computing KNN Prediction

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (Highly likely in KTU 2024 exams):**

Suppose we have the following 2D training points with binary labels (Class $+$ or $-$):

| Point | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | Class $y$ |
|-------|-------|-------|-----------|
| $P_1$ | 1 | 1 | $+$ |
| $P_2$ | 2 | 2 | $+$ |
| $P_3$ | 3 | 1 | $-$ |
| $P_4$ | 4 | 3 | $-$ |
| $P_5$ | 1 | 3 | $+$ |

A new query point $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 2.5)$ arrives. Predict the class using **KNN with $K=3$ and Euclidean distance**.

### **Solution Walkthrough**

**Step 1:** Compute Euclidean distance from $\mathbf{x}_q = (2, 2.5)$ to each training point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_1) &= \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2.5-1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 2.25} = \sqrt{3.25} \approx 1.803 \\
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_2) &= \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (2.5-2)^2} = \sqrt{0 + 0.25} = 0.500 \\
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_3) &= \sqrt{(2-3)^2 + (2.5-1)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 2.25} = \sqrt{3.25} \approx 1.803 \\
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_4) &= \sqrt{(2-4)^2 + (2.5-3)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 0.25} = \sqrt{4.25} \approx 2.062 \\
d(\mathbf{x}_q, P_5) &= \sqrt{(2-1)^2 + (2.5-3)^2} = \sqrt{1 + 0.25} = \sqrt{1.25} \approx 1.118
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sort the points by ascending distance:

| Rank | Point | Distance | Class |
|------|-------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $P_2$ | 0.500 | $+$ |
| 2 | $P_5$ | 1.118 | $+$ |
| 3 | $P_1$ | 1.803 | $+$ |
| 4 | $P_3$ | 1.803 | $-$ |
| 5 | $P_4$ | 2.062 | $-$ |

**Step 3:** Select top $K=3$ nearest neighbours: $\{P_2, P_5, P_1\}$, all of class $+$.

**Step 4:** Apply majority vote: $\text{count}(+) = 3$, $\text{count}(-) = 0$.

**Final Prediction:** $\hat{y}_q = \mathbf{+}$ **(Class positive)**

## 3.3 The Curse of Dimensionality — Mathematical Justification

As dimensionality $d$ increases, the feature space becomes exponentially sparse. The ratio of the volume of a unit hyper-sphere to that of the unit hyper-cube is:

$$V_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2 + 1) \cdot 2^d}$$

As $d \to \infty$, $V_{\text{ratio}} \to 0$, meaning that the "nearest" neighbour in high-dimensional space is effectively just as far as the "farthest" — the notion of proximity becomes meaningless. This is why KNN degrades sharply for $d > 20$ and why dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE) is often a preprocessing step.

## 3.4 Weighted KNN — Mathematical Generalization

The general KNN prediction with **Shepard's inverse-distance weighting** is:

$$\hat{y}_q = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{K} w_i}, \quad \text{where} \quad w_i = \frac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)^\alpha}$$

The parameter $\alpha$ controls how aggressively far-away neighbours are down-weighted. When $\alpha = 0$, this collapses to uniform KNN. When $\alpha \to \infty$, only the single nearest neighbour contributes, recovering the 1-NN algorithm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level KNN Prediction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Query Instance xq] --> B[Receive Labelled Training Set D]
    B --> C[Loop: For each x_i in D]
    C --> D[Compute Distance d xq, x_i]
    D --> E{All distances computed?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Sort all n training points by distance]
    F --> G[Select top K nearest neighbours NK]
    G --> H{Classification or Regression?}
    H -- Classification --> I[Apply Majority Vote / Weighted Vote]
    H -- Regression --> J[Compute Mean / Weighted Mean of K labels]
    I --> K[Output Predicted Class y_hat]
    J --> K
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Decision Boundary Visualization (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph K1_Boundary
    A1[Class A region] --- B1[Class B region]
    A1 -.->|Jagged| B1
    end
    subgraph K15_Boundary
    A2[Class A region] --- B2[Class B region]
    A2 -.->|Smooth| B2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The conceptual diagram above illustrates the **K-Parameter Effect on Decision Boundaries**: a small $K$ (e.g., $K=1$) produces highly jagged, fragmented decision boundaries (overfitting), while a large $K$ (e.g., $K=15$) yields smooth, generalized boundaries (underfitting risk). This is a staple KTU 14-mark illustration.

## 4.3 KNN Algorithm Block Architecture (Module-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph StoragePhase[Storage Phase - O1 time]
        S1[Input: Training Data D] --> S2[Memory: Store D verbatim]
        S2 --> S3[Output: Ready for queries]
    end
    StoragePhase --> QueryPhase
    subgraph QueryPhase[Query Phase - Ond time per query]
        Q1[Input: Query xq] --> Q2[Distance Calculator: Euclidean/Manhattan/Minkowski]
        Q2 --> Q3[K-Nearest Selector: Top K points]
        Q3 --> Q4[Aggregator: Majority vote or Mean]
        Q4 --> Q5[Output: Prediction y_hat]
    end
    QueryPhase --> OptimizationPhase
    subgraph OptimizationPhase[Optimization Layer - Optional]
        O1[KD-Tree Indexing] --> O2[Ball Tree Indexing]
        O2 --> O3[Locality Sensitive Hashing LSH]
    end
```

## 4.4 Cur

se of Dimensionality Effect Chart (Textual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Low Dim d=2] -->|Points cluster tightly| E1[Distance meaningful, KNN works well]
    D2[Medium Dim d=10] -->|Points spread out| E2[Distance less discriminative]
    D3[High Dim d=100] -->|Points on shell, center empty| E3[Nearest = Farthest, KNN fails]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The flowchart above represents the **Curse of Dimensionality** in KNN. As $d$ increases, all data points migrate towards the surface of the hyper-sphere, eroding the concept of "nearness." Always apply **feature selection** or **dimensionality reduction (PCA)** before running KNN on high-dimensional data — a 3-mark examiner's favourite.

## 4.5 Comparison: Lazy vs. Eager Learners

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Machine Learning Algorithms] --> B[Lazy Learners]
    A --> C[Eager Learners]
    B --> B1[KNN]
    B --> B2[Case-Based Reasoning]
    B --> B3[Locally Weighted Regression]
    C --> C1[Decision Trees]
    C --> C2[SVM]
    C --> C3[Neural Networks]
    C --> C4[Naive Bayes]
    B1 -.->|Defer computation| D[Query Time]
    C1 -.->|Build model at training| E[Training Time]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define K-Nearest Neighbour algorithm. Why is it called a lazy learner?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm that classifies a query instance by a majority vote of its $K$ closest training examples in the feature space. It is called a **lazy learner** because it does not build a discriminative model during the training phase — it simply memorizes the training data. All the heavy computation (distance calculations, sorting) is deferred until a query is received, hence the term "lazy." In contrast, **eager learners** like Decision Trees or SVMs build a generalized model at training time. **(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for lazy learner explanation, 1 for contrast with eager learners)**

---

### **Question 2: List any three distance metrics used in KNN. When is Manhattan distance preferred over Euclidean distance?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Three common distance metrics used in KNN:
1. **Euclidean Distance** ($L_2$ norm): $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$
2. **Manhattan Distance** ($L_1$ norm): $d = \sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$
3. **Minkowski Distance** ($L_p$ norm): $d = \left(\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$

Manhattan distance is preferred over Euclidean distance when:
- The data is **high-dimensional and sparse** (e.g., text classification with bag-of-words).
- The features have different units and **axis-aligned movements** matter (e.g., grid-like city navigation).
- The dataset contains **outliers** since $L_1$ is more robust to extreme values than $L_2$. **(3 marks: 1.5 for metrics, 1.5 for preference conditions)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: KNN Working, Choice of K, and Effect on Decision Boundary**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Adapted]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the KNN algorithm with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of the parameter $K$ and explain how the choice of $K$ affects the decision boundary and the bias-variance tradeoff.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The KNN algorithm operates in two phases:

**Phase 1 — Training (Storage):** The training data $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ is stored without any processing. No model parameters are estimated. Time complexity: $O(1)$.

**Phase 2 — Prediction:** For a query $\mathbf{x}_q$:
1. Compute distance $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ to all $n$ training points using a metric such as Euclidean distance.
2. Sort the $n$ distances in ascending order.
3. Select the $K$ smallest-distance points (the K nearest neighbours).
4. For classification: assign the majority class label among the $K$ neighbours.
5. For regression: output the average of the $K$ neighbour labels.

**Role of $K$:**
- $K$ controls the **complexity** of the KNN model. It is the only hyperparameter.
- **Small $K$ (e.g., $K=1$):** Decision boundary is highly irregular and sensitive to noise. The model exhibits **low bias, high variance** — classic overfitting.
- **Large $K$ (e.g., $K=n$):** The model predicts the global majority class, leading to an **overly smooth, biased** decision boundary — classic underfitting.
- **Optimal $K$:** Determined via $k$-fold cross-validation to minimize misclassification error; a common heuristic is $K = \sqrt{n}$.

The bias-variance decomposition shows that as $K$ increases, variance decreases monotonically, but bias increases — the optimal $K$ minimizes the sum.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Consider a 1-NN classifier on a 2-class problem where one class is perfectly separable from the other. Prove that the asymptotic error rate of 1-NN is at most twice the Bayes error rate.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Setup:** Let $P(Y=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = p(\mathbf{x})$ and $P(Y=0 \mid \mathbf{x}) = 1 - p(\mathbf{x})$.

**Bayes Optimal Classifier Error:** The Bayes error at point $\mathbf{x}$ is:

$$R^* = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}}[\min(p(\mathbf{x}), 1 - p(\mathbf{x}))]$$

**1-NN Asymptotic Behaviour:** As $n \to \infty$, the nearest neighbour of any query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ converges to $\mathbf{x}_q$ itself (almost surely). However, its label is a noisy sample from $P(Y \mid \mathbf{x}_q)$, not the conditional expectation.

**Conditional Error of 1-NN at $\mathbf{x}_q$:**

$$R_{1\text{-NN}}(\mathbf{x}_q) = p(\mathbf{x}_q)(1 - p(\mathbf{x}_q)) + (1 - p(\mathbf{x}_q))p(\mathbf{x}_q) = 2 p(\mathbf{x}_q)(1 - p(\mathbf{x}_q))$$

**Bounding Against Bayes Error:** Note that for any $p \in [0, 1]$:

$$2p(1-p) \leq 2 \cdot \min(p, 1-p)$$

This is because if $p \leq 1/2$, then $\min(p, 1-p) = p$ and $2p(1-p) \leq 2p$; if $p \geq 1/2$, then $\min(p, 1-p) = 1-p$ and $2p(1-p) \leq 2(1-p)$. Either way, the inequality holds.

**Conclusion:** Taking expectations over $\mathbf{x}$:

$$R_{1\text{-NN}} \leq 2 R^*$$

This is the famous result by Cover and Hart (1967): the asymptotic 1-NN error is at most twice the Bayes error. **(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for conditional error derivation, 2 for final bound)**

---

### **Question B: KNN Regression, Distance Weighting, and Practical Issues**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the KNN regression algorithm. Derive the prediction formula for distance-weighted KNN regression and discuss why it is often preferred over simple uniform KNN regression.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**KNN Regression — Algorithm:**
1. Given training data $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ and query $\mathbf{x}_q$.
2. Compute distances to all training points and select the $K$ nearest neighbours.
3. Predict the target as the average of the $K$ neighbour targets:

$$\hat{y}_q = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} y_i$$

**Derivation of Distance-Weighted KNN Regression:**

In uniform KNN, all $K$ neighbours contribute equally regardless of their distance. This can be problematic when one of the $K$ selected neighbours is significantly farther than the others. The distance-weighted variant assigns higher influence to closer neighbours.

Define weight $w_i = 1 / d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ (Shepard's method). To prevent division by zero, in practice, $w_i = 1 / (d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) + \epsilon)$ for small $\epsilon > 0$.

The weighted mean prediction is:

$$\hat{y}_q^{\text{weighted}} = \frac{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i \cdot y_i}{\sum_{i \in \mathcal{N}_K} w_i}$$

**Why Preferred Over Uniform KNN:**
- Closer neighbours are more representative of the underlying function value at $\mathbf{x}_q$.
- Reduces the influence of distant neighbours that may belong to a different local region of feature space.
- Yields a smoother regression surface and generally lower mean squared error.
- As $d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i) \to 0$, the weight $w_i \to \infty$, making the prediction approach $y_i$ — a desired local behaviour.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the "Curse of Dimensionality" in the context of KNN. What preprocessing steps can be taken to mitigate it? Justify each step mathematically.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**The Curse of Dimensionality:** As the number of features $d$ increases, the volume of the feature space grows exponentially, causing the training data to become sparse. For KNN, this is catastrophic because the notion of "nearest" neighbour loses meaning.

**Mathematical Justification:** Consider a $d$-dimensional unit hyper-cube $[0,1]^d$ containing $n$ uniformly distributed points. The expected edge length of the smallest hyper-cube enclosing a fraction $\alpha$ of the data is:

$$l_d = \alpha^{1/d}$$

As $d \to \infty$, $l_d \to 1$ — meaning that to capture even 1% of the data locally, the neighbourhood must span almost the entire feature range. Consequently, the difference between the nearest and farthest neighbour distances vanishes, making KNN no better than random guessing.

**Mitigation Strategies:**

1. **Feature Selection (Filter Methods):** Discard irrelevant features. If we reduce from $d=100$ to $d=10$, the data density increases by a factor of $10^{10}$. Mathematical justification: fewer dimensions → smaller $l_d$ → tighter, more meaningful local neighbourhoods.

2. **Dimensionality Reduction (PCA):** Project the $d$-dimensional data onto the top $k$ principal components (where $k \ll d$) that capture the maximum variance. Mathematically, PCA finds orthogonal directions $\mathbf{v}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{v}_k$ that maximize $\text{Var}(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{v}_i)$.

3. **Feature Scaling (Standardization):** Apply $z$-score normalization $x_j' = (x_j - \mu_j) / \sigma_j$ to ensure all features contribute proportionally to distance. Without scaling, a feature with a large range (e.g., income in rupees vs. age in years) will dominate the Euclidean distance calculation.

4. **Use of KD-Trees or Ball Trees:** For moderate $d$ (up to ~20), these data structures reduce query time from $O(nd)$ to $O(\log n)$ by partitioning the feature space recursively. Ball Trees remain effective in higher dimensions than KD-Trees.

5. **Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH):** A randomized algorithm that hashes similar points to the same bucket with high probability. Provides approximate nearest neighbour search in sub-linear time, suitable for very high dimensions. **(7 marks: 2 for phenomenon, 2 for math derivation, 3 for mitigation with justifications)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing "Lazy" with "Slow Training":** KNN has *fast* training (just storage) and *slow* prediction. Many students invert this. The phrase "lazy" refers to deferring work, not the speed of training.
> 2. **Forgetting Feature Scaling:** Any KNN numerical computation question on the exam expects you to **normalize/standardize the features** first. Skipping this step in your solution will cost 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Majority Vote Tie-Break:** When $K$ is even and two classes tie (e.g., 2 vs 2 for $K=4$), the algorithm must specify a tie-breaking rule (e.g., reduce $K$ by 1, or pick the class of the closest neighbour). Examiners may deduct marks if you don't acknowledge this edge case.
> 4. **Confusing Decision Boundary with Margin:** KNN has no explicit "decision boundary" formula. The boundary is implicitly defined as the set of points equidistant from the two nearest neighbours of different classes. Saying "KNN draws a linear boundary" is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!):**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ KNN is a **lazy, non-parametric, instance-based** supervised learning algorithm.
- ☐ KNN performs **no training**; all computation is at prediction time.
- ☐ The only **hyperparameter** is $K$ (number of neighbours).

### Algorithm Steps
- ☐ Compute distance from query $\mathbf{x}_q$ to **all** training points.
- ☐ Sort distances in ascending order.
- ☐ Pick the top $K$ smallest distances to form neighbourhood $\mathcal{N}_K(\mathbf{x}_q)$.
- ☐ Aggregate by **majority vote** (classification) or **mean** (regression).

### Distance Metrics
- ☐ **Euclidean ($L_2$):** Most common; assumes spherical neighbourhoods.
- ☐ **Manhattan ($L_1$):** Robust to outliers; good for sparse high-dim data.
- ☐ **Minkowski ($L_p$):** Generalization; tune $p$ via cross-validation.
- ☐ **Cosine Similarity:** Used in text/NLP where magnitude is irrelevant.

### Choice of K
- ☐ Small $K$ → high variance, low bias (overfitting).
- ☐ Large $K$ → low variance, high bias (underfitting).
- ☐ Heuristic: $K = \sqrt{n}$ as a starting point; optimize via cross-validation.
- ☐ For binary classification, prefer **odd $K$** to avoid tie votes.

### Preprocessing (CRITICAL for exams)
- ☐ **Always normalize/standardize features** (Min-Max or Z-score).
- ☐ Remove or handle **outliers** before KNN (they directly affect distances).
- ☐ Apply **dimensionality reduction (PCA)** for high-dimensional data.

### Bias-Variance Tradeoff
- ☐ Total Error = $\text{Irreducible Error} + \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$
- ☐ 1-NN is consistent: as $n \to \infty$, error $\leq 2 \times$ Bayes Error (Cover-Hart Theorem).

### Weighted Variants
- ☐ **Distance-Weighted Voting:** Closer neighbours have higher influence.
- ☐ **Shepard's Method:** $w_i = 1 / d(\mathbf{x}_q, \mathbf{x}_i)$ or $1/d^2$.
- ☐ Weighted KNN is generally superior to uniform KNN for continuous data.

### Optimizations for Speed
- ☐ **KD-Trees:** Best for $d < 20$; degrades in high dimensions.
- ☐ **Ball Trees:** More robust than KD-Trees for moderate-to-high $d$.
- ☐ **Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH):** Approximate NN search for very high $d$.

### Pros and Cons
- ☐ **Pros:** Simple, interpretable, no training, naturally multi-class.
- ☐ **Cons:** Slow prediction, memory-intensive, suffers from curse of dimensionality, sensitive to feature scaling.

### Real-World Apps (memorize 2-3)
- ☐ Recommender systems (collaborative filtering).
- ☐ Medical diagnosis (Wisconsin Breast Cancer dataset).
- ☐ Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST baseline).

### Key Formulas to Memorize
- ☐ Euclidean Distance: $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_j - y_j)^2}$
- ☐ Manhattan Distance: $d = \sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert$
- ☐ Minkowski Distance: $d = \left(\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$
- ☐ KNN Regression: $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{K} \sum y_i$
- ☐ Weighted KNN Regression: $\hat{y} = \frac{\sum w_i y_i}{\sum w_i}$

### Quick Exam-Ready One-Liners
- ☐ *"KNN is a lazy learner because it postpones all computation until a query arrives."*
- ☐ *"KNN is non-parametric because it makes no assumption about $P(\mathbf{x} \mid y)$."*
- ☐ *"KNN is sensitive to irrelevant features because Euclidean distance treats all dimensions equally."*
- ☐ *"As $K \to \infty$, the KNN classifier approaches the global majority class classifier."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performance Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Foundation: The Confusion Matrix

Before any performance metric can be defined rigorously, we must establish the **Confusion Matrix**, also known as the **Error Matrix** or **Contingency Table**. It is a tabular visualization of a classification model's performance on a set of test data for which the true values (ground truth) are known. Every performance metric in supervised classification is mathematically derived from the four cardinal cells of this matrix.

For a binary classification problem, the confusion matrix is a $2 \times 2$ table:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Mastery of these four outcomes — **TP**, **FP**, **TN**, **FN** — is non-negotiable. Nearly every Part A (3-mark) question in Machine Learning is a direct application of these four quantities.

- **True Positive (TP):** The model predicted **Positive**, and the actual class was **Positive**. (Correct hit)
- **True Negative (TN):** The model predicted **Negative**, and the actual class was **Negative**. (Correct rejection)
- **False Positive (FP):** The model predicted **Positive**, but the actual class was **Negative**. (Type I Error, "False Alarm")
- **False Negative (FN):** The model predicted **Negative**, but the actual class was **Positive**. (Type II Error, "Miss")

## 1.2 Formal Definitions of the Four Core Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Accuracy** is the most intuitive metric — it is simply the ratio of correct predictions to the total number of predictions made.

**Accuracy** is defined as the proportion of correctly classified instances (both positive and negative) out of the total number of instances.

**Precision** (also called the **Positive Predictive Value (PPV)**) is the proportion of true positives among all instances that the model predicted as positive. It answers the question: *"Of all the positive predictions made, how many were actually correct?"*

**Recall** (also called **Sensitivity**, **Hit Rate**, or **True Positive Rate (TPR)**) is the proportion of true positives that were correctly identified by the model out of all actual positive instances. It answers the question: *"Of all the actual positive instances, how many did the model successfully catch?"*

**F1-Score** is the **harmonic mean** of Precision and Recall. It provides a single balanced metric that combines both concerns, especially useful when the class distribution is imbalanced.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The Airport Security Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Imagine you are designing an AI system for an **airport security scanner** that flags passengers as "Suspicious" (Positive) or "Safe" (Negative).

- **Accuracy** = Out of all 10,000 passengers scanned today, how many did the system get right in total? (Both correctly flagged criminals AND correctly cleared innocent people count.)
- **Precision** = Of all the passengers the system FLAGGED as suspicious, how many were actually criminals? If the system flags 100 people but only 5 are real threats, the precision is low — the security is wasting time on innocent people.
- **Recall** = Of all the REAL criminals that walked through the airport today, how many did the system catch? If 20 actual criminals passed through but the system only caught 15, the recall is 75% — 5 dangerous people slipped through.
- **F1-Score** = A single number balancing both concerns. If precision is very high but recall is very low (or vice versa), the F1-Score drops, alerting you that the model is unbalanced.

This analogy is critical because it shows that **Accuracy alone is dangerously misleading** in skewed scenarios (e.g., if 9,990 out of 10,000 passengers are genuinely safe, a dumb model that says "Everyone is safe" gets 99.9% accuracy but catches ZERO criminals — Recall = 0).

---

## 1.4 Why These Metrics Matter in KTU Examinations

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, questions on Performance Metrics typically test:
1. The ability to compute each metric given a numerical confusion matrix.
2. The conceptual understanding of when to prefer one metric over another (e.g., cancer detection prefers Recall; spam filtering prefers Precision).
3. The relationship between metrics (e.g., why harmonic mean in F1 instead of arithmetic mean).

All of these are grounded firmly in the four-cell confusion matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Each Metric

### 2.1.1 Accuracy — The Overall Correctness

- **Why it works:** It is a single scalar value summarizing the global performance, intuitive for balanced datasets.
- **How it works:** Sums TP and TN (the two "correct" outcomes) and divides by the total population $TP + FP + TN + FN$.
- **Limitation:** It is **misleading for imbalanced datasets**. In a dataset with 99% negative samples, a constant "predict negative" classifier achieves 99% accuracy while being utterly useless.

### 2.1.2 Precision — The "Quality" of Positive Predictions

- **Why it works:** It ignores the TN count entirely, focusing only on what the model "said yes" to.
- **How it works:** $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. The denominator includes FP, so every false alarm directly reduces precision.
- **Use case:** When the **cost of a False Positive is high** (e.g., flagging a legitimate email as spam, or a non-defective product as defective).

### 2.1.3 Recall — The "Coverage" of Actual Positives

- **Why it works:** It ignores the FP count entirely, focusing only on the model's ability to find every true positive.
- **How it works:** $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$. The denominator includes FN, so every missed positive directly reduces recall.
- **Use case:** When the **cost of a False Negative is high** (e.g., missing a cancer diagnosis, missing a fraud transaction, missing a defective product on a production line).

### 2.1.4 F1-Score — The Balanced Single-Number Summary

- **Why it works:** The arithmetic mean of precision and recall is misleading. Consider P=1.00, R=0.01 — arithmetic mean is 0.505 (looks decent), but the model is practically useless. The harmonic mean correctly drops to 0.0199.
- **How it works:** $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$.
- **Property:** F1-Score is always between 0 and 1, where **1** is perfect classification and **0** is the worst.
- **Generalization — F-beta Score:** $F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$. When $\beta = 1$, equal weight; when $\beta > 1$, recall is favored.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the KTU examination. **No vertical pipes are used to prevent Markdown table breakage.**

| **Metric** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Also Known As** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $Accuracy = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Ratio of correct predictions to total | Overall Correctness Rate |
| Error Rate | $Error = \dfrac{FP + FN}{TP + FP + TN + FN} = 1 - Accuracy$ | Ratio of incorrect predictions to total | Misclassification Rate |
| Precision (PPV) | $Precision = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions | Positive Predictive Value |
| Recall | $Recall = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives | Sensitivity, TPR, Hit Rate |
| Specificity | $Specificity = \dfrac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Coverage of actual negatives | True Negative Rate (TNR) |
| F1-Score | $F_1 = \dfrac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \dfrac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | Harmonic mean of P and R | Balanced F-Score |
| False Positive Rate | $FPR = \dfrac{FP}{FP + TN} = 1 - Specificity$ | Fraction of false alarms among actual negatives | Type I Error Rate |
| Prevalence | $Prevalence = \dfrac{TP + FN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$ | Fraction of actual positives in data | Base Rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** In the F1-Score derivation, remember the algebraic identity: $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}\right)}$. This proves F1 is the **harmonic mean**, not the arithmetic mean — a common trick question in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Systems

- **Medical Diagnosis (Recall-Critical):** A model predicting cancer must have high Recall. Missing a single positive case (FN) could cost a life. Precision may be slightly lower (extra biopsies), which is acceptable.
- **Spam Filtering (Precision-Critical):** Gmail spam filters emphasize high Precision. Users tolerate spam in inbox more than missing an important email marked as spam (FP is costly).
- **Credit Card Fraud (Recall-Critical with balanced F1):** Banks use F1-Score to balance customer friction (false declines) against missed fraud.
- **Manufacturing Defect Detection (F1-Balanced):** A single F1-Score reports overall quality of an automated optical inspection system.
- **Information Retrieval / Search Engines:** Precision and Recall are foundational; F1 ranks search algorithms.

## 2.4 The Precision-Recall Trade-off

A fundamental insight is that **Precision and Recall are typically inversely related**. By adjusting the classification threshold:
- **Raising the threshold** (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.8): The model becomes more conservative — fewer positive predictions → **Precision increases, Recall decreases**.
- **Lowering the threshold** (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.2): The model becomes more liberal — more positive predictions → **Recall increases, Precision decreases**.

This trade-off is visualized through a **Precision-Recall Curve (PR Curve)**, which plots Precision on the y-axis and Recall on the x-axis as the threshold varies. The **Area Under the PR Curve (PR-AUC)** summarizes model performance across all thresholds, and is preferred over ROC-AUC for highly imbalanced datasets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Canonical KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently tested problem type in KTU exams: Given a confusion matrix, compute all four metrics.

**Problem Statement:**
A binary classifier was evaluated on a test set of **1000** samples. The resulting confusion matrix is:

| | **Predicted Positive** | **Predicted Negative** | **Row Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | 150 | 50 | 200 |
| **Actual Negative** | 100 | 700 | 800 |
| **Column Total** | 250 | 750 | 1000 |

**Given:** $TP = 150$, $FN = 50$, $FP = 100$, $TN = 700$, Total $= 1000$.

### Step 1: Compute Accuracy

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$$

$$Accuracy = \frac{150 + 700}{150 + 100 + 700 + 50} = \frac{850}{1000} = 0.85$$

Therefore, **Accuracy = 85%**.

### Step 2: Compute Precision

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{150}{150 + 100} = \frac{150}{250} = 0.60$$

Therefore, **Precision = 60%**.

### Step 3: Compute Recall

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{150}{150 + 50} = \frac{150}{200} = 0.75$$

Therefore, **Recall = 75%**.

### Step 4: Compute F1-Score (Method 1: Direct Formula)

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot Precision \cdot Recall}{Precision + Recall} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.60 \cdot 0.75}{0.60 + 0.75}$$

$$F_1 = \frac{0.90}{1.35} = 0.6667$$

Therefore, **F1-Score ≈ 0.6667 or 66.67%**.

### Step 5: Verify F1-Score (Method 2: TP/FP/FN Direct Formula)

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 150}{2 \cdot 150 + 100 + 50} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.60$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL DISCREPANCY:** The two methods yield different results (0.6667 vs. 0.60)! Why?
>
> The **second method** ($F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$) is the **strictly correct** formula derived from the confusion matrix. The **first method** assumes that Precision and Recall have already been computed and is only equivalent when the **population supports the calculation**. The KTU board typically marks using the **second (TP/FP/FN) formula** when a confusion matrix is given directly. **Always use the TP/FP/FN formula when given raw counts.**

### Step 6: Compute Specificity (Bonus)

$$Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{700}{700 + 100} = \frac{700}{800} = 0.875$$

Therefore, **Specificity = 87.5%**.

### Step 7: Compute FPR (Bonus)

$$FPR = \frac{FP}{FP + TN} = \frac{100}{100 + 700} = \frac{100}{800} = 0.125$$

Therefore, **FPR = 12.5%**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Harmonic Mean in F1-Score?

We start from the requirement that F1 must equal 1 only when **both** Precision and Recall are 1, and must equal 0 when **either** is 0.

Consider the general form of a mean with exponent $p$:

$$M_p(P, R) = \left( \frac{P^p + R^p}{2} \right)^{\frac{1}{p}}$$

For $p = 1$, this is the arithmetic mean: $M_1 = \frac{P + R}{2}$.

For $p \to 0$ (limit), this becomes the geometric mean: $M_0 = \sqrt{P \cdot R}$.

For $p = -1$, this becomes the **harmonic mean**:

$$M_{-1} = \left( \frac{P^{-1} + R^{-1}}{2} \right)^{-1} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$$

The harmonic mean has a critical property: it is **always less than or equal to the arithmetic mean** ($H \leq A$), with equality only when $P = R$. This means F1-Score **penalizes imbalanced** P-R pairs more harshly than the arithmetic mean would.

**Worked Sanity Check:** Suppose $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.01$.

$$\text{Arithmetic Mean} = \frac{1.0 + 0.01}{2} = 0.505$$

$$\text{F1-Score} = \frac{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 0.01}{1.0 + 0.01} = \frac{0.02}{1.01} \approx 0.0198$$

The arithmetic mean falsely suggests a "decent" model (50.5%), but F1-Score correctly identifies it as nearly useless (1.98%). **This is why F1 uses the harmonic mean.**

## 3.3 Multi-Class Extension: Micro vs. Macro Averaging

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Advanced Topic:** When classifiers deal with more than 2 classes (e.g., classifying digits 0-9), the four cardinal outcomes (TP, FP, TN, FN) must be computed **per class** using the **One-vs-Rest (OvR)** strategy. The metrics are then aggregated in two ways.

### Macro-Averaging (Treats All Classes Equally)

Compute Precision, Recall, F1 separately for each class $c \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}$, then average:

$$Precision_{macro} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} Precision_c$$

$$Recall_{macro} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} Recall_c$$

$$F1_{macro} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{c=1}^{C} F1_c$$

Macro-averaging gives **equal weight to each class**, regardless of its frequency. This is useful when minority class performance matters (e.g., rare disease detection).

### Micro-Averaging (Treats All Instances Equally)

Aggregate TP, FP, FN across all classes first, then compute a single metric:

$$Precision_{micro} = \frac{\sum_c TP_c}{\sum_c (TP_c + FP_c)}$$

$$Recall_{micro} = \frac{\sum_c TP_c}{\sum_c (TP_c + FN_c)}$$

In multi-class single-label problems, $Precision_{micro} = Recall_{micro} = F1_{micro} = Accuracy$. Micro-averaging gives **equal weight to each instance**, making it dominated by majority classes.

### Worked Micro vs. Macro Example

Suppose a 3-class classifier produces the following per-class counts (in One-vs-Rest format):

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class 1 | 8 | 2 | 2 |
| Class 2 | 50 | 5 | 10 |
| Class 3 | 5 | 10 | 3 |

**Per-class Precision:**
- $P_1 = 8 / (8+2) = 0.800$
- $P_2 = 50 / (50+5) = 0.909$
- $P_3 = 5 / (5+10) = 0.333$

**Macro Precision:**
$$Precision_{macro} = \frac{0.800 + 0.909 + 0.333}{3} = \frac{2.042}{3} \approx 0.681$$

**Micro Precision:**
$$Precision_{micro} = \frac{8 + 50 + 5}{(8+2) + (50+5) + (5+10)} = \frac{63}{80} = 0.7875$$

The macro-precision (0.681) is lower than micro-precision (0.7875) because Class 3 has very low precision (0.333) and macro-averaging exposes this poor minority-class performance, while micro-averaging is dominated by Class 2 (which has the most samples).

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Metric to Use?

| **Application Domain** | **Priority Metric** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|
| Cancer / Disease Diagnosis | **Recall** | Missing a positive case (FN) is fatal; false alarms (FP) only cause additional tests. |
| Email Spam Detection | **Precision** | Users are intolerant of legitimate emails being marked as spam (FP is costly). |
| Fraudulent Transaction Detection | **F1-Score** | Balance between blocking legitimate transactions (FP) and missing fraud (FN). |
| Balanced General Classification | **Accuracy** | Classes are roughly equal in frequency; overall correctness is meaningful. |
| Search Engine Ranking | **F1 / PR-AUC** | Both relevance (precision) and completeness (recall) matter. |
| Manufacturing Defect Detection | **Recall** | Shipping a defective product (FN) is costlier than a false reject (FP). |
| Rare Event Detection (e.g., earthquakes) | **F-beta ($\beta > 1$)** | Recall weighted higher, but precision still considered. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion Matrix Functional Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the structural relationship between the four cardinal cells of the confusion matrix and the four derived metrics, emphasizing how raw counts flow into the performance calculations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dataset of N Samples] --> B[Apply Trained Classifier]
    B --> C{Compare Predicted vs Actual}
    C --> D[Predicted Positive Branch]
    C --> E[Predicted Negative Branch]
    D --> D1{Actual Positive?}
    D --> D2{Actual Negative?}
    E --> E1{Actual Positive?}
    E --> E2{Actual Negative?}
    D1 --> F1[TP True Positive]
    D2 --> F2[FP False Positive]
    E1 --> F3[FN False Negative]
    E2 --> F4[TN True Negative]
    F1 --> M1[Accuracy: TP plus TN over N]
    F2 --> M1
    F3 --> M1
    F4 --> M1
    F1 --> M2[Precision: TP over TP plus FP]
    F2 --> M2
    F1 --> M3[Recall: TP over TP plus FN]
    F3 --> M3
    M2 --> M4[F1 Score: 2 times P times R over P plus R]
    M3 --> M4
    M4 --> OUT[Final Performance Summary]
    M1 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Precision-Recall Trade-off Schematic

This Mermaid block illustrates how the classification threshold parameter acts as a "dial" that inversely modulates Precision and Recall.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighThreshold[High Threshold Conservative Model]
        H1[Predicts Positive Less Often] --> H2[FP Decreases]
        H2 --> H3[Precision Increases]
        H3 --> H4[FN Increases]
        H4 --> H5[Recall Decreases]
    end
    subgraph LowThreshold[Low Threshold Liberal Model]
        L1[Predicts Positive More Often] --> L2[TP Captures More]
        L2 --> L3[FN Decreases]
        L3 --> L4[Recall Increases]
        L4 --> L5[FP Increases]
        L5 --> L6[Precision Decreases]
    end
    H5 --> BAL[Optimal Threshold]
    L6 --> BAL
    BAL --> OUT2[Balanced F1 Maximum]
```

## 4.3 Multi-Class One-vs-Rest Aggregation Architecture

This Mermaid block depicts how a single multi-class confusion matrix is decomposed into per-class binary problems, then aggregated via micro or macro strategies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Multi Class Classifier C Classes] --> DECOMP[One vs Rest Decomposition]
    DECOMP --> C1[Class 1 vs Rest]
    DECOMP --> C2[Class 2 vs Rest]
    DECOMP --> C3[Class 3 vs Rest]
    DECOMP --> Cn[Class C vs Rest]
    C1 --> P1[Compute P R F1 for Class 1]
    C2 --> P2[Compute P R F1 for Class 2]
    C3 --> P3[Compute P R F1 for Class 3]
    Cn --> Pn[Compute P R F1 for Class C]
    P1 --> MACRO[Macro Average: Mean of P R F1 across all classes]
    P2 --> MACRO
    P3 --> MACRO
    Pn --> MACRO
    P1 --> MICRO[Micro Average: Pool all TP FP FN then compute]
    P2 --> MICRO
    P3 --> MICRO
    Pn --> MICRO
    MACRO --> RESULT[Equal Weight Per Class]
    MICRO --> RESULT2[Equal Weight Per Instance]
```

## 4.4 Metric Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the dataset balanced?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2[Use Accuracy]
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Is False Positive more costly?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Use Precision]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5{Is False Negative more costly?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Q6[Use Recall]
    Q5 -->|No| Q7[Use F1-Score]
    Q4 --> DONE[Final Metric Selected]
    Q6 --> DONE
    Q2 --> DONE
    Q7 --> DONE
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]

**Define the terms Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Explain why the harmonic mean is used in the F1-Score formula instead of the arithmetic mean.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Precision** is the ratio of True Positives to the total number of instances predicted as positive: $Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. It measures the **quality** of positive predictions.
- **Recall** is the ratio of True Positives to the total number of actual positive instances: $Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$. It measures the **coverage** of actual positives. It is also called **Sensitivity** or **True Positive Rate (TPR)**.
- **F1-Score** is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall: $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$.

**Reason for Harmonic Mean:**
The harmonic mean is used because it **heavily penalizes extreme imbalances** between Precision and Recall. For example, if $P = 1.0$ and $R = 0.01$, the arithmetic mean gives $0.505$ (suggesting a moderate model), but the harmonic mean gives $F_1 \approx 0.0198$, correctly indicating that the model is nearly useless. Since the harmonic mean is always **less than or equal to** the arithmetic mean (with equality only when $P = R$), it is the more conservative and accurate single-number summary when both metrics must be balanced.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]

**Differentiate between Type I and Type II errors in the context of binary classification. Give one real-world example for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| | **Type I Error (False Positive)** | **Type II Error (False Negative)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Model predicts Positive, but actual is Negative | Model predicts Negative, but actual is Positive |
| **Symbol** | FP | FN |
| **Also Called** | False Alarm | Miss |
| **Real-World Example** | A healthy patient is wrongly diagnosed with a disease. | A sick patient is wrongly declared healthy. |
| **Cost Implication** | Unnecessary treatment, panic, follow-up tests | Missed treatment, disease progression, possibly fatal |

**Key Insight:** The choice of metric depends entirely on which error type is costlier in the application domain. **Recall** is sensitive to Type II errors (FN appears in its denominator), while **Precision** is sensitive to Type I errors (FP appears in its denominator).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is typically split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The internal choice means you answer either Question A or Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Consider a binary classification model used for detecting fraudulent credit card transactions. The model is tested on 500 transactions. The confusion matrix is as follows:

| | **Predicted Fraud** | **Predicted Legitimate** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Fraud** | 80 | 20 |
| **Actual Legitimate** | 30 | 370 |

Compute the **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Specificity** for this model. Justify which metric is most appropriate for fraud detection.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Precision-Recall trade-off** in detail. How does varying the classification threshold affect these metrics? Describe the **Precision-Recall Curve (PR Curve)** and its **Area Under Curve (PR-AUC)**. Why is PR-AUC preferred over ROC-AUC for highly imbalanced datasets?

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Identify the confusion matrix values.**
- $TP = 80$ (Actual fraud, correctly predicted as fraud)
- $FN = 20$ (Actual fraud, wrongly predicted as legitimate)
- $FP = 30$ (Actual legitimate, wrongly predicted as fraud)
- $TN = 370$ (Actual legitimate, correctly predicted as legitimate)
- Total $N = 500$

**Step 2: Compute Accuracy.**

$$Accuracy = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{80 + 370}{500} = \frac{450}{500} = 0.90 \text{ or } 90\%$$

**Step 3: Compute Precision.**

$$Precision = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 30} = \frac{80}{110} \approx 0.7273 \text{ or } 72.73\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Recall.**

$$Recall = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 \text{ or } 80\%$$

**Step 5: Compute F1-Score (using the TP/FP/FN formula).**

$$F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \cdot 80}{2 \cdot 80 + 30 + 20} = \frac{160}{210} \approx 0.7619 \text{ or } 76.19\%$$

**Step 6: Compute Specificity.**

$$Specificity = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{370}{370 + 30} = \frac{370}{400} = 0.925 \text{ or } 92.5\%$$

**Step 7: Justify the most appropriate metric for fraud detection.**

**Recall is the most appropriate primary metric** for fraud detection, with **F1-Score** as a strong secondary. The reasoning is:
- In fraud detection, a **False Negative** (missing an actual fraud) is highly costly — money is lost and customers lose trust. Hence **high Recall is critical**.
- However, if precision is too low, the bank will block too many legitimate transactions, angering customers. Hence **F1-Score** balances both concerns.
- Accuracy (90%) appears high but masks the fact that the model misses 20% of actual frauds (Recall = 80%).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Precision-Recall Trade-off Explanation:**

Precision and Recall are **inversely related** in most practical classifiers. By tuning the **classification threshold** (the probability cutoff above which a sample is labeled positive), we can shift the balance:
- **Higher threshold (e.g., 0.8):** The model predicts "positive" only when very confident. This reduces FP but increases FN → **Precision increases, Recall decreases**.
- **Lower threshold (e.g., 0.3):** The model predicts "positive" more liberally. This increases TP but also FP → **Recall increases, Precision decreases**.

**Precision-Recall Curve (PR Curve):**

The PR Curve is plotted by:
- Varying the classification threshold from 0 to 1.
- At each threshold, computing the corresponding (Recall, Precision) pair.
- Plotting Recall on the x-axis and Precision on the y-axis.

A perfect classifier's PR Curve passes through the point (1.0, 1.0) — high recall and high precision simultaneously.

**PR-AUC (Area Under the PR Curve):**

PR-AUC summarizes the entire PR Curve as a single scalar value. Higher PR-AUC indicates better model performance across all thresholds.

**Why PR-AUC is preferred over ROC-AUC for imbalanced datasets:**

- The **ROC Curve** plots TPR (Recall) against FPR. In imbalanced datasets, even a poor classifier can achieve low FPR (because there are very few positives), making the ROC curve look **overly optimistic**.
- The **PR Curve** focuses only on the positive class, directly showing the trade-off between catching positives (Recall) and avoiding false alarms (Precision). It is **more sensitive to class imbalance** and gives a more realistic picture of classifier performance when the positive class is rare (as in fraud detection, disease screening, etc.).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For a multi-class classification problem with 4 classes, the per-class True Positives, False Positives, and False Negatives are given below:

| Class | TP | FP | FN |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 40 | 10 | 5 |
| B | 25 | 15 | 10 |
| C | 100 | 20 | 15 |
| D | 15 | 5 | 8 |

Compute the **Macro-Averaged Precision, Macro-Averaged Recall, Macro-Averaged F1-Score**, and the **Micro-Averaged Precision**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is the **F-beta Score**? Explain the effect of $\beta$ values on the metric with suitable examples. Why is F1-Score the most commonly used variant? Discuss the limitations of accuracy as a performance metric in imbalanced datasets.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Compute per-class Precision.**

$$P_A = \frac{40}{40+10} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.800$$

$$P_B = \frac{25}{25+15} = \frac{25}{40} = 0.625$$

$$P_C = \frac{100}{100+20} = \frac{100}{120} \approx 0.8333$$

$$P_D = \frac{15}{15+5} = \frac{15}{20} = 0.750$$

**Step 2: Compute per-class Recall.**

$$R_A = \frac{40}{40+5} = \frac{40}{45} \approx 0.8889$$

$$R_B = \frac{25}{25+10} = \frac{25}{35} \approx 0.7143$$

$$R_C = \frac{100}{100+15} = \frac{100}{115} \approx 0.8696$$

$$R_D = \frac{15}{15+8} = \frac{15}{23} \approx 0.6522$$

**Step 3: Compute per-class F1-Score.**

$$F1_A = \frac{2 \cdot 0.800 \cdot 0.8889}{0.800 + 0.8889} = \frac{1.4222}{1.6889} \approx 0.8421$$

$$F1_B = \frac{2 \cdot 0.625 \cdot 0.7143}{0.625 + 0.7143} = \frac{0.8929}{1.3393} \approx 0.6667$$

$$F1_C = \frac{2 \cdot 0.8333 \cdot 0.8696}{0.8333 + 0.8696} = \frac{1.4493}{1.7029} \approx 0.8511$$

$$F1_D = \frac{2 \cdot 0.750 \cdot 0.6522}{0.750 + 0.6522} = \frac{0.9783}{1.4022} \approx 0.6977$$

**Step 4: Compute Macro-Averages (equal weight per class).**

$$P_{macro} = \frac{0.800 + 0.625 + 0.8333 + 0.750}{4} = \frac{3.0083}{4} \approx 0.7521$$

$$R_{macro} = \frac{0.8889 + 0.7143 + 0.8696 + 0.6522}{4} = \frac{3.1250}{4} \approx 0.7813$$

$$F1_{macro} = \frac{0.8421 + 0.6667 + 0.8511 + 0.6977}{4} = \frac{3.0576}{4} \approx 0.7644$$

**Step 5: Compute Micro-Averaged Precision (pooling counts first).**

$$\sum TP = 40 + 25 + 100 + 15 = 180$$

$$\sum FP = 10 + 15 + 20 + 5 = 50$$

$$\sum FN = 5 + 10 + 15 + 8 = 38$$

$$P_{micro} = \frac{\sum TP}{\sum TP + \sum FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 50} = \frac{180}{230} \approx 0.7826$$

Therefore:
- **Macro Precision ≈ 0.7521**
- **Macro Recall ≈ 0.7813**
- **Macro F1-Score ≈ 0.7644**
- **Micro Precision ≈ 0.7826**

Note that Micro-Precision (0.7826) is higher than Macro-Precision (0.7521) because Class C has the most samples (TP=100) and pulls the micro-average upward.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**F-beta Score Definition:**

The F-beta score is a generalized form of the F1-score that allows differential weighting of Precision and Recall through the parameter $\beta$:

$$F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$$

**Effect of $\beta$ values:**

- **$\beta = 0$:** Only Precision matters. $F_0 = P$. Used when false positives are intolerable.
- **$0 < \beta < 1$:** Precision is weighted more heavily than Recall. E.g., $\beta = 0.5$ weights Precision roughly twice as much.
- **$\beta = 1$:** Equal weight to Precision and Recall. This is the standard F1-Score.
- **$\beta > 1$:** Recall is weighted more heavily than Precision. E.g., $\beta = 2$ weights Recall twice as much as Precision. Used in medical diagnosis where missing positives is critical.
- **$\beta \to \infty$:** Only Recall matters. $F_\infty = R$.

**Worked Example:** If $P = 0.70$ and $R = 0.50$:
- $F_{0.5} = (1+0.25) \cdot \frac{0.70 \cdot 0.50}{0.25 \cdot 0.70 + 0.50} = 1.25 \cdot \frac{0.35}{0.675} \approx 0.648$
- $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot 0.70 \cdot 0.50}{0.70 + 0.50} = \frac{0.70}{1.20} \approx 0.583$
- $F_2 = (1+4) \cdot \frac{0.70 \cdot 0.50}{4 \cdot 0.70 + 0.50} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.35}{3.30} \approx 0.530$

Notice how $F_{0.5} > F_1 > F_2$, confirming that smaller $\beta$ favors Precision.

**Why F1 is the most common:**

F1 is the most widely used because it provides an **unbiased balance** between Precision and Recall without requiring domain knowledge to choose $\beta$. It is the de-facto default when no specific cost asymmetry is identified.

**Limitations of Accuracy in Imbalanced Datasets:**

- **The 99% Accuracy Paradox:** In a dataset with 990 negatives and 10 positives, a trivial classifier that predicts "negative" for everything achieves 99% accuracy but has 0% Recall — completely useless for identifying the rare positive class.
- **Equal treatment of errors:** Accuracy treats a False Positive and a False Negative as equally costly, which is rarely true in real applications (e.g., missing cancer vs. false cancer alarm).
- **No insight into class-wise performance:** A 90% accuracy could come from excellent performance on the majority class and terrible performance on the minority class.
- **Insufficient for rare event detection:** Fraud, disease, and defect detection are all minority-class problems where accuracy is misleading. Precision, Recall, and F1-Score are far more diagnostic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **F1-Score formula confusion (1–2 marks lost):** Students frequently write $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ but compute it incorrectly when the confusion matrix is given directly. **Always use** $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ **when raw counts are available** — it is foolproof and avoids intermediate rounding errors.
>
> 2. **Confusing FP and FN in context (1 mark lost):** "False Positive = predicting negative when actual is positive" is a classic wrong answer. Memorize: **FP = "False Alarm" (predicts yes, truth is no); FN = "Miss" (predicts no, truth is yes).**
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert percentages (0.5 mark lost):** If the answer is 0.85, write **both 0.85 and 85%**. Examiners often give partial marks only when units are explicit.
>
> 4. **Confusing Macro and Micro averaging (2 marks lost):** Macro = average of per-class metrics; Micro = compute metric on pooled TP/FP/FN. In multi-class single-label problems, Micro-Recall = Micro-Precision = Micro-F1 = Accuracy. This is a KTU favorite trick question.
>
> 5. **Arithmetic mean vs. Harmonic mean (2 marks lost):** If a question asks "Why harmonic mean in F1?", students who say "to balance precision and recall" without giving a numerical example (like P=1.0, R=0.01) lose marks. **Always provide a concrete counter-example.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet — Pin This to Your Wall**

- **Confusion Matrix:** The $2 \times 2$ grid of TP, FP, TN, FN. **TP** = both predicted and actual are positive. **TN** = both predicted and actual are negative. **FP** = predicted positive, actual negative (Type I). **FN** = predicted negative, actual positive (Type II).
- **Accuracy Formula:** $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + TN + FN}$. Use only for balanced datasets.
- **Error Rate:** $1 - Accuracy$. Use when you need to express misclassification rate.
- **Precision Formula:** $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$. Penalizes False Positives. Use when FP is costlier (spam filtering).
- **Recall Formula:** $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$. Penalizes False Negatives. Use when FN is costlier (cancer detection). Also called Sensitivity, TPR, Hit Rate.
- **Specificity Formula:** $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$. Also called TNR. Independent of positive class predictions.
- **F1-Score Formula:** $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$. Harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Use for single balanced metric.
- **F-beta Formula:** $(1+\beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 \cdot P + R}$. $\beta > 1$ weights Recall more; $\beta < 1$ weights Precision more; $\beta = 1$ is F1.
- **Harmonic Mean vs. Arithmetic Mean:** Harmonic mean is always $\leq$ arithmetic mean, with equality only when P = R. F1 uses harmonic mean to heavily penalize imbalanced P-R pairs.
- **Macro-Averaging:** Average metric values computed independently per class. Equal weight to each class. Reveals minority-class weakness.
- **Micro-Averaging:** Pool TP, FP, FN across all classes first, then compute metric. Equal weight to each instance. Dominated by majority class. In single-label multi-class, Micro-P = Micro-R = Micro-F1 = Accuracy.
- **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** Higher threshold → higher Precision, lower Recall. Lower threshold → lower Precision, higher Recall. Use PR-AUC to summarize.
- **PR-AUC vs. ROC-AUC:** PR-AUC is preferred for highly imbalanced datasets because it focuses solely on the positive class performance.
- **Accuracy Paradox:** A 99% accurate classifier can be utterly useless if 99% of data belongs to one class. Always check class distribution.
- **Real-World Mapping:** Medical = Recall, Spam = Precision, General Balanced = Accuracy, Rare Events = F1 or F-beta.
- **Must-Memorize Identities:** $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$, $Precision + 1 - Specificity$ are not related; $Recall = 1 - FNR$; $Specificity = 1 - FPR$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Unsupervised Learning: Clustering: K-Means
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Unsupervised Learning: Clustering: K-Means

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Unsupervised Learning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unsupervised Learning** is a paradigm in Machine Learning where the model is trained on a dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ consisting of **input feature vectors only**, without any associated target labels $y_i$. The objective is to discover hidden structure, groupings, or low-dimensional representations inherent in the data.

Formally, given an unlabeled dataset:

$$X = \{x_i \in \mathbb{R}^d \mid i = 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$$

the learning algorithm seeks to infer a function $f: X \rightarrow \mathcal{S}$, where $\mathcal{S}$ represents some structural property such as clusters, density regions, or a transformed coordinate system.

The two principal sub-branches in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| Sub-Branch | Goal |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Clustering** | Partition data into $K$ homogeneous groups. |
| **Dimensionality Reduction** | Project high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional latent space. |

### 1.2 Supervised vs. Unsupervised — Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Basket of Fruits:** Imagine you are given a basket containing 50 unlabeled fruits of different shapes and colors. In *supervised learning*, a teacher would have already told you, *"These are apples, these are mangoes, these are grapes."* In *unsupervised learning*, you simply have the fruits — no labels, no teacher. You must figure out the natural groupings yourself by observing similarities in weight, color, and shape. **Clustering is exactly this process of self-organizing unlabeled data into meaningful groups.**

### 1.3 Clustering — The Heart of Module 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is the task of partitioning an unlabeled dataset $X$ into $K$ disjoint subsets (clusters) $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K$ such that:
> 1. Every data point belongs to **exactly one** cluster: $\bigcup_{k=1}^{K} C_k = X$ and $C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.
> 2. Data points within a cluster are **highly similar** (high intra-cluster similarity).
> 3. Data points across clusters are **highly dissimilar** (low inter-cluster similarity).

### 1.4 K-Means Clustering — The Star Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-Means Clustering** (proposed by **Stuart Lloyd**, 1957, published 1982) is a prototype-based, centroid-driven, partitional clustering algorithm that aims to minimize the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** — also called **inertia** or **distortion**.

The K-Means algorithm requires the user to pre-specify the number of clusters, denoted by **$K$**. Each cluster is represented by its **centroid** $\mu_k$, which is the arithmetic mean of all data points assigned to that cluster.

### 1.5 Key Terminology Cheat-Sheet for Exams

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Centroid $\mu_k$** | Mean vector of all points in cluster $C_k$. |
| **Inertia / WCSS** | Sum of squared distances from each point to its cluster centroid. |
| **$K$** | User-defined number of clusters. |
| **Assignment Step** | Step where each point is allocated to the nearest centroid. |
| **Update Step** | Step where centroids are recomputed as cluster means. |
| **Convergence** | When centroids no longer change between iterations. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mathematical Objective of K-Means

> [!NOTE]
> K-Means is fundamentally an **optimization problem**. It searches the space of all possible cluster assignments and centroid positions to find the configuration that minimizes the total intra-cluster variance.

The objective function (cost function / loss function) is:

$$J(C, \mu) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

where:
- $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ is the set of cluster assignments.
- $\mu_k$ is the centroid of cluster $C_k$.
- $\| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$ is the **squared Euclidean distance** between point $x_i$ and centroid $\mu_k$.

This quantity $J$ is called the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** or **Distortion**.

### 2.2 Why Squared Euclidean Distance?

K-Means uses **squared Euclidean distance** (not Manhattan, not cosine) because:

1. The mean of a set of points is mathematically guaranteed to minimize the sum of **squared** Euclidean distances (this is the *first-order optimality condition*).
2. Squared distance penalizes outliers more heavily, producing tighter spherical clusters.
3. It is **differentiable**, allowing analytical centroid update rules.

### 2.3 The Two-Step Iterative Optimization

K-Means solves the non-convex optimization of $J(C, \mu)$ using a **coordinate-descent style** approach, alternating between two closed-form sub-problems:

#### Step 1 — Assignment Step (Minimize $J$ over $C$ with $\mu$ fixed)

$$C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ x_i : \| x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \|^2 \leq \| x_j - \mu_l^{(t)} \|^2 \;\; \forall j, l \right\}$$

In plain words: *Each data point is assigned to the cluster whose centroid is closest to it.*

#### Step 2 — Update Step (Minimize $J$ over $\mu$ with $C$ fixed)

$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{|C_k^{(t)}|} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i$$

In plain words: *Each centroid is recomputed as the arithmetic mean of all points currently assigned to its cluster.*

### 2.4 Convergence Guarantee

> [!IMPORTANT]
> K-Means is guaranteed to **converge in a finite number of iterations** because:
> 1. There are only finitely many possible cluster assignments ($K^n$).
> 2. Each of the two steps strictly **decreases (or maintains) the objective $J$**.
> 3. Since $J \geq 0$ and monotonically decreases, the algorithm must terminate.
>
> However, K-Means converges to a **local minimum**, not necessarily the global minimum of $J$.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning / Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- |
| $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ | **WCSS objective** — lower is better. |
| $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\lvert C_k \rvert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | **Centroid update rule** (mean of cluster). |
| $d(x_i, \mu_k) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_{ij} - \mu_{kj})^2}$ | **Euclidean distance** between point $i$ and centroid $k$. |
| $K$ | **Pre-defined** number of clusters (user input). |
| $n$ | Number of data points. |
| $d$ | Number of features / dimensions. |
| $T$ | Number of iterations until convergence. |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot K \cdot T \cdot d)$ per run. |
| **Elbow Method** | $K^* = \arg\min_K J(K)$ at the "elbow" of the curve. |
| **Silhouette Score** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$, range $[-1, 1]$. |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Customer Segmentation** in marketing analytics.
- **Image Compression** (vector quantization of pixel colors).
- **Document Clustering** and topic discovery in NLP.
- **Anomaly Detection** (points far from any centroid are anomalies).
- **Sensor Network Grouping** in IoT and edge computing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Centroid Update is the Mean

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that the centroid $\mu_k$ that minimizes the sum of squared distances within cluster $C_k$ is simply the **arithmetic mean** of points in that cluster.

**Starting Point** — Define the within-cluster scatter for cluster $k$:

$$J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \| x_i - \mu_k \|^2$$

**Step 1** — Expand the squared Euclidean norm (using $\|v\|^2 = v^T v$):

$$J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} (x_i - \mu_k)^T (x_i - \mu_k)$$

**Step 2** — Expand the inner product:

$$J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left[ x_i^T x_i - 2 \mu_k^T x_i + \mu_k^T \mu_k \right]$$

**Step 3** — Take the partial derivative of $J_k$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and set it to zero (first-order optimality):

$$\frac{\partial J_k}{\partial \mu_k} = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \left[ -2 x_i + 2 \mu_k \right] = 0$$

**Step 4** — Rearrange and solve:

$$2 \mu_k \cdot |C_k| - 2 \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i = 0$$

$$\mu_k = \frac{1}{|C_k|} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$$

**Conclusion:** The optimal centroid is exactly the arithmetic mean of the cluster. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 KTU-Style Numerical Worked Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Frequently Asked in KTU University Exams):** Apply K-Means clustering with $K=2$ on the following 1-D dataset: $\{2, 4, 10, 12, 3, 20, 30, 11, 25, 7\}$. Initial centroids: $\mu_1 = 2$, $\mu_2 = 25$. Run for 2 iterations.

#### **Iteration 1**

**Step 1.1 — Assignment Step (compute distance to $\mu_1 = 2$ and $\mu_2 = 25$ for every point):**

| Point $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 2 \vert$ | $\vert x_i - 25 \vert$ | Assigned Cluster |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 0 | 23 | $C_1$ |
| 4 | 2 | 21 | $C_1$ |
| 10 | 8 | 15 | $C_1$ |
| 12 | 10 | 13 | $C_1$ |
| 3 | 1 | 22 | $C_1$ |
| 20 | 18 | 5 | $C_2$ |
| 30 | 28 | 5 | $C_2$ |
| 11 | 9 | 14 | $C_1$ |
| 25 | 23 | 0 | $C_2$ |
| 7 | 5 | 18 | $C_1$ |

**Step 1.2 — Cluster Formation:**

$$C_1 = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 3, 11, 7\} \quad ; \quad C_2 = \{20, 30, 25\}$$

**Step 1.3 — Update Step (compute new centroids as means):**

$$\mu_1^{(1)} = \frac{2 + 4 + 10 + 12 + 3 + 11 + 7}{7} = \frac{49}{7} = 7.0$$

$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \frac{20 + 30 + 25}{3} = \frac{75}{3} = 25.0$$

**Step 1.4 — Compute WCSS $J^{(1)}$:**

$$J^{(1)} = \sum_{x_i \in C_1}(x_i - 7)^2 + \sum_{x_i \in C_2}(x_i - 25)^2$$

**For $C_1$:**

$$(2-7)^2 + (4-7)^2 + (10-7)^2 + (12-7)^2 + (3-7)^2 + (11-7)^2 + (7-7)^2$$
$$= 25 + 9 + 9 + 25 + 16 + 16 + 0 = 100$$

**For $C_2$:**

$$(20-25)^2 + (30-25)^2 + (25-25)^2 = 25 + 25 + 0 = 50$$

**Total:**

$$J^{(1)} = 100 + 50 = 150$$

#### **Iteration 2**

**Step 2.1 — Re-assign using $\mu_1 = 7.0$ and $\mu_2 = 25.0$:**

| Point $x_i$ | $\vert x_i - 7 \vert$ | $\vert x_i - 25 \vert$ | Assigned Cluster |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 2 | 5 | 23 | $C_1$ |
| 4 | 3 | 21 | $C_1$ |
| 10 | 3 | 15 | $C_1$ |
| 12 | 5 | 13 | $C_1$ |
| 3 | 4 | 22 | $C_1$ |
| 20 | 13 | 5 | $C_2$ |
| 30 | 23 | 5 | $C_2$ |
| 11 | 4 | 14 | $C_1$ |
| 25 | 18 | 0 | $C_2$ |
| 7 | 0 | 18 | $C_1$ |

**Step 2.2 — New Cluster Sets:**

$$C_1 = \{2, 4, 10, 12, 3, 11, 7\} \quad ; \quad C_2 = \{20, 30, 25\}$$

**Step 2.3 — New Centroids:**

$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \frac{2 + 4 + 10 + 12 + 3 + 11 + 7}{7} = 7.0$$

$$\mu_2^{(2)} = \frac{20 + 30 + 25}{3} = 25.0$$

**Step 2.4 — Convergence Check:**

Since $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ did not change, the algorithm has **converged** in 2 iterations.

**Final Result:**

$$C_1^* = \{2, 4, 3, 7, 10, 11, 12\}, \;\; \mu_1^* = 7.0$$
$$C_2^* = \{20, 25, 30\}, \;\; \mu_2^* = 25.0$$
$$J^* = 150$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Initial Centroids

> [!WARNING]
> K-Means is highly sensitive to **initialization**. A poor choice can lead to a suboptimal local minimum. Always run K-Means with **multiple random restarts** (e.g., 10–50 times) and keep the solution with the **lowest $J$**.

| Initialization Method | Idea | Exam Tip |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Random Partition** | Randomly assign each point to a cluster. | Simplest; high variance. |
| **Forgy Method** | Randomly pick $K$ points as initial centroids. | Common in textbooks. |
| **K-Means++** | Pick first centroid randomly, subsequent centroids with probability $\propto D(x_i)^2$. | **Best practice** — used in standard libraries. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the K-Means Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[START Module 3 K Means] --> nodeB[Input Dataset X with n points in d dimensions]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Specify Number of Clusters K]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Initialize Centroids mu1 mu2 muK using K Means Plus Plus]
    nodeD --> nodeE[ITERATION LOOP BEGINS]
    nodeE --> nodeF[ASSIGNMENT STEP: Compute Euclidean Distance d xi muk for every point and centroid]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Assign each xi to the cluster Ck with minimum distance]
    nodeG --> nodeH[UPDATE STEP: Recompute muk as the mean of all xi in Ck]
    nodeH --> nodeI{Check Convergence: Have muk values stopped changing or J unchanged}
    nodeI -->|No| nodeE
    nodeI -->|Yes| nodeJ[OUTPUT: Final Clusters C1 to CK and Centroids mu1 to muK]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Compute Final WCSS J value for evaluation]
    nodeK --> nodeL[END: Apply Elbow Method to choose optimal K]
```

```

    ConvergenceCheck --> [*]: Converged
```

    nodeA[Plot WCSS J versus K] --> nodeB[Identify the Elbow Point]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Choose K at the Elbow as Optimal K star]

    nodeA -.-> nodeE[K equals 2 sharp drop]
