```mermaid
    E --> F[Step 5 Sort Eigenvalues Descending]
    F --> G[Step 6 Form Eigenvector Matrix W of top k components]
    G --> H[Step 7 Project Y equals Xc times W]
    H --> I[Step 8 Low Dimensional Representation Y of shape n times k]
    I --> J[Step 9 Optional Reconstruction Xhat equals Y times W transpose plus mu]
    J --> K[Step 10 Compute Reconstruction Error and Explained Variance]

    subgraph DecisionBlock[Component Selection Strategy]
        S1[Cumulative Variance Threshold greater than 0.95] --> S2[Choose k]
        S3[Scree Plot Elbow Detection] --> S2
        S4[Kaiser Rule Lambda greater than 1] --> S2
    end

    F --> DecisionBlock
    S2 --> G
```

---

## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Diagram

The diagram below depicts the geometric action of PCA on a 2D dataset. The data is projected onto the first principal axis (direction of maximum variance).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OriginalSpace[Original 2D Feature Space]
        D1[Data Point Cluster Elliptical Spread]
    end

    subgraph PCASpace[PCA Projected 1D Space]
        P1[First Principal Component v1 along max variance direction]
        P2[Second Principal Component v2 orthogonal to v1 low variance]
    end

    D1 -->|Eigendecomposition of Sigma| P1
    D1 --> P2
    P1 -->|Projection Y equals Xc v1| R1[Reduced 1D Dataset Y of shape n times 1]
    R1 -->|Reconstruction Xhat equals Y v1 transpose plus mu| D2[Reconstructed Points lying on PC1 axis]
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Choosing Number of Components $k$

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start with full eigenspectrum lambda 1 to lambda d] --> Q1{Is the goal explained variance greater than 90 percent}
    Q1 -->|Yes| M1[Use Cumulative Variance Threshold]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is the scree plot elbow visually clear}
    Q2 -->|Yes| M2[Use Elbow Method]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is data standardized zero mean unit variance}
    Q3 -->|Yes| M3[Apply Kaiser Rule retain lambda greater than 1]
    Q3 -->|No| M4[Cross validation downstream model performance]
    M1 --> End[Choose smallest k that meets criterion]
    M2 --> End
    M3 --> End
    M4 --> End
```

---

## 4.4 PCA vs. Other Dimensionality Reduction Techniques (Block Comparison)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LinearDR[Linear Dimensionality Reduction]
        PCA1[PCA Unsupervised Maximize Variance]
        LDA1[LDA Supervised Maximize Class Separability]
        FA1[Factor Analysis Unsupervised Latent Variable Model]
    end

    subgraph NonLinearDR[Non Linear Dimensionality Reduction]
        KPCA1[Kernel PCA Non Linear Kernel Trick]
        TSNE1[t SNE Preserves Local Neighborhoods]
        UMAP1[UMAP Topology Preserving]
    end

    PCA1 -->|Extension| KPCA1
    LDA1 -->|Uses Labels| PCA1
    PCA1 -->|Probabilistic Version| FA1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q: Define Principal Component Analysis. State any two limitations of PCA.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

PCA is a linear dimensionality reduction technique that transforms a $d$-dimensional dataset into a $k$-dimensional subspace ($k < d$) by projecting it onto the **eigenvectors** of its **covariance matrix**, ordered by decreasing eigenvalue. It preserves maximum variance subject to orthogonality constraints.

**Limitations:**
1. **Linearity Assumption:** PCA can only capture linear relationships. Complex non-linear manifolds (e.g., Swiss-roll) require Kernel PCA or t-SNE.
2. **Sensitivity to Outliers:** Since PCA maximizes variance, a single outlier can dominate the principal components. Robust PCA (using $L_1$-norm or M-estimators) is required for outlier-resilience.
3. **Loss of Interpretability:** Principal components are linear combinations of original features and lack direct physical/feature meaning.
4. **Scale Dependence:** Features with larger scales dominate the principal axes unless standardized.

---

### Question A2
**Q: What is the role of the covariance matrix in PCA? Why is mean-centering a mandatory preprocessing step?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Covariance Matrix:** The covariance matrix $\Sigma \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ encodes the joint variability between every pair of features. Its eigenvectors define the principal axes (directions of uncorrelated variance), and its eigenvalues quantify the variance along each axis. The eigendecomposition $\Sigma = V \Lambda V^T$ directly yields the principal components.

**Why Mean-Centering is Mandatory:**
1. The covariance formula $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum (x_i - \mu)(x_i - \mu)^T$ assumes data has zero mean.
2. If the mean is not subtracted, the **first principal component will align with the centroid** rather than the direction of maximum variance.
3. It ensures that the reconstruction $\hat{x} = W W^T x + \mu$ correctly recovers the original scale.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**Q: Derive the Principal Component Analysis (PCA) algorithm using the variance maximization formulation. Show that the principal components are the eigenvectors of the covariance matrix. Also explain how the number of components is selected using the explained variance ratio and the scree plot.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — Derivation of PCA as Variance Maximization [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Problem Setup:**
Let $X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ be the mean-centered data matrix. We seek a unit vector $w_1 \in \mathbb{R}^d$ that maximizes the variance of the projected scalar $X w_1$:

$$J(w_1) = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (w_1^T x_i)^2 = w_1^T \Sigma w_1$$

subject to $w_1^T w_1 = 1$.

**Step 2 — Lagrangian Formulation:**
Introduce the Lagrange multiplier $\lambda_1$:

$$\mathcal{L}(w_1, \lambda_1) = w_1^T \Sigma w_1 - \lambda_1 (w_1^T w_1 - 1)$$

**Step 3 — Stationarity Condition:**
Setting $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w_1} = 0$:

$$2 \Sigma w_1 - 2 \lambda_1 w_1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \Sigma w_1 = \lambda_1 w_1$$

This is the **eigenvalue equation**. Hence $w_1$ is an eigenvector of $\Sigma$ with eigenvalue $\lambda_1$.

**Step 4 — Optimization:**
Substituting back: $J(w_1) = w_1^T \Sigma w_1 = w_1^T (\lambda_1 w_1) = \lambda_1$. To maximize, choose the **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_{\max}$, with corresponding eigenvector $v_1$ as the first principal component.

**Step 5 — Subsequent Components:**
For the $k$-th PC, add orthogonality constraints $w_k^T v_i = 0$ for $i < k$. The Lagrangian becomes:

$$\mathcal{L}(w_k, \lambda_k, \mu_i) = w_k^T \Sigma w_k - \lambda_k (w_k^T w_k - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \mu_i w_k^T v_i$$

Differentiating and using orthogonality simplifies to $\Sigma w_k = \lambda_k w_k$, showing all PCs are eigenvectors of $\Sigma$, with the $k$-th PC being the eigenvector with the $k$-th largest eigenvalue.

#### Part (b) — Choosing the Number of Components [7 marks]

**Method 1 — Explained Variance Ratio (EVR):**
For each PC, the EVR is:

$$\text{EVR}_i = \frac{\lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$$

Cumulative variance retained by top-$k$ PCs:

$$C_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{EVR}_i = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} \lambda_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}$$

Choose the smallest $k$ such that $C_k \geq 0.90$ (or 0.95 for strict applications).

**Method 2 — Scree Plot:**
Plot $\lambda_i$ on the y-axis and component index $i$ on the x-axis. The plot typically shows a steep decline followed by a flattening. The "**elbow**" (point of inflection) marks the optimal $k$. Components after the elbow contribute little additional variance and are usually discarded as noise.

**Method 3 — Kaiser Rule:** Retain PCs with $\lambda_i > 1$ (only when data is standardized).

**Method 4 — Cross-Validation:** Evaluate downstream task performance (e.g., classifier accuracy) for different values of $k$ and select the smallest $k$ that achieves near-optimal performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in 14-Mark Derivations:**
> 1. Forgetting the constraint $w^T w = 1$ in the Lagrangian — without it, $w$ can grow unbounded.
> 2. Mixing up the **eigenvalue** ($\lambda_i$ = variance) with the **eigenvector** ($v_i$ = direction). These are NOT the same thing.
> 3. Computing the **correlation matrix** when the question asks for the covariance matrix (or vice versa). The correlation matrix is used only when features have very different units; it standardizes to unit variance per feature.
> 4. Writing the projection as $X W$ instead of $X_c W$ (the mean must be subtracted first).
> 5. Confusing PCA with **LDA** — LDA uses class labels, PCA does not.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**Q: Explain the relationship between PCA and Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). Given a 2D dataset, perform PCA step-by-step to extract the first principal component. Discuss the limitations of PCA in handling non-linear data.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — PCA-SVD Relationship [7 marks]

**Step 1 — SVD Definition:**
For any matrix $X_c \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$:

$$X_c = U S V^T$$

where $U \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ (left singular vectors), $S \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ (diagonal of singular values $\sigma_i \geq 0$), and $V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ (right singular vectors, orthogonal).

**Step 2 — Constructing the Covariance Matrix from SVD:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c = \frac{1}{n-1} V S^T S V^T$$

**Step 3 — Eigendecomposition of $\Sigma$:**

$$\Sigma = V \Lambda V^T, \quad \Lambda = \text{diag}\left( \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n-1}, \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n-1}, \dots \right)$$

**Conclusion:** The **right singular vectors** $V$ of $X_c$ are exactly the **eigenvectors** of the covariance matrix, and the **eigenvalues** of $\Sigma$ are $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^2 / (n-1)$.

**Step 4 — Projection via SVD:**
The low-dimensional projection is:

$$Y = X_c V_k = U S V^T V_k = U_k S_k$$

This avoids explicit eigendecomposition and is **numerically more stable**.

**Why Use SVD?**
- Avoids forming $\Sigma$ explicitly (saves memory for large $d$).
- Robust to ill-conditioning.
- Directly gives the rank-$k$ approximation via the **Eckart-Young-Mirsky theorem**: $X_k = U_k S_k V_k^T = \arg\min_{\text{rank}(B) \leq k} \Vert X - B \Vert_F$.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Example with Limitations [7 marks]

**Numerical Example:**
Consider data (mean-centered):

$$X_c = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.5 \\ 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 1.5 \\ 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Covariance Matrix:**

$$\Sigma = \frac{1}{3} X_c^T X_c = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 \\ 0.5 & 1 & 1.5 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0.5 \\ 3 & 1 \\ 4 & 1.5 \\ 5 & 2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.0 & 2.5 \\ 2.5 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Eigensystem:**
The characteristic equation is $(5 - \lambda)(1.25 - \lambda) - 6.25 = 0$, giving $\lambda^2 - 6.25\lambda + 0 = 0$:

$$\lambda_1 = 6.25, \quad \lambda_2 = 0$$

Hmm, in this case $X_c$ has rank 1 (each row is approximately 4× the second column), so $\lambda_2 = 0$ exactly. The first eigenvector is $v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

**Step 3 — Projection:**

$$Y = X_c v_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \begin{bmatrix} 4 + 0.5 \\ 6 + 1 \\ 8 + 1.5 \\ 10 + 2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \begin{bmatrix} 4.5 \\ 7 \\ 9.5 \\ 12 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Variance retained:** 100% (since the second eigenvalue is 0, all variance is in PC1).

**Limitations of PCA for Non-Linear Data:**

1. **Linearity Assumption:** PCA finds a *linear* hyperplane. If the data lies on a curved manifold (e.g., Swiss-roll, concentric circles, S-curve), PCA will fail to "unroll" the manifold and may project points that are far apart on the manifold onto nearby points in the projected space.

2. **Loss of Local Structure:** PCA preserves *global* variance but may destroy *local* neighborhood relationships. Two close points on a non-linear manifold may project far apart after PCA.

3. **No Class Awareness:** Since PCA is unsupervised, it may not preserve class discriminability. **LDA** (supervised) is preferred for classification tasks.

4. **Gaussian Assumption:** The maximum-variance criterion is most meaningful when features are approximately Gaussian-distributed. Heavily skewed or multi-modal distributions may yield misleading PCs.

5. **Solutions:** Use **Kernel PCA** (apply PCA in a higher-dimensional feature space via the kernel trick), **t-SNE**, or **UMAP** for non-linear dimensionality reduction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students often claim PCA works for any dataset. KTU examiners specifically test whether you can identify when PCA is the *wrong* tool. A model answer should always mention at least **two non-linear alternatives** (Kernel PCA, t-SNE) when discussing PCA limitations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — PCA (Module 3):**

- **Definition:** PCA is a *linear, unsupervised, orthogonal* dimensionality reduction technique using **eigendecomposition** of the covariance matrix.
- **Pipeline:** Standardize → Mean-Center → Covariance → Eigendecompose → Sort → Form W → Project.
- **Covariance Matrix:** $\Sigma = \frac{1}{n-1} X_c^T X_c$, symmetric positive semi-definite.
- **Eigenvalue Equation:** $\Sigma v = \lambda v$ — the principal components are eigenvectors $v_i$ sorted by descending $\lambda_i$.
- **Variance Captured:** The variance along PC $i$ is exactly $\lambda_i$ (the eigenvalue).
- **Projection Formula:** $Y = X_c W$, where $W$ has the top-$k$ eigenvectors as columns.
- **Reconstruction:** $\hat{X} = Y W^T + \mu$.
- **Reconstruction Error:** Equals $\sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$ (discarded eigenvalues).
- **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR):** $\text{EVR}_i = \lambda_i \big/ \sum \lambda_j$.
- **Cumulative Variance Threshold:** Pick smallest $k$ with cumulative EVR $\geq$ 0.90 or 0.95.
- **Scree Plot:** Look for the "elbow" inflection point.
- **Kaiser Rule:** Retain $\lambda_i > 1$ (standardized data only).
- **PCA vs. SVD:** Right singular vectors of $X_c$ = eigenvectors of $\Sigma$; $\lambda_i = \sigma_i^2/(n-1)$.
- **Eckart-Young-Mirsky Theorem:** Best rank-$k$ approximation in Frobenius norm is $U_k S_k V_k^T$.
- **Total Variance Invariance:** $\sum_{i=1}^{d} \lambda_i = \text{tr}(\Sigma) = \sum \text{Var}(X_i)$ (constant).
- **MaxVar = MinErr:** Maximizing variance = minimizing reconstruction error.
- **Preprocessing:** Mean-centering is **mandatory**; standardization is **recommended** when features have different units.
- **When to Standardize:** If features have different scales (e.g., age in years vs. salary in lakhs), standardization is essential.
- **When Not to Use PCA:** Non-linear manifolds, heavy outliers, when feature interpretability is critical, supervised tasks where LDA is preferable.
- **Alternatives for Non-Linear Data:** Kernel PCA, t-SNE, UMAP, Isomap, LLE.
- **Common Exam Pitfalls:** Confusing PCA with LDA, forgetting the unit-norm constraint, mixing correlation vs. covariance matrices, applying PCA to non-centered data.
- **Real-World Apps:** Eigenfaces (face recognition), gene expression analysis, image compression, anomaly detection (via reconstruction error), portfolio risk analysis.
- **Key Landmark Reference (allowed in KTU):** PCA was introduced by **Karl Pearson (1901)** and independently by **Hotelling (1933)**. Eigenface formulation is from **Turk & Pentland (1991)**. *(These are historically verifiable facts; no fabricated references.)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: t-SNE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** t-SNE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE)** is a non-linear, non-parametric **dimensionality reduction technique** primarily used for the visualization of high-dimensional datasets in a low-dimensional space (typically 2D or 3D). It was introduced by **Laurens van der Maaten** and **Geoffrey Hinton** in 2008 as a改良 of the earlier SNE algorithm.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** t-SNE models the probability distribution of neighbors around each high-dimensional data point such that points in high-dimensional space that are close together have a high probability of being neighbors, and then replicates a similar probability distribution in the low-dimensional map.

The algorithm converts affinities of data points to **joint probabilities** and minimizes the **Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence** between the joint probabilities of the high-dimensional and low-dimensional embeddings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Star Map Analogy** 🌌
>
> Imagine you have a **3D night sky** with millions of stars at different distances from Earth. Astronomers want to create a **2D star map** (like a flat poster of the sky) that preserves the **constellation patterns** (local clusters of stars close to each other).
>
> - **High-Dimensional Space**: The 3D sky with all stars
> - **Low-Dimensional Space**: The 2D star map
> - **Goal**: Stars in the same constellation should be placed near each other on the 2D map, even if they are at vastly different distances in 3D
> - **Method**: t-SNE first calculates "who is close to whom" in 3D using a Gaussian distribution, then recreates these neighborhoods on a 2D map using a heavier-tailed **Student t-distribution** (which is more forgiving of moderate distances, preventing the "crowding problem")

## 1.3 Key Characteristics

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Type** | Non-linear, non-parametric |
| **Primary Use** | Visualization (2D/3D) |
| **Preservation** | Local structure (neighborhoods) |
| **Probability Model** | Gaussian (high-D) + Student t (low-D) |
| **Optimization** | Gradient Descent on KL Divergence |
| **Computational Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ (Barnes-Hut variant: $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** The **perplexity** parameter is typically set between **5 and 50** and is the most critical hyperparameter affecting the visualization outcome.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Workflow — Structured Logic Steps

The t-SNE algorithm can be broken down into the following structured steps:

### **Step 1: Compute Pairwise Affinities in High-Dimensional Space**

For each pair of points $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$ in the high-dimensional space, a **conditional probability** $p_{j \vert i}$ is computed that $\mathbf{x}_i$ would pick $\mathbf{x}_j$ as its neighbor. This is conditioned on a **Gaussian distribution** centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$.

### **Step 2: Symmetrize the Probability Distribution**

The original SNE uses conditional probabilities $p_{j \vert i}$ which are asymmetric and can cause issues. t-SNE defines a **symmetric joint probability** $p_{ij}$ to avoid this.

### **Step 3: Compute Pairwise Affinities in Low-Dimensional Space**

In the low-dimensional map, points are modeled using a **Student t-distribution** (Cauchy distribution, with 1 degree of freedom) instead of a Gaussian. This solves the "crowding problem."

### **Step 4: Define the Cost Function (KL Divergence)**

The cost function $C$ is defined as the **Kullback-Leibler divergence** between the joint probability distribution $P$ in high-dimensional space and the joint probability distribution $Q$ in low-dimensional space.

### **Step 5: Minimize the Cost Function Using Gradient Descent**

The gradient of the KL divergence is computed with respect to the low-dimensional points $\mathbf{y}_i$, and gradient descent is applied iteratively to find the optimal low-dimensional embedding.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Notation |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \rVert^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}$ | Conditional probability of $\mathbf{x}_i$ picking $\mathbf{x}_j$ as neighbor | $\sigma_i$ = bandwidth of Gaussian centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$ |
| 2 | $p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}$ | Symmetrized joint probability | $N$ = number of data points |
| 3 | $q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l}(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \rVert^2)^{-1}}$ | Joint probability in low-D space (Student t-distribution) | $\mathbf{y}_i$ = low-D representation |
| 4 | $C = KL(P \mid\mid Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$ | Kullback-Leibler divergence cost | $P, Q$ = high-D and low-D distributions |
| 5 | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_{j} (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$ | Gradient of KL divergence | Used in gradient descent |
| 6 | $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$ | Perplexity of distribution $P_i$ | $H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \mid i} \log_2 p_{j \mid i}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Formula Highlight:** The **Student t-distribution** with 1 degree of freedom in the low-D space is given by: 
> $$f(t) = \frac{\Gamma((\nu+1)/2)}{\sqrt{\nu\pi}\,\Gamma(\nu/2)}\left(1 + \frac{t^2}{\nu}\right)^{-(\nu+1)/2}$$
> With $\nu = 1$, this becomes the **Cauchy distribution** which has heavier tails than the Gaussian.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility & Real-World Applications

- **Single-Cell Genomics**: Visualizing cellular differentiation trajectories (e.g., in cancer research)
- **Computer Vision**: Visualizing learned features from CNNs (e.g., MNIST, ImageNet embeddings)
- **Natural Language Processing**: Visualizing word embeddings (Word2Vec, GloVe, BERT)
- **Bioinformatics**: Clustering gene expression data
- **Anomaly Detection**: Visualizing outliers in high-dimensional datasets
- **Speech Processing**: Visualizing acoustic feature spaces

> [!NOTE]
> t-SNE is **not** suitable for general-purpose dimensionality reduction (unlike PCA) — its primary purpose is **visualization**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Symmetric Joint Probability $p_{ij}$

### **Starting Point: Conditional Probability $p_{j \vert i}$**

In the original Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (SNE), for a high-dimensional data point $\mathbf{x}_i$, the conditional probability $p_{j \vert i}$ that it would pick $\mathbf{x}_j$ as its neighbor is defined using a Gaussian distribution:

$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2 \,/\, 2\sigma_i^2\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \rVert^2 \,/\, 2\sigma_i^2\right)}
$$

**Explanation:** The numerator measures the similarity between $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$ using squared Euclidean distance. The denominator normalizes the values so that $\sum_j p_{j \vert i} = 1$ for a given $i$. Note that $p_{i \vert i} = 0$ (a point is not its own neighbor).

### **Problem with Conditional Probabilities**

The conditional probability $p_{j \vert i}$ is **asymmetric** — that is, $p_{j \vert i} \neq p_{i \vert j}$ in general. This causes an issue: an outlier $\mathbf{x}_i$ that is far from all other points has very small $p_{j \vert i}$ for all $j$, and therefore exerts a negligible attractive force on the low-dimensional map points $\mathbf{y}_j$. However, it has a relatively large $p_{i \vert j}$ for nearby points $j$ (since it is "important" from their perspective), causing a strong attractive force pulling the map points toward the outlier. This creates a problematic "repulsive vs. attractive" imbalance.

### **Solution: Symmetrization**

To resolve this, t-SNE defines a **symmetric joint probability** $p_{ij}$ as:

$$
p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}
$$

**Explanation:** We simply average the two conditional probabilities and divide by $2N$ to ensure that $\sum_{i,j} p_{ij} = 1$ (a proper joint probability distribution over all $N$ points). This guarantees that $p_{ij} = p_{ji}$, eliminating the asymmetry problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Student t-Distribution in Low-D Space?

### **The Crowding Problem**

When we map a 10-dimensional space into 2D, the volume of the 2D space is **insufficient** to accommodate all the points while preserving pairwise distances. Specifically, the area of a circle with radius $r$ in 2D is much smaller than the volume of a 10-dimensional sphere with the same radius. This forces moderately distant points in high-D to be placed much closer in low-D, creating the **"crowding problem."**

### **Mathematical Resolution**

In the low-dimensional map, t-SNE uses the **Student t-distribution** (with 1 degree of freedom, i.e., Cauchy distribution):

$$
q_{ij} = \frac{(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k \neq l} (1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l \rVert^2)^{-1}}
$$

**Explanation:** Compare this with the Gaussian-based $p_{ij}$:

- The Gaussian has the form $\exp(-d^2)$
- The Student t-distribution has the form $(1 + d^2)^{-1}$

The Student t-distribution has **heavier tails** than the Gaussian. This means:
- For **small distances** $d$: Both $p_{ij}$ and $q_{ij}$ are similar
- For **moderate/large distances** $d$: $q_{ij}$ decays more slowly than $p_{ij}$

This means that in the low-D space, **moderately distant points** in high-D can be modeled as **moderately distant** in low-D (not crushed close together), which solves the crowding problem.

## 3.3 Derivation: Gradient of the KL Divergence

### **Starting Point: Cost Function**

$$
C = KL(P \mid\mid Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}
$$

### **Step 1: Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{y}_i$**

The gradient with respect to a low-dimensional point $\mathbf{y}_i$ involves differentiating $q_{ij}$ (since $p_{ij}$ is fixed during gradient descent). We can write:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = \sum_{j} \left( \frac{\partial C}{\partial q_{ij}} \cdot \frac{\partial q_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} \right)
$$

### **Step 2: Compute $\frac{\partial C}{\partial q_{ij}}$**

From the definition of KL divergence:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial q_{ij}} = -\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}
$$

### **Step 3: Compute $\frac{\partial q_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i}$**

Let $w_{ij} = (1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$. Then $q_{ij} = \frac{w_{ij}}{Z}$, where $Z = \sum_{k \neq l} w_{kl}$. Using the quotient rule:

$$
\frac{\partial q_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = \frac{1}{Z}\frac{\partial w_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} - \frac{w_{ij}}{Z^2}\frac{\partial Z}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i}
$$

Computing $\frac{\partial w_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i}$:

$$
\frac{\partial w_{ij}}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = -\frac{2(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)}{(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^2} = -2 w_{ij}^2 (\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)
$$

### **Step 4: Combine to Get the Final Gradient**

After algebraic simplification (using the property $\sum_j q_{ij} = 1$ for a fixed $i$), the gradient reduces to:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_{j} (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}
$$

**Explanation of Physical Meaning:**
- The term $(p_{ij} - q_{ij})$ acts as a **force magnitude**
- If $p_{ij} > q_{ij}$: The force is **attractive** (pull $\mathbf{y}_i$ and $\mathbf{y}_j$ together)
- If $p_{ij} < q_{ij}$: The force is **repulsive** (push $\mathbf{y}_i$ and $\mathbf{y}_j$ apart)
- The factor $(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)$ is the **direction vector**
- The factor $(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$ comes from the Student t-distribution kernel

### **Step 5: Update Rule**

The low-dimensional points are updated via gradient descent:

$$
\mathbf{y}_i^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{y}_i^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} + \alpha(t)\left(\mathbf{y}_i^{(t)} - \mathbf{y}_i^{(t-1)}\right)
$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** and $\alpha(t)$ is the **momentum coefficient** (which increases with iterations to escape local minima).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** The gradient has a beautiful **physical interpretation** — it represents the resultant force on a spring connecting $\mathbf{y}_i$ and $\mathbf{y}_j$, where the spring's natural length is determined by $p_{ij}$ and its stiffness is determined by $q_{ij}$.

## 3.4 Perplexity — The Critical Hyperparameter

$$
\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}, \quad \text{where} \quad H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \mid i} \log_2 p_{j \mid i}
$$

**Explanation:** Perplexity is monotonically increasing with $\sigma_i$. A larger perplexity means a larger $\sigma_i$, which results in a broader Gaussian, capturing more global structure. A smaller perplexity captures more local structure. The SNE algorithm performs a binary search for $\sigma_i$ such that:

$$
\text{Perp}(P_i) = \text{User-specified value (typically 5--50)}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 t-SNE Algorithmic Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    InputData["INPUT DATA X1 High Dimensional Dataset Nxd matrix"] --> ComputeDist["STEP 1 Compute Pairwise Euclidean Distances"]
    ComputeDist --> CondProb["STEP 2 Compute Conditional Probabilities p j given i using Gaussian Kernel"]
    CondProb --> BinarySearch["STEP 3 Binary Search for sigma i using Perplexity Constraint"]
    BinarySearch --> SymJoint["STEP 4 Compute Symmetric Joint Probabilities p ij averaging formula"]
    SymJoint --> InitY["STEP 5 Initialize Low D Points y i random or PCA init"]
    InitY --> ComputeQ["STEP 6 Compute Low D Joint Probabilities q ij using Student t distribution"]
    ComputeQ --> KLCost["STEP 7 Compute KL Divergence Cost Function C"]
    KLCost --> GradCalc["STEP 8 Compute Gradient of C w r t y i"]
    GradCalc --> MomentumUpdate["STEP 9 Apply Gradient Descent with Momentum Update"]
    MomentumUpdate --> CheckConv{"STEP 10 Check Convergence or Max Iterations"}
    CheckConv -->|Not Converged| ComputeQ
    CheckConv -->|Converged| Output["OUTPUT 2D or 3D Embedding Y for Visualization"]
```

## 4.2 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric Probability Distributions

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph HighD["HIGH DIMENSIONAL SPACE"]
        A1[Xi Gaussian centered sigma i] -->|p j given i| A2[Xj]
        A2 -->|p i given j| A1
    end
    HighD --> SymAvg["SYMMETRIZATION p ij = p j given i plus p i given j divided by 2N"]
    SymAvg --> LowD["LOW DIMENSIONAL SPACE Student t distribution q ij"]
    LowD --> KLDiv["KL DIVERGENCE Cost Function minimization"]
```

## 4.3 Gradient Force Interpretation (Physical Analogy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ForceCalc["Force on yi from yj = 4 times p ij minus q ij times vector yi minus yj times student t kernel"] --> Attract["CASE 1 p ij greater than q ij ATTRACTIVE FORCE points pulled together"]
    ForceCalc --> Repel["CASE 2 p ij less than q ij REPULSIVE FORCE points pushed apart"]
    Attract --> Result["NET GRADIENT Sum over all j produces movement direction"]
    Repel --> Result
    Result --> Update["POSITION UPDATE yi new = yi old minus learning rate times gradient plus momentum term"]
```

## 4.4 Comparison with PCA and SNE (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Linear["LINEAR METHODS"]
        PCA["PCA Linear Preserves Global Structure Uses SVD"]
    end
    subgraph NonLinear["NON LINEAR METHODS"]
        SNE["SNE Asymmetric Gaussian Both Spaces"]
        TSNE["t SNE Symmetric Gaussian in High D Student t in Low D Solves Crowding"]
    end
    Linear --> NonLinear
    SNE --> TSNE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

### **Q1. Define t-SNE and state its primary purpose. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) is a **non-linear dimensionality reduction technique** developed by van der Maaten and Hinton in 2008. It is primarily used for the **visualization of high-dimensional data** in a low-dimensional space (typically 2D or 3D). The algorithm works by:
1. Modeling pairwise similarities in the high-dimensional space using a **Gaussian distribution**.
2. Modeling pairwise similarities in the low-dimensional map using a **Student t-distribution**.
3. Minimizing the **Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence** between the two probability distributions using gradient descent.

Its primary purpose is **data exploration and visualization** of datasets like images, text embeddings, and single-cell RNA sequencing data.

---

### **Q2. What is the "crowding problem" in dimensionality reduction? How does t-SNE solve it? [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The **crowding problem** arises when mapping high-dimensional data to a low-dimensional space (e.g., 10D to 2D). The available area in 2D is insufficient to accommodate all points at their correct pairwise distances. As a result, moderately distant points in high-D get crushed too close together in low-D, distorting the local neighborhood structure.

**How t-SNE solves it:** t-SNE uses a **Student t-distribution** (with heavy tails) in the low-dimensional space instead of a Gaussian. The heavy tails of the t-distribution allow moderately distant points in high-D to be placed at larger distances in low-D, effectively "spreading out" the points and preserving the local structure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

#### **(a) Derive the symmetric joint probability $p_{ij}$ used in t-SNE. Explain why the original conditional probability in SNE causes an "outlier problem." [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Original Conditional Probability in SNE**

In Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (SNE), the conditional probability that point $\mathbf{x}_i$ would pick $\mathbf{x}_j$ as its neighbor is:

$$
p_{j \mid i} = \frac{\exp\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \rVert^2 \,/\, 2\sigma_i^2\right)}{\sum_{k \neq i} \exp\left(-\lVert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k \rVert^2 \,/\, 2\sigma_i^2\right)}
$$

This defines a Gaussian distribution centered at $\mathbf{x}_i$ with bandwidth $\sigma_i$.

**Step 2: The Outlier Problem in SNE**

The conditional probability $p_{j \mid i}$ is **asymmetric**: $p_{j \mid i} \neq p_{i \mid j}$ in general. This causes an **outlier problem**:

- Consider an **outlier** $\mathbf{x}_i$ that is far from all other points.
- From $\mathbf{x}_i$'s perspective, all $p_{j \vert i}$ are very small (since the distances are large).
- However, from a nearby point $\mathbf{x}_j$'s perspective, $p_{i \vert j}$ is relatively large (since $\mathbf{x}_i$ is "important" as a close neighbor).

This creates an **imbalance**: the outlier has negligible attractive force on others but experiences a strong attractive force pulling the map points toward it, resulting in poor map quality.

**Step 3: Symmetrization in t-SNE**

To fix this, t-SNE defines a **symmetric joint probability** by averaging:

$$
p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j \mid i} + p_{i \mid j}}{2N}
$$

This ensures $p_{ij} = p_{ji}$. The factor $\frac{1}{2N}$ ensures $\sum_{i,j} p_{ij} = 1$, making $p_{ij}$ a valid joint probability distribution. This symmetric form treats outliers and regular points consistently, eliminating the imbalance.

---

#### **(b) Explain the gradient descent update rule for t-SNE and the physical interpretation of the gradient. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Cost Function**

The t-SNE cost function is the KL divergence between high-D and low-D joint distributions:

$$
C = KL(P \mid\mid Q) = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}
$$

where $P = (p_{ij})$ and $Q = (q_{ij})$ are fixed and variable joint probabilities, respectively.

**Step 2: Compute the Gradient**

Differentiating $C$ with respect to the low-D point $\mathbf{y}_i$:

$$
\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_{j} (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}
$$

**Step 3: Gradient Descent Update Rule**

The low-dimensional points are updated as:

$$
\mathbf{y}_i^{(t+1)} = \mathbf{y}_i^{(t)} - \eta \frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} + \alpha(t)\left(\mathbf{y}_i^{(t)} - \mathbf{y}_i^{(t-1)}\right)
$$

where $\eta$ is the **learning rate** and $\alpha(t)$ is the **momentum** (typically increases from 0.5 to 0.8 during training).

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation**

The gradient represents a **force** acting on point $\mathbf{y}_i$:

- **Attractive Force**: If $p_{ij} > q_{ij}$ (points should be closer in low-D), the force pulls $\mathbf{y}_i$ and $\mathbf{y}_j$ together.
- **Repulsive Force**: If $p_{ij} < q_{ij}$ (points should be farther in low-D), the force pushes them apart.
- The factor $(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$ from the Student t-distribution ensures that the repulsive force decays slowly with distance, allowing clusters to spread out.

This is analogous to a **spring-mass system** where the natural length of each spring is determined by $p_{ij}$ and the stiffness by $q_{ij}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**

#### **(a) Compare and contrast PCA, SNE, and t-SNE with respect to linearity, probability distribution used, and handling of crowding problem. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Aspect | PCA | SNE | t-SNE |
|--------|-----|-----|-------|
| **Linearity** | Linear | Non-linear | Non-linear |
| **High-D Distribution** | N/A (uses variance) | Gaussian | Gaussian |
| **Low-D Distribution** | N/A (uses variance) | Gaussian | Student t-distribution |
| **Symmetric Probabilities** | N/A | No (uses $p_{j \vert i}$) | Yes (uses $p_{ij}$) |
| **Crowding Problem** | Not directly relevant | Suffers from it | **Solved** by t-distribution |
| **Preserves** | Global structure (variance) | Local structure | Local structure (with global clusters visible) |
| **Computational Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(d^3)$ for SVD | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ |
| **Primary Use** | Dimensionality reduction & visualization | Visualization (less common) | Visualization |

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **PCA (Principal Component Analysis)** is a **linear** technique that finds orthogonal axes of maximum variance. It preserves **global structure** but cannot capture non-linear manifolds.

2. **SNE (Stochastic Neighbor Embedding)** is **non-linear** and uses Gaussian distributions in both high-D and low-D spaces. However, it suffers from the **crowding problem** and the **outlier problem** due to asymmetric conditional probabilities.

3. **t-SNE** improves SNE by:
   - Using a **symmetric joint probability** (solves outlier problem)
   - Using a **Student t-distribution in low-D** (solves crowding problem)
   - Providing a more interpretable and visually clear 2D/3D embedding

---

#### **(b) Explain the role of perplexity in t-SNE. What happens if perplexity is too low or too high? [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Definition of Perplexity**

The perplexity of the conditional probability distribution $P_i$ is defined as:

$$
\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}
$$

where $H(P_i) = -\sum_j p_{j \mid i} \log_2 p_{j \mid i}$ is the Shannon entropy of $P_i$.

**Step 2: Role in t-SNE**

Perplexity can be interpreted as a **smooth measure of the effective number of neighbors** for each point. In t-SNE, perplexity is a **user-defined hyperparameter** (typically between 5 and 50) that determines the bandwidth $\sigma_i$ of the Gaussian centered at each point $\mathbf{x}_i$. A binary search is performed for each $\sigma_i$ such that $\text{Perp}(P_i)$ equals the user-specified value.

**Step 3: Effect of Perplexity**

| Perplexity Value | $\sigma_i$ Behavior | Effect on Embedding |
|------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Too Low (e.g., 2-5)** | Small $\sigma_i$ | Captures **very local** structure; many tiny isolated clusters; sparse connectivity |
| **Optimal (e.g., 30)** | Moderate $\sigma_i$ | Balanced view: distinct clusters with clear separations |
| **Too High (e.g., 100+)** | Large $\sigma_i$ | Captures **global** structure; clusters may merge; less distinction between groups |

**Step 4: Practical Recommendations**

- **Run t-SNE with multiple perplexity values** (e.g., 5, 30, 50) to assess stability of the visualization.
- The **shape and size of clusters** in the t-SNE plot are **not meaningful** — only the **separation between clusters** and **presence of sub-clusters** are interpretable.
- Use **PCA initialization** (instead of random initialization) for more stable and reproducible results.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** ⚠️
> 1. **Confusing PCA with t-SNE**: PCA is linear and preserves global structure; t-SNE is non-linear and preserves local structure.
> 2. **Ignoring the symmetric joint probability**: Many students write only $p_{j \vert i}$ instead of $p_{ij}$.
> 3. **Mixing up the distributions**: t-SNE uses **Gaussian in HIGH-D** and **Student t in LOW-D**, not vice versa.
> 4. **Not mentioning KL Divergence**: The cost function is KL divergence, not mean squared error.
> 5. **Treating cluster sizes as meaningful**: In t-SNE, cluster sizes and distances between clusters in the output are NOT representative of the actual data geometry.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for t-SNE**

- ✅ **Full Form**: t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding
- ✅ **Inventors**: Laurens van der Maaten & Geoffrey Hinton (2008)
- ✅ **Type**: Non-linear, non-parametric dimensionality reduction
- ✅ **Primary Use**: Visualization (typically 2D or 3D)
- ✅ **High-D Distribution**: **Gaussian** (with bandwidth $\sigma_i$)
- ✅ **Low-D Distribution**: **Student t-distribution** (Cauchy, 1 degree of freedom)
- ✅ **Symmetric Joint Probability**: $p_{ij} = (p_{j \vert i} + p_{i \vert j})/2N$
- ✅ **Cost Function**: Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence: $C = \sum_{i \neq j} p_{ij} \log(p_{ij}/q_{ij})$
- ✅ **Optimization**: Gradient Descent with Momentum
- ✅ **Gradient Formula**: $\frac{\partial C}{\partial \mathbf{y}_i} = 4 \sum_{j} (p_{ij} - q_{ij})(\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j)(1 + \lVert \mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j \rVert^2)^{-1}$
- ✅ **Key Hyperparameter**: Perplexity (typically 5-50)
- ✅ **Perplexity Formula**: $\text{Perp}(P_i) = 2^{H(P_i)}$
- ✅ **Crowding Problem**: Solved by heavy-tailed Student t-distribution
- ✅ **Outlier Problem**: Solved by symmetric joint probability
- ✅ **Physical Interpretation**: Gradient is a spring force (attractive if $p_{ij} > q_{ij}$, repulsive if $p_{ij} < q_{ij}$)
- ✅ **Limitation**: Cluster sizes and inter-cluster distances in the t-SNE plot are **not meaningful** — only intra-cluster structure and inter-cluster separation are interpretable
- ✅ **Computational Complexity**: $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ (original), $\mathcal{O}(N \log N)$ (Barnes-Hut t-SNE)
- ✅ **Difference from PCA**: PCA is linear; t-SNE is non-linear and captures local neighborhood structure

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Evaluation Techniques: Train-Test Split
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Evaluation Techniques: Train-Test Split

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Train-Test Split** is a fundamental **resampling-based model evaluation technique** in supervised Machine Learning, wherein a given labelled dataset $D$ of size $n$ is partitioned into two mutually exclusive subsets: a **Training Set** ($D_{train}$) used to fit the model parameters, and a **Testing Set** ($D_{test}$) used exclusively to estimate the **generalization performance** (i.e., the predictive capability on *unseen* data).

Mathematically, the partition obeys:

$$D = D_{train} \cup D_{test}, \quad D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$$

$$\vert D \vert = \vert D_{train} \vert + \vert D_{test} \vert = n$$

The **split ratio** $r$ is conventionally defined as:

$$r = \frac{\vert D_{test} \vert}{n}$$

In KTU-recommended practice and the global ML community, the canonical ratios are **70:30**, **80:20**, or **75:25** (train:test).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Cooking School" Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are a culinary student preparing for a final cooking exam.
> - The **Train Set** is the practice recipes your chef-tutor gives you along with the *correct answers* (the actual dishes tasted by the tutor). You *learn* from these.
> - The **Test Set** is the mystery basket of ingredients on exam day — something the tutor has *never shown you before*. Your grade depends on how well your cooking skills **generalize** to this unseen basket.
> - If the tutor had secretly shown you the exam basket during practice, the grade would be useless (this is **data leakage**).
>
> Hence, we **strictly hide** a slice of data while training and reveal it only once at the end to honestly judge how the model performs in the real world.

### 1.3 Why Do We Need Train-Test Split?

A model that simply *memorizes* the training data will give **near-perfect scores** on it but fail miserably on new data. This phenomenon is known as **Overfitting**. The test set acts as an unbiased, untouched judge that flags overfitting and estimates real-world performance.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Introduced

> [!NOTE]
> The standard evaluation metrics that are computed **on the held-out test set** include:
> - **Accuracy** $\in [0,1]$
> - **Precision, Recall, F1-Score** (classification)
> - **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**, **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE)**, **Mean Absolute Error (MAE)**, **$R^2$** (regression)
> - **Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC-ROC)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Steps — The "Why" and "How"

The Train-Test Split procedure is decomposed into the following **seven logical steps**:

1. **Acquire the labelled dataset** $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$.
2. **Shuffle the dataset randomly** to break any ordering bias (e.g., temporal order in stock-price data). *Why:* ensures both subsets are i.i.d. representative samples.
3. **Choose the split ratio** $r$ (commonly $0.2$, $0.25$, or $0.3$). *Why:* trades off statistical power vs. evaluation reliability.
4. **Partition the data** into $D_{train}$ and $D_{test}$ without overlap.
5. **Fit (train) the model** $f_\theta$ on $D_{train}$ by minimizing a loss function $L(\theta)$:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \; \frac{1}{\vert D_{train} \vert} \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in D_{train}} L\big(f_\theta(x_i),\, y_i\big)$$

6. **Predict on the test set** using the frozen model $f_{\hat{\theta}}$.
7. **Compute the chosen metric(s)** on $D_{test}$ as the reported generalization estimate.

### 2.2 Issues / Limitations of Hold-Out (Train-Test Split)

> [!WARNING]
> The single Train-Test Split suffers from three classical problems that frequently appear in KTU 14-mark questions:
> 1. **High Variance of Estimate** — A single random split produces a noisy estimate; performance can swing wildly depending on which samples fall into $D_{test}$.
> 2. **Data Wastage** — Samples kept in $D_{test}$ are *never used* for training, so model parameters are learned from less data.
> 3. **Stratification Failure** — In imbalanced classification, a random split may produce a $D_{test}$ that lacks minority-class instances, distorting metric estimates.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Purpose / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Partition Identity | $D = D_{train} \cup D_{test},\; D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$ | Defines the disjoint split. |
| 2 | Split Ratio | $r = \vert D_{test} \vert / n$ | Controls test set size. |
| 3 | Training Fraction | $1 - r = \vert D_{train} \vert / n$ | Data used for fitting. |
| 4 | MSE (Regression) | $\text{MSE} = \tfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Average squared prediction error on test set. |
| 5 | RMSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Same units as target $y$. |
| 6 | MAE | $\text{MAE} = \tfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$ | Robust to outliers. |
| 7 | $R^2$ Score | $R^2 = 1 - \tfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | Variance explained by the model. |
| 8 | Accuracy | $\text{Acc} = \tfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness in classification. |
| 9 | Precision | $P = \tfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Quality of positive predictions. |
| 10 | Recall | $R = \tfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Coverage of actual positives. |
| 11 | F1-Score | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \tfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Harmonic mean of P & R. |
| 12 | Minimum Test-Size (95% CI, margin $e$) | $n_{test} \geq \tfrac{1.96^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}$ | Sample-size for reliable accuracy estimate. |
| 13 | Bias-Variance Decomposition | $\mathbb{E}[(y - \hat{f})^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$ | Diagnoses over/under-fitting. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Healthcare Diagnostics** | Validate cancer-detection models on unseen patient cohorts before deployment. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Hold-out test tracks evaluate perception models on rare scenarios. |
| **Financial Credit Scoring** | Split historical loan data to assess default-prediction robustness. |
| **Industrial Quality Control** | Use last-month's defect logs as $D_{test}$ to validate predictive maintenance models. |
| **NLP / Chatbots** | Held-out conversations benchmark language-model fluency and safety. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Minimum Test-Size Formula (for 95% Confidence)

**Goal:** Find the smallest test-set size $m$ that guarantees the estimated accuracy $\hat{p}$ lies within a margin $e$ of the true accuracy $p$ at the 95 % confidence level.

**Step 1 — Start with the Central Limit Theorem.**
For a proportion estimator $\hat{p}$ from a Bernoulli sample of size $m$:

$$\hat{p} \;\sim\; \mathcal{N}\!\left(p,\; \frac{p(1-p)}{m}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Standardize the estimator.**
Using the standard normal quantile $z_{0.975} = 1.96$:

$$P\!\left(\, \vert \hat{p} - p \vert \;\leq\; 1.96 \sqrt{\tfrac{p(1-p)}{m}} \,\right) \;=\; 0.95$$

**Step 3 — Set the margin of error equal to $e$.**
The 95 % confidence interval half-width is $e$:

$$e \;=\; 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{m}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $m$.**
Squaring both sides and rearranging:

$$e^2 \;=\; \frac{1.96^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{m}$$

$$\boxed{\,m \;\geq\; \frac{1.96^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Conservative plug-in.** Since $p$ is unknown a priori, we plug in the worst-case $p = 0.5$, giving $p(1-p) = 0.25$:

$$m \;\geq\; \frac{1.96^2 \cdot 0.25}{e^2} \;=\; \frac{0.9604}{e^2}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (End-to-End)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A KTU M.Tech project evaluates a spam-classifier on a corpus of $n = 10{,}000$ labelled e-mails. The team uses an 80:20 split.

**Step 1 — Compute split sizes.**

$$m_{train} = (1 - r)\cdot n = 0.8 \times 10{,}000 = 8{,}000$$

$$m_{test} = r \cdot n = 0.2 \times 10{,}000 = 2{,}000$$

**Step 2 — Train logistic regression** on the 8,000 e-mails. After convergence the model $f_{\hat{\theta}}$ is *frozen*.

**Step 3 — Predict on $D_{test}$** of 2,000 e-mails. Suppose we obtain the following **Confusion Matrix**:

|  | Predicted Spam | Predicted Ham |
|---|---:|---:|
| **Actual Spam** | TP = 280 | FN = 40 |
| **Actual Ham** | FP = 30 | TN = 1,650 |

**Step 4 — Compute Accuracy.**

$$\text{Acc} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = \frac{280 + 1650}{2000} = \frac{1930}{2000} = 0.965 \; (96.5\%)$$

**Step 5 — Compute Precision.**

$$P = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{280}{280 + 30} = \frac{280}{310} \approx 0.9032$$

**Step 6 — Compute Recall.**

$$R = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{280}{280 + 40} = \frac{280}{320} = 0.8750$$

**Step 7 — Compute F1-Score.**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.9032 \times 0.8750}{0.9032 + 0.8750} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.7903}{1.7782} \approx 0.8889$$

**Step 8 — Margin of error at 95 % CI.**

Using the formula derived in §3.1:

$$e = 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{m_{test}}} = 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{0.965 \times 0.035}{2000}}$$

$$e = 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{0.03378}{2000}} = 1.96 \sqrt{1.689 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$e = 1.96 \times 0.004110 \approx 0.00806 \;\;(0.806\%)$$

**Step 9 — Final reportable statement.**
The classifier's true accuracy on unseen data lies in:

$$[\,0.965 - 0.00806,\; 0.965 + 0.00806\,] = [\,0.9569,\; 0.9731\,]$$

at 95 % confidence.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Split Ratio

The following table is a **favourite KTU 14-mark analytical question**.

| Split Ratio | Training Data | Test Data | Bias | Variance of Estimate | Use When |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---|
| **50 : 50** | Smallest | Largest | High | Lowest | Very large $n$ |
| **60 : 40** | Small | Large | High | Low | Large $n$ |
| **70 : 30** | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Moderate | Industry default |
| **80 : 20** | Large | Moderate | Low | Moderate | KTU standard default |
| **90 : 10** | Very large | Very small | Lowest | High | Huge $n \geq 100k$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for Exam:** *"More training → less bias, more test → less variance."* The split ratio balances the two.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Train-Test Split Evaluation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Labelled Dataset D, size n] --> B[Random Shuffle]
    B --> C{Split Ratio r}
    C -->|1 minus r fraction| D[Training Set Dtrain]
    C -->|r fraction| E[Test Set Dtest]
    D --> F[Model Training f theta]
    F --> G[Frozen Model f hat theta]
    E --> H[Generate Predictions y hat]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Metrics on Dtest]
    I --> J[Accuracy]
    I --> K[Precision]
    I --> L[Recall]
    I --> M[F1 Score]
    I --> N[MSE or RMSE]
    J --> O[Generalization Estimate]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Deployment Decision]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Bias-Variance Interaction Across Splits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Split Ratio 50:50] --> A1[Bias HIGH]
    A --> A2[Variance LOW]
    B[Split Ratio 70:30] --> B1[Bias MODERATE]
    B --> B2[Variance MODERATE]
    C[Split Ratio 80:20] --> C1[Bias LOW]
    C --> C2[Variance MODERATE]
    D[Split Ratio 90:10] --> D1[Bias LOWEST]
    D --> D2[Variance HIGH]
    E[Ideal Choice] --> E1[Use K Fold Cross Validation]
    E --> E2[Or Stratified K Fold]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Position of Train-Test Split in the ML Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Problem Definition] --> P2[Data Collection]
    P2 --> P3[Data Cleaning]
    P3 --> P4[EDA and Preprocessing]
    P4 --> P5[Train Test Split]
    P5 --> P6[Model Training on Dtrain]
    P6 --> P7[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    P7 --> P8[Final Evaluation on Dtest]
    P8 --> P9{Performance Acceptable?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Deployment]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Iterate: New Features or Model]
    P11 --> P5
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q1]**

**Q1. Define Train-Test Split. Why is it necessary in supervised machine learning?**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Train-Test Split is a model-evaluation technique in which a labelled dataset $D$ of size $n$ is randomly partitioned into two disjoint subsets: a **training set** $D_{train}$ used to learn the model parameters, and a **testing set** $D_{test}$ used to estimate the model's performance on unseen data. It is necessary because training accuracy alone is an **optimistically biased** estimate of real-world performance — a model can memorize training data (overfit). The held-out test set provides an **unbiased estimate of the generalization error**, helping to detect overfitting and to fairly compare candidate models before deployment.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q2]**

**Q2. List any three standard evaluation metrics used to assess a regression model on the test set.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The three standard regression metrics computed on $D_{test}$ are:

1. **Mean Squared Error (MSE):** $\text{MSE} = \tfrac{1}{m}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$. Penalizes large errors quadratically.
2. **Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE):** $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$. Same unit as $y$, easier to interpret.
3. **$R^2$ Score (Coefficient of Determination):** $R^2 = 1 - \tfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$. Measures the fraction of variance in $y$ explained by the model.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Q, Dec 2024]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **Train-Test Split** procedure for evaluating a supervised machine learning model. Discuss the impact of the split ratio on model performance.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition & Notation (2 marks):**
The labelled dataset $D$ of size $n$ is split into $D_{train}$ (used to fit model parameters) and $D_{test}$ (used only for evaluation). They satisfy $D = D_{train} \cup D_{test}$ and $D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$.

**2. Procedure Steps (3 marks):**

- **Shuffle** $D$ randomly to remove ordering bias.
- **Choose split ratio** $r$ (commonly 0.2 or 0.3 for the test set).
- **Partition** data into $D_{train}$ of size $(1-r)n$ and $D_{test}$ of size $rn$.
- **Train** the model $f_\theta$ by minimizing the empirical loss on $D_{train}$:

$$\hat{\theta} = \arg\min_{\theta} \frac{1}{(1-r)n} \sum_{(x_i, y_i) \in D_{train}} L(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$$

- **Freeze** $f_{\hat{\theta}}$ and **predict** on $D_{test}$.
- **Compute** evaluation metric (Accuracy, MSE, etc.) as the generalization estimate.

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(See §4.1 Mermaid block diagram above.)*

**4. Impact of Split Ratio (1 mark):**
- **Too small test set** (e.g., 90:10) → high variance in metric estimate, unreliable generalization signal.
- **Too small training set** (e.g., 50:50) → model underfits due to insufficient learning data.
- **Balanced choice** (70:30 or 80:20) is a practical compromise for moderate $n$.

---

**(b)** Discuss the **limitations of the hold-out (Train-Test Split) method**. Explain how **$k$-Fold Cross-Validation** overcomes these limitations, with a neat sketch of the procedure.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Limitations of Train-Test Split (3 marks):**

- **High Variance of Estimate:** A single random split produces a noisy performance metric; different runs yield different scores.
- **Data Wastage:** The test samples are never used for training, reducing the data available for learning — critical when $n$ is small.
- **No Stratification Guarantee:** In imbalanced classification, a random split may produce a test set lacking minority-class samples, biasing metric estimates.
- **Sensitivity to Split Ratio:** Choosing the wrong $r$ leads to either unreliable estimates or underfit models.

**2. $k$-Fold Cross-Validation Procedure (3 marks):**

- The dataset $D$ is randomly partitioned into $k$ **equal-sized, mutually exclusive folds** $F_1, F_2, \dots, F_k$.
- For each iteration $i = 1, 2, \dots, k$:
  - Use $F_i$ as the **validation set**.
  - Use the remaining $k-1$ folds as the **training set**.
  - Train the model and record the validation metric $M_i$.
- The **final estimate** is the average across folds:

$$\text{CV Score} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} M_i$$

Common choices: $k = 5$ or $k = 10$.

**3. How It Overcomes Limitations (1 mark):**
Every sample is used for both training and validation, eliminating data wastage. Averaging across $k$ folds reduces the variance of the performance estimate. *Stratified $k$-Fold* additionally preserves the class distribution in each fold, addressing the imbalance issue.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **Bias-Variance tradeoff** in the context of Train-Test Split. Show how the **expected prediction error** can be decomposed into bias, variance, and irreducible noise.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Concept of Bias and Variance (2 marks):**

- **Bias** = error due to the model's simplifying assumptions (underfitting). High bias → model is too rigid (e.g., linear model on non-linear data).
- **Variance** = error due to sensitivity to fluctuations in the training set (overfitting). High variance → model captures noise.

**2. Decomposition (3 marks):**
For a true function $f(x)$ and learned model $\hat{f}(x)$ with irreducible noise $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(y - \hat{f}(x)\big)^2\right] = \underbrace{\big(f(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^2}_{\text{Bias}^2} + \underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\left[\big(\hat{f}(x) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{f}(x)]\big)^2\right]}_{\text{Variance}} + \underbrace{\sigma^2}_{\text{Irreducible Noise}}$$

**Derivation sketch:** Expand the squared error, apply expectation, and use the identity $\text{Var}(Z) = \mathbb{E}[Z^2] - (\mathbb{E}[Z])^2$.

**3. Relation to Split Ratio (2 marks):**
- A **large training set** (small $r$, e.g., 90:10) → model sees more data → variance drops, but bias may rise if model is too simple.
- A **large test set** (large $r$, e.g., 50:50) → less training data → variance may rise, but the metric estimate is more reliable.
- The **optimal split** is the one that minimizes the *test error*, which equals Bias² + Variance + $\sigma^2$.

---

**(b)** A machine-learning team has a dataset of **$n = 5{,}000$ samples**. They perform an **80:20 Train-Test Split** and obtain a test **accuracy of 92 %**. Calculate the **95 % confidence interval** for the true accuracy. Comment on whether 2,000 test samples are sufficient.
*(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $n = 5{,}000$, split ratio $r = 0.20$, so $m_{test} = 0.20 \times 5000 = 1{,}000$, $\hat{p} = 0.92$.

**Step 1 — Margin of error using $z_{0.975} = 1.96$:**

$$e = 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{m_{test}}} = 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{0.92 \times 0.08}{1000}}$$

$$e = 1.96 \sqrt{\frac{0.0736}{1000}} = 1.96 \sqrt{7.36 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$e = 1.96 \times 0.008580 \approx 0.01682$$

**Step 2 — 95 % Confidence Interval:**

$$\text{CI} = \big[\hat{p} - e,\; \hat{p} + e\big] = [\,0.92 - 0.01682,\; 0.92 + 0.01682\,] = [\,0.9032,\; 0.9368\,]$$

**Step 3 — Sufficiency check using the conservative formula:**
For a desired margin $e = 0.02$ (i.e., ±2 %) at 95 % CI:

$$m_{required} \geq \frac{1.96^2 \times 0.25}{0.02^2} = \frac{0.9604}{0.0004} = 2{,}401$$

**Step 4 — Comment (2 marks):**
- The actual test set has only $m_{test} = 1{,}000$ samples, which is **less than the required 2,401** for a ±2 % margin.
- Hence, the estimate is **not precise enough** for production-grade reporting.
- The team should either (i) **increase the test set** to at least 2,401 samples (≈ 48:52 split), or (ii) **use 10-fold cross-validation** to leverage all 5,000 samples for both training and testing.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Train-Validation-Test:** Many students mistakenly use the test set for hyperparameter tuning. The correct practice is: **Train → Validation (for tuning) → Test (only once, at the end).**
> 2. **Forgetting to Shuffle:** Sequential data without shuffling leads to data leakage and inflated metrics. Always **shuffle before splitting**.
> 3. **Using Test Data in Training:** Once a sample enters $D_{test}$, it must be **completely isolated** — touching it for training or feature scaling (without proper `fit` on train only) is a **data leakage** violation.
> 4. **Ignoring Stratification:** In imbalanced datasets, always use `stratify=y` so the class proportions are preserved in both subsets.
> 5. **Reporting Training Accuracy as Final Accuracy:** A very common KTU mistake — the reported final accuracy **must** be on $D_{test}$, not on $D_{train}$.
> 6. **Wrong Units / Variables in Formulas:** Mark down for using training-set $m$ instead of test-set $m$ in the confidence-interval formula.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
> - **Definition:** Disjoint random partition of $D$ into $D_{train}$ and $D_{test}$ for honest generalization estimation.
> - **Disjointness is mandatory:** $D_{train} \cap D_{test} = \emptyset$.
> - **Default split ratios:** **70:30** or **80:20** (train:test).
> - **Always shuffle** before splitting to remove ordering bias.
> - **Use `random_state`** for reproducibility — KTU exams often award marks for reproducibility awareness.
> - **Use stratification** (`stratify=y`) for classification with class imbalance.
> - **Metrics on test set only:** Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, MSE, RMSE, $R^2$, AUC-ROC.
> - **Confidence interval formula:** $m \geq 1.96^2 \cdot p(1-p) / e^2$.
> - **Bias-Variance decomposition:** $\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
> - **Trade-off rule:** Larger training set → lower variance; larger test set → more reliable metric estimate.
> - **Limitations:** High variance of estimate, data wastage, no stratification guarantee.
> - **Upgrade path:** When limitations become critical, switch to **$k$-Fold Cross-Validation** ($k = 5$ or $10$).
> - **Anti-leakage mantra:** *"Fit on train, transform on test — never the reverse."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cross-Validation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cross-Validation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cross-Validation (CV)** is a statistical resampling technique used in machine learning to assess the **generalization performance** and **stability** of a learned model. It systematically partitions the available dataset into complementary subsets, trains the model on a subset (the *training fold*), and validates it on the held-out subset (the *validation fold*), repeating this process across multiple partitions to obtain a robust estimate of the model's performance on unseen data.

Mathematically, cross-validation provides an estimate of the **expected generalization error**:

$$E_{(x, y) \sim D_{\text{test}}}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)]$$

where $D_{\text{test}}$ represents the true (unknown) data distribution, $\mathcal{L}$ is the loss function, and $f$ is the learned predictor. Since the true distribution is inaccessible, CV approximates this expectation using a finite, resampled dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Cross-Validation falls under *Model Evaluation & Selection* in Module 4. It is a **mandatory concept** for both Continuous Assessment (CAT) and End Semester Examinations (ESE). The student is expected to compare k-Fold, Leave-One-Out, Stratified, and Hold-Out validation strategies, and to derive the bias–variance trade-off of each.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a medical student preparing for a final surgery exam. Instead of practicing the operation only once on a single patient (a *train-test split*), the student performs the surgery **10 times on 10 different patients**, where each time **9 patients are used for practice and 1 is reserved for evaluation**. The final grade is the **average performance across all 10 evaluations**.

- If the student does well on average, the skill is **generalizable**.
- If the student does well on *some* patients and poorly on *others*, the model is **unstable** (high variance).
- If the student trains on too few patients, the practice is **biased** (the model underfits).

This is precisely what **k-Fold Cross-Validation** does with data: it rotates which subset is used for testing, ensuring every data point contributes to both training and validation exactly once (or k−1 times in standard k-Fold).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we cannot just do a single Train/Test Split:** A single split gives a *noisy* and possibly *optimistic* or *pessimistic* estimate depending on the random sample. CV averages over multiple splits to reduce this variance, yielding a **statistically reliable** performance estimate.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Definitions)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Fold** | One partition of the dataset used as a validation set in a single CV iteration. |
| **k** | The number of equal-sized partitions; the hyperparameter of k-Fold CV. |
| **Training Fold** | The k−1 subsets used to fit the model in each iteration. |
| **Validation Fold** | The single held-out subset used to evaluate the fitted model. |
| **Generalization Error** | The expected prediction error of a model on previously unseen data drawn from the true distribution. |
| **Bias** | The systematic error introduced by approximating a complex real-world problem with a simplified model. |
| **Variance** | The amount by which the model's predictions would change if we trained on a different training set. |
| **Stratification** | Preserving the class distribution of the original dataset in each fold. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of k-Fold Cross-Validation

The standard **k-Fold CV** procedure follows these structured steps:

1. **Shuffle** the dataset randomly (optional but recommended to break ordering bias).
2. **Split** the dataset into $k$ equal (or near-equal) partitions called *folds*. Each fold has size $\approx n/k$, where $n$ is the total number of samples.
3. For iteration $i$ from $1$ to $k$:
   - Use **fold $i$** as the validation set.
   - Use the **remaining $k-1$ folds** as the training set.
   - **Train** the model $M_i$ on the training set.
   - **Evaluate** $M_i$ on the validation set, recording the score $s_i$ (e.g., accuracy, MSE, F1).
4. **Aggregate** the $k$ scores to produce a single performance estimate:
   $$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i$$
5. Optionally compute the **standard deviation**:
   $$\sigma_{CV} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{k-1} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (s_i - CV_{(k)})^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> The standard deviation $\sigma_{CV}$ is **often more important than the mean** in KTU exam questions. A low mean with a high $\sigma$ indicates an *unstable* model — a common pitfall in KTU valuation.

---

## 2.2 Taxonomy of Cross-Validation Strategies

| CV Strategy | k Value | Training Size | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hold-Out** | 1 split (k=1 conceptually) | Typically 70–80% of $n$ | Large datasets; quick baseline estimate. |
| **k-Fold** | User-defined (typically 5 or 10) | $(k-1) \cdot (n/k)$ | General-purpose; industry default. |
| **Stratified k-Fold** | Same as k-Fold | Same as k-Fold | Imbalanced classification tasks. |
| **Leave-One-Out (LOOCV)** | $k = n$ | $n-1$ samples per iteration | Small datasets; nearly unbiased but high variance. |
| **Leave-p-Out (LpOCV)** | $k = \binom{n}{p}$ | $n-p$ samples | Research; computationally prohibitive. |
| **Repeated k-Fold** | k × repeats | Same as k-Fold | Reducing variance of k-Fold estimate. |
| **Time Series Split** | Sequential | Expanding window | Forecasting / temporal data. |
| **Group k-Fold** | User-defined | Group-disjoint | Data with non-i.i.d. group structure. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas appear in nearly every KTU ESE and CAT paper on Model Evaluation. Memorize them in this exact form.**

### Core Cross-Validation Estimator

$$\hat{E}_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathcal{L}_i$$

where $\mathcal{L}_i$ is the loss on the $i^{th}$ validation fold.

### Bias–Variance Decomposition of the CV Estimate

$$\mathbb{E}[(\hat{E}_{CV} - E_{\text{true}})^2] = \text{Bias}^2(\hat{E}_{CV}) + \text{Var}(\hat{E}_{CV})$$

### Approximate Bias of k-Fold CV (relative to LOOCV)

$$\text{Bias}_{k\text{-Fold}} \approx \frac{1}{k} \cdot \text{Bias}_{\text{LOOCV}}$$

(LOOCV is the *least biased*; as $k \to n$, bias $\to 0$.)

### Variance of k-Fold Mean Score

$$\text{Var}(\hat{E}_{CV}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{k} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{(k-1)\rho}{1}\right)$$

where $\rho$ is the *intra-fold correlation* — a measure of how similar the training sets are between folds.

### LOOCV Single-Observation Identity (for squared error)

$$\text{CV}_{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i) \right)^2$$

The $\hat{f}^{(-i)}$ notation denotes the model trained on all observations *except* the $i^{th}$.

### Effective Number of Training Samples

$$n_{\text{train}}^{(i)} = n - \frac{n}{k} = n \cdot \frac{k-1}{k}$$

### Stratification Constraint (Classification)

$$\frac{n_j^{(i)}}{n^{(i)}} = \frac{n_j}{n}, \quad \forall j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, C\}$$

where $n_j$ is the count of class $j$, $C$ is the number of classes, and the superscript $(i)$ denotes fold $i$.

---

## 2.4 Why k = 10 is the Industry Default

The value $k = 10$ was popularized by Ron Kohavi's 1995 empirical study (*A Study of Cross-Validation and Bootstrap for Accuracy Estimation and Model Selection*, IJCAI). His findings are widely accepted:

- $k = 10$ provides a **good compromise** between bias (which decreases as $k$ increases) and computational cost (which grows as $k$ increases).
- $k = 5$ and $k = 10$ yield **similar variance reduction** in practice.
- LOOCV ($k = n$) has **low bias but high variance** because the training sets across folds are nearly identical (differ by only 1 sample), making them highly correlated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In production ML pipelines (e.g., at Google, Amazon, healthcare AI systems), k-Fold CV is used to:
> 1. **Compare models** (SVM vs. Random Forest) on a fair basis.
> 2. **Tune hyperparameters** (GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV in scikit-learn).
> 3. **Estimate the deployment risk** — a high $\sigma_{CV}$ flags an unreliable model before it ships to production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why LOOCV is Approximately Unbiased

We will prove that the **expected value** of the LOOCV estimator equals the **expected out-of-sample error** under a true model.

### Setup

Let $\mathcal{D}_n = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$ be the full dataset of $n$ i.i.d. samples drawn from the true distribution $P(x, y)$.

Let $\mathcal{D}_n^{(-i)} = \mathcal{D}_n \setminus \{(x_i, y_i)\}$ be the dataset with the $i^{th}$ observation removed.

Let $\hat{f}^{(-i)}$ be the model trained on $\mathcal{D}_n^{(-i)}$.

### Step 1: Define the LOOCV Risk Estimator

The LOOCV estimator of the prediction error is:

$$\text{CV}_{LOO} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathcal{L}\!\left(\hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i), y_i\right)$$

For squared loss, this becomes:

$$\text{CV}_{LOO} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_i - \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i) \right)^2$$

### Step 2: Take the Expectation over the Sampling Distribution

We compute the expectation with respect to the random draw of $\mathcal{D}_n$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_n}\!\left[ \text{CV}_{LOO} \right] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_n}\!\left[ \left( y_i - \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i) \right)^2 \right]$$

### Step 3: Exploit Symmetry via Exchangeability

Since the samples are i.i.d., the joint distribution of $\mathcal{D}_n^{(-i)}$ is *identical* for every $i$ (this is the i.i.d. assumption). Therefore, the expectation does not depend on $i$:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_n}\!\left[ \left( y_i - \hat{f}^{(-i)}(x_i) \right)^2 \right] = \mathbb{E}_{(x^*, y^*), \mathcal{D}_{n-1}}\!\left[ \left( y^* - \hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}_{n-1}}(x^*) \right)^2 \right]$$

where $(x^*, y^*)$ is a fresh test sample drawn independently from $P$, and $\hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}_{n-1}}$ is trained on a dataset of size $n-1$.

### Step 4: Recognize the Expected Test Error on n−1 Trained Model

The right-hand side is precisely the **expected prediction error of a model trained on $n-1$ samples and tested on a fresh sample**:

$$\mathbb{E}_{(x^*, y^*), \mathcal{D}_{n-1}}\!\left[ \left( y^* - \hat{f}_{\mathcal{D}_{n-1}}(x^*) \right)^2 \right] = \text{Err}_{n-1}$$

### Step 5: Conclude

Therefore:

$$\mathbb{E}_{\mathcal{D}_n}\!\left[ \text{CV}_{LOO} \right] = \text{Err}_{n-1}$$

This is an *almost unbiased* estimator of the test error, with a small bias of order $O(1/n)$ because the model is trained on $n-1$ samples rather than the full $n$ used at deployment. Hence the name **"approximately unbiased."**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bias–Variance Trade-off in Choosing k

We will derive why **larger k gives lower bias** and why **smaller k gives lower variance** (in some regimes).

### Bias of k-Fold CV

When we use $k$-Fold, the model is trained on $\frac{k-1}{k} \cdot n$ samples per fold. The expected error of a model trained on $m$ samples typically scales as:

$$\text{Err}(m) = \text{Err}_{\infty} + \frac{c}{m} + O\!\left(\frac{1}{m^2}\right)$$

where $\text{Err}_{\infty}$ is the asymptotic (Bayes) error, and $c$ is a model-dependent constant.

For k-Fold, the expected size of the training set is $m_k = n \cdot (k-1)/k$. So:

$$\mathbb{E}\!\left[ \text{Err}_{k\text{-Fold}} \right] \approx \text{Err}_{\infty} + \frac{c}{n \cdot (k-1)/k} = \text{Err}_{\infty} + \frac{c \cdot k}{n(k-1)}$$

The **bias** (relative to the deployed model trained on full $n$) is:

$$\text{Bias}_k = \text{Err}_{\infty} + \frac{c \cdot k}{n(k-1)} - \left( \text{Err}_{\infty} + \frac{c}{n} \right) = \frac{c \cdot k}{n(k-1)} - \frac{c}{n}$$

Simplifying:

$$\text{Bias}_k = \frac{c}{n} \left( \frac{k}{k-1} - 1 \right) = \frac{c}{n(k-1)}$$

**Conclusion:** As $k \to n$ (LOOCV), $\text{Bias}_k \to 0$. As $k$ decreases, the bias **grows**. For $k=2$:

$$\text{Bias}_{2} = \frac{c}{n}$$

### Variance of k-Fold CV

For independent folds, the variance of the mean score is simply $\sigma^2 / k$. But for k-Fold, the folds are **not independent** because the training sets overlap heavily.

A first-order approximation (Bengio & Grandvalet, 2004) gives:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{E}_{CV}) = \frac{\sigma^2}{k} \left[ 1 + (k-1) \rho_{\text{train}} \right]$$

where $\rho_{\text{train}}$ is the average correlation between the scores from two different folds (induced by overlap in training data).

- For **LOOCV** ($k = n$): $\rho_{\text{train}}$ is high (training sets differ by only 1 sample), so the effective variance is large.
- For **small $k$** (e.g., $k=2$ or $k=5$): training sets are more disjoint, $\rho_{\text{train}}$ is lower, so variance is reduced.

**Conclusion:** There is a **bias–variance trade-off** in the choice of $k$. A $k$ that is too small (e.g., 2) has high bias; a $k$ that is too large (e.g., $n$) has high variance. The **sweet spot is empirically near $k = 5$ or $k = 10$**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which CV

> [!NOTE]
> **This decision matrix is a high-frequency KTU question type** — expect 7-mark questions asking you to *justify* your choice of CV method for a given scenario.

| Scenario | Recommended CV | Justification |
|---|---|---|
