| **Large dataset ($n > 100{,}000$), imbalanced classes** | **Stratified k-Fold (k=5)** | Computationally cheap; preserves class balance. |
| **Very small dataset ($n < 100$)** | **LOOCV** | Maximizes training data per fold; minimizes bias. |
| **Time-series data (stock prices, sensor logs)** | **Time Series Split** | Prevents future leakage into training. |
| **Grouped data (multiple samples per patient)** | **Group k-Fold** | Ensures groups don't leak across folds. |
| **Hyperparameter tuning** | **Repeated k-Fold + GridSearch** | Reduces noise in selection. |
| **Quick baseline estimate** | **Hold-Out (70/30)** | Fastest; acceptable for huge datasets. |
| **Research paper needing tightest error bound** | **10 × 10-Fold (Nested CV)** | Lowest variance and unbiased evaluation. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Operational Flow of k-Fold Cross-Validation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Dataset D of size n] --> B[Shuffle D Randomly]
    B --> C[Partition D into k Equal Folds]
    C --> D[Initialize Scores List Empty]
    D --> E[For i = 1 to k]
    E --> F[Assign Fold i as Validation Set]
    F --> G[Concatenate Remaining k-1 Folds as Training Set]
    G --> H[Train Model M on Training Set]
    H --> I[Evaluate M on Validation Fold]
    I --> J[Record Score s sub i]
    J --> K{i equals k?}
    K -->|No| E
    K -->|Yes| L[Compute Mean Score mu CV]
    L --> M[Compute Standard Deviation sigma CV]
    M --> N[Output Final Estimate and Uncertainty]
    N --> O[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block Architecture of CV Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Dataset] --> B{Is Data Temporal?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Use Time Series Split]
    B -->|No| D{Are There Natural Groups?}
    D -->|Yes| E[Use Group k Fold]
    D -->|No| F{Is Dataset Small n less than 100?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Use Leave One Out CV]
    F -->|No| H{Are Classes Imbalanced?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Use Stratified k Fold]
    H -->|No| J{Is Goal Hyperparameter Tuning?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Use Repeated k Fold with GridSearch]
    J -->|No| L[Use Standard k Fold with k equals 10]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bias-Variance Trade-off in k

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose k] --> B[Large k near n]
    A --> C[Small k like 2 or 5]
    B --> D[Low Bias, High Variance]
    C --> E[High Bias, Low Variance]
    D --> F[Empirical Sweet Spot at k equals 5 or 10]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Minimize Total Estimation Error]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Stratified k-Fold Partitioning (Class Preservation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DATASET["Full Dataset: 1000 Samples, Class A 700, Class B 300"]
        direction LR
        DATASET_A[Class A: 700 samples]
        DATASET_B[Class B: 300 samples]
    end

    subgraph FOLD1["Fold 1 (Validation)"]
        F1A[Class A: 70 samples]
        F1B[Class B: 30 samples]
    end

    subgraph FOLD2["Fold 2 (Validation)"]
        F2A[Class A: 70 samples]
        F2B[Class B: 30 samples]
    end

    subgraph FOLD3["Fold 3 (Validation)"]
        F3A[Class A: 70 samples]
        F3B[Class B: 30 samples]
    end

    DATASET --> FOLD1
    DATASET --> FOLD2
    DATASET --> FOLD3
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In the diagram above, notice that every fold preserves the 70:30 class ratio of the original dataset.** This is the defining property of Stratified k-Fold, and it is **worth 2 marks by itself** in a KTU short-answer question.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define *cross-validation*. Mention any two advantages of k-Fold cross-validation over the hold-out method.

**Model Answer:**

Cross-validation is a resampling-based model evaluation technique in which the dataset is partitioned into $k$ complementary subsets; the model is trained on $k-1$ subsets and validated on the held-out $k^{th}$ subset, repeating this process $k$ times so that every observation serves as validation data exactly once.

**Two advantages over hold-out:**

1. **Reduced variance of the performance estimate:** By averaging over $k$ different train/validation splits, the random fluctuation caused by the luck of a single hold-out split is smoothed out. The standard deviation of the mean is $\sigma / \sqrt{k}$, which decreases as $k$ increases.

2. **Better utilization of data:** In hold-out, a fixed fraction (e.g., 30%) of the data is "wasted" for testing and never contributes to model training. In k-Fold CV, every sample contributes to training in $k-1$ of the $k$ iterations, yielding a more data-efficient estimate — especially valuable on small datasets.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** What is *stratified* k-Fold cross-validation? Why is it preferred for imbalanced classification problems?

**Model Answer:**

Stratified k-Fold cross-validation is a variant of k-Fold in which each fold is constructed so that it preserves the **class proportion** of the original dataset. If the original dataset has 70% Class A and 30% Class B, every fold will also contain approximately 70% Class A and 30% Class B.

**Why preferred for imbalanced data:**

In a standard k-Fold split on imbalanced data, a small fold may contain *zero* samples of the minority class, leading to:
- A validation score of either 0% or 100% for that class.
- High variance in the per-fold metric.
- A misleading aggregate performance estimate.

Stratification guarantees that every fold is *representative* of the class distribution, producing **stable, comparable per-fold metrics** and a reliable mean F1 / AUC estimate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions on Module 4 carry 14 marks with a sub-division of 7 + 7 marks. The student is offered a choice between two full questions; only one is to be attempted.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **k-Fold cross-validation** procedure. Discuss how the choice of $k$ affects the **bias and variance** of the resulting error estimate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The k-Fold CV procedure is as follows (supported by the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):

1. The dataset of $n$ samples is randomly partitioned into $k$ approximately equal folds.
2. For each $i = 1, 2, \ldots, k$:
   - Fold $i$ is held out as the validation set.
   - The remaining $k-1$ folds form the training set.
   - A model $M_i$ is trained and evaluated on fold $i$, yielding score $s_i$.
3. The final CV score is the mean:
   $$CV_{(k)} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i$$

**Effect of k on Bias:** In each fold, the model is trained on $n(k-1)/k$ samples. As $k$ increases, this approaches $n$ (the full dataset), so the trained model is closer to the deployed model — hence **bias decreases as $k$ increases**. Quantitatively (derived in Section 3.2):

$$\text{Bias}_k \approx \frac{c}{n(k-1)}$$

**Effect of k on Variance:** The training sets across folds overlap heavily when $k$ is large (e.g., LOOCV folds differ by only 1 sample). This high overlap induces strong correlation $\rho$ between fold scores, so the variance does not shrink as $1/k$ but as:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{E}_{CV}) \propto \frac{\sigma^2}{k}[1 + (k-1)\rho]$$

As $k \to n$, the variance **increases** (since $\rho$ stays high). The empirical sweet spot is $k = 5$ or $k = 10$, where bias is moderate and variance is reasonably low.

---

**(b)** A dataset has $n = 100$ samples. Compute the LOOCV training-set size, the LOOCV validation-set size, and the 5-Fold training-set size. Briefly explain why LOOCV is preferred when $n$ is small. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**LOOCV** corresponds to $k = n = 100$:

$$n_{\text{val}}^{\text{LOO}} = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{100}{100} = 1 \text{ sample}$$

$$n_{\text{train}}^{\text{LOO}} = n - 1 = 99 \text{ samples}$$

**5-Fold CV** corresponds to $k = 5$:

$$n_{\text{val}}^{5\text{-Fold}} = \frac{n}{k} = \frac{100}{5} = 20 \text{ samples}$$

$$n_{\text{train}}^{5\text{-Fold}} = n - 20 = 80 \text{ samples}$$

**Why LOOCV is preferred for small $n$:**

When $n$ is small, every training sample is precious. In 5-Fold, each model is trained on only 80 samples; if the underlying model needs more data to converge, the per-fold estimate is biased downward. LOOCV uses 99 of the 100 samples for training, so the trained model is as close as possible to the model that would be deployed on the full dataset. This **minimizes the optimistic bias** of the CV estimator, which scales as $c / n(k-1)$. For $n = 100, k = 100$, this bias is $c / 9900 \approx 0$, whereas for $k = 5$ it is $c / 400$ — roughly **25× larger**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students frequently write "$k = n$ means we use all data for training." This is **incorrect**. In LOOCV, exactly **one** sample is held out per iteration, and the model is trained on $n-1$ samples. The model is evaluated $n$ times — once per held-out sample. Writing the wrong formula is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Compare **Hold-Out**, **k-Fold**, and **Leave-One-Out** cross-validation with respect to (i) bias of the error estimate, (ii) variance of the error estimate, (iii) computational cost, and (iv) data utilization. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Hold-Out | k-Fold | Leave-One-Out |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Bias** | **High** (model trains on smaller subset, e.g., 70% of $n$) | **Moderate** (model trains on $(k-1)/k$ of $n$) | **Lowest** (model trains on $n-1$ samples) |
| **(ii) Variance** | **High** (single split, no averaging) | **Low** (averages over $k$ splits) | **Highest** (training sets are nearly identical across folds, high $\rho$) |
| **(iii) Computational Cost** | **Lowest** (1 training run) | **Moderate** ($k$ training runs) | **Highest** ($n$ training runs) |
| **(iv) Data Utilization** | **Poor** (test set never used in training) | **Good** (each sample trains in $k-1$ folds) | **Maximum** (each sample trains in $n-1$ folds) |

**Summary Trade-off:** Hold-Out is fast but unstable. LOOCV is data-efficient but expensive and noisy. k-Fold (typically $k=5$ or $k=10$) offers the best balance — which is why it is the **default in scikit-learn and most ML libraries**.

---

**(b)** Explain the concept of **stratified k-Fold cross-validation** with a suitable example. Show mathematically that each fold preserves the original class ratio. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** Stratified k-Fold is a variant of k-Fold in which each fold is constructed such that the **relative frequency** of each class in the fold matches that of the original dataset. This is critical for classification tasks, especially with imbalanced classes.

**Example:** Suppose the original dataset has $n = 1000$ samples with class distribution:
- Class 0: 800 samples (80%)
- Class 1: 200 samples (20%)

For 5-Fold Stratified CV, each fold of 200 samples must contain:
- Class 0: $0.80 \times 200 = 160$ samples
- Class 1: $0.20 \times 200 = 40$ samples

**Mathematical proof of preservation:**

Let $n_j$ be the total count of class $j$ in the dataset, and $n_j^{(i)}$ be the count in fold $i$. Stratification requires:

$$\frac{n_j^{(i)}}{n^{(i)}} = \frac{n_j}{n}, \quad \forall j \in \{1, \ldots, C\}$$

Solving for $n_j^{(i)}$:

$$n_j^{(i)} = n^{(i)} \cdot \frac{n_j}{n}$$

Since $n^{(i)} = n/k$ (fold size):

$$n_j^{(i)} = \frac{n}{k} \cdot \frac{n_j}{n} = \frac{n_j}{k}$$

Summing over all folds:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_j^{(i)} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_j}{k} = k \cdot \frac{n_j}{k} = n_j$$

This confirms that every sample of class $j$ is assigned to exactly one fold, and the per-fold class ratio equals the global class ratio. The fold-level class proportion is:

$$\frac{n_j^{(i)}}{n/k} = \frac{n_j/k}{n/k} = \frac{n_j}{n} = \text{(global proportion)}$$

Hence, the **original class distribution is preserved in every fold**. $\blacksquare$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> A common student mistake is to apply Stratified k-Fold to a **regression** problem. Stratification is defined for *categorical* target variables. For regression with continuous targets, you must use a different strategy: either plain k-Fold, or K-Fold with target binning (e.g., `StratifiedKFold` after `KBinsDiscretizer`). Writing "use StratifiedKFold for regression" without this caveat will be marked **incorrect** in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the KTU exam hall? Read this section.**

- [ ] **Cross-Validation (CV)** is a resampling technique to estimate **generalization error** when a separate test set is unavailable or insufficient.
- [ ] **k-Fold CV** splits data into $k$ folds; trains on $k-1$, validates on 1; repeats $k$ times; reports the **mean** and **standard deviation** of the $k$ scores.
- [ ] **Hold-Out** is a single split (e.g., 70/30) — fast but high variance.
- [ ] **LOOCV** is $k = n$ — minimum bias but maximum computational cost and high variance.
- [ ] **Stratified k-Fold** preserves the **class distribution** of the original dataset in every fold; mandatory for **imbalanced classification**.
- [ ] **Bias decreases** as $k$ increases; **variance generally decreases** as $k$ decreases (with the LOOCV exception due to high fold overlap).
- [ ] **Sweet spot:** $k = 5$ or $k = 10$ is the **industry default** (Kohavi, 1995).
- [ ] **Time Series CV** uses an *expanding* (or rolling) window — **never** a random shuffle, which leaks future information.
- [ ] **Group k-Fold** prevents leakage when one "subject" contributes multiple correlated samples (e.g., multiple images from the same patient).
- [ ] **Nested CV** = inner loop for hyperparameter tuning + outer loop for unbiased evaluation — used in research and AutoML pipelines.
- [ ] The **standard deviation of CV scores** ($\sigma_{CV}$) indicates **model stability** — report it alongside the mean.
- [ ] **Two formulas to memorize for KTU exams:**
  1. Mean CV score: $\hat{E}_{CV} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} s_i$
  2. Approximate bias: $\text{Bias}_k = \frac{c}{n(k-1)}$

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" CV methods in a KTU long-answer, always structure your answer as a **table with 3–4 explicit criteria** (bias, variance, cost, data use). Examiners allocate **at least 1 mark per row of correct comparison** — a clean table is worth 4 marks by itself.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hyperparameter Tuning: Grid Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Hyperparameter?

In Machine Learning, every model is governed by two distinct types of configuration values:

- **Parameters (Model Parameters):** Values that are *learned automatically* from the training data during the learning process. Example: the weights $w$ and bias $b$ in linear regression, learned by minimizing a loss function.
- **Hyperparameters (Model Configuration):** Values that are *set manually by the practitioner* **before** training begins. They control the **learning process itself** or the **structural capacity** of the model. The model never learns them from data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Hyperparameter)**
> A hyperparameter is a configuration variable external to the model whose value cannot be estimated from the training data and must be set prior to the training process to govern the algorithm's behaviour, complexity, and generalization capability.

Common examples across KTU-syllabus models:

| Algorithm | Key Hyperparameters |
|---|---|
| K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) | $K$ (number of neighbours), distance metric |
| Decision Tree | max depth, min samples split, criterion |
| Support Vector Machine (SVM) | $C$ (regularization), kernel type, $\gamma$ |
| Random Forest | $n\_estimators$, max features, max depth |
| Gradient Boosting | learning rate $\eta$, $n\_estimators$, max depth |

## 1.2 What is Hyperparameter Tuning?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hyperparameter Tuning (Definition)**
> Hyperparameter tuning (also called *hyperparameter optimization* or *model selection*) is the systematic process of searching for the optimal combination of hyperparameter values that yields the **best generalization performance** (typically measured by validation accuracy, F1-score, or lowest error) on unseen data.

## 1.3 What is Grid Search?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Grid Search (Definition)**
> Grid Search is an exhaustive, brute-force hyperparameter tuning technique that enumerates **every possible combination** of hyperparameter values from a pre-defined discrete search space (the "grid") and evaluates model performance for each combination, finally selecting the combination that produces the best validation score.

Mathematically, if the search space contains hyperparameters $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ and each $\theta_i$ takes a finite set of candidate values $V_i = \{v_{i,1}, v_{i,2}, \dots, v_{i, k_i}\}$, then Grid Search evaluates the Cartesian product:

$$
\mathcal{G} = V_1 \times V_2 \times \dots \times V_n
$$

and returns $\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta \in \mathcal{G}} \; \text{Score}(\theta)$.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Restaurant Menu"

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and want to order the perfect meal. The chef tells you:

- You must pick **exactly one** item from column A (Starters): Soup, Salad, Spring Roll (3 options)
- You must pick **exactly one** item from column B (Main Course): Biryani, Pasta, Noodles, Burger (4 options)
- You must pick **exactly one** item from column C (Dessert): Ice Cream, Cake (2 options)

**Grid Search** is like the most thorough diner in the world — they will try **every single combination** of (Starter × Main × Dessert) = $3 \times 4 \times 2 = 24$ possible meals, score each, and finally announce: *"The best meal is Biryani + Soup + Ice Cream with a score of 9/10!"*

They do **not** skip any combination, do **not** guess, and do **not** assume. They systematically cover the entire "grid" of possibilities. The drawback? If you add 3 more desserts, the total jumps to $3 \times 4 \times 5 = 60$ — and you have to taste **all 60 meals**.

## 1.5 Why KTU Emphasizes Grid Search

- It is the **most fundamental** and **most easily understood** hyperparameter search method.
- It is **deterministic and reproducible** — same grid, same result.
- It guarantees finding the best combination **within the specified search space**.
- It serves as the **baseline** against which more advanced methods (Random Search, Bayesian Optimization) are compared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEPET413 Module 4):** The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists *Grid Search* as a core topic under Model Selection & Evaluation, making it a frequent 3-mark and 14-mark examination item.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — How Grid Search Works

Grid Search follows a strictly defined, sequential pipeline. Every step is essential for the procedure to be statistically valid.

**Step 1 — Define the Hyperparameter Search Space**
The practitioner specifies the discrete candidate values for each hyperparameter. For example, for an SVM:

$$
C \in \{0.1, 1, 10\}, \quad \gamma \in \{0.01, 0.1, 1\}
$$

This forms a 2-dimensional grid of $3 \times 3 = 9$ cells.

**Step 2 — Enumerate the Cartesian Product**
The algorithm systematically generates every combination:

$$
\mathcal{G} = \{(0.1, 0.01), (0.1, 0.1), (0.1, 1), (1, 0.01), \dots, (10, 1)\}
$$

Total number of combinations:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert
$$

where $\vert V_i \vert$ is the cardinality of the candidate set for hyperparameter $i$.

**Step 3 — For Each Combination, Train and Evaluate**
For each point $\theta_j \in \mathcal{G}$, train the model on the training fold and evaluate on the validation fold. Usually, $k$-fold cross-validation is applied, multiplying the number of fits by $k$.

**Step 4 — Aggregate Cross-Validation Scores**
For each combination, compute the mean cross-validation score across the $k$ folds:

$$
\overline{S}(\theta_j) = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{f=1}^{k} S_{f}(\theta_j)
$$

**Step 5 — Select the Best Combination**
Return the configuration with the highest mean (or lowest loss, depending on the metric):

$$
\theta^* = \arg\max_{j \in \{1, \dots, N_{\text{comb}}\}} \;\overline{S}(\theta_j)
$$

**Step 6 — Retrain on Full Training Set (Optional but Standard)**
The winning combination $\theta^*$ is retrained on the *entire* training data (no folds), producing the final production model.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Total Grid Combinations | $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert$ | Number of points in the grid |
| Total Model Fits (with k-fold CV) | $N_{\text{fit}} = k \cdot N_{\text{comb}}$ | Total training runs required |
| Mean CV Score | $\overline{S}(\theta) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{f=1}^{k} S_f(\theta)$ | Average validation score across folds |
| Best Hyperparameter Set | $\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta \in \mathcal{G}} \overline{S}(\theta)$ | Selection rule |
| Total Wall-Clock Cost | $T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{fit}} \cdot t_{\text{fit}}$ | Where $t_{\text{fit}}$ is avg time per fit |
| Exhaustive Search Guarantee | $\theta^* \in \mathcal{G} \Rightarrow \text{guaranteed optimum in space}$ | Determinism property |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When the question asks "How many models will be trained?", always compute $N_{\text{fit}} = k \times N_{\text{comb}}$. A common KTU pitfall is forgetting the factor of $k$ for cross-validation.

## 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why" Behind the Pipeline

- **Discrete grid (Step 1):** Grid Search *cannot* handle continuous ranges — it requires an enumerated set. The user is responsible for choosing meaningful values (often on a log-scale for regularization parameters).
- **Cartesian product (Step 2):** Exhaustive enumeration ensures no combination is missed. This is Grid Search's *core strength* and *core weakness* simultaneously.
- **Cross-validation (Steps 3–4):** A single train/validation split is noisy and dataset-dependent. K-fold CV averages out random fluctuations and provides a stable, low-variance estimate of true performance.
- **Selection (Step 5):** Uses the *mean* CV score as the criterion. Some implementations also use standard deviation to assess stability.
- **Retraining (Step 6):** Once the best hyperparameters are identified, the model is re-trained on the maximum amount of data available, improving final performance.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Grid Search is the industry workhorse in many applied scenarios:

- **Medical diagnosis systems** where regulators demand *reproducible* and *auditable* model selection procedures.
- **Credit scoring and financial risk models** where hyperparameter choices must be defensible and documented.
- **Edge/embedded ML deployment** where the final model is small and search-time computation is acceptable.
- **Academic benchmarking** where reproducibility is paramount.

In the era of deep learning, where search spaces can be astronomically large, Grid Search is often replaced by Random Search or Bayesian Optimization — but for classical ML (SVM, Decision Trees, KNN), Grid Search remains the gold standard.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Exhaustive Computational Analysis

This section presents a complete, KTU-examination-style worked example demonstrating the full Grid Search procedure from first principles.

### Problem Setup

We are tuning a **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** classifier with an **RBF kernel** on a binary classification dataset. The hyperparameters to tune are:

$$
C \in \{0.1, 1, 10\}, \quad \gamma \in \{0.01, 0.1, 1\}
$$

We use **5-fold stratified cross-validation** and the evaluation metric is **accuracy**.

**Task:** Compute the total number of model fits, and determine the best hyperparameter combination given the (hypothetical) CV scores below.

### Step 3.1.1 — Compute the Number of Grid Combinations

We apply the formula $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert$:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} = \vert V_C \vert \times \vert V_\gamma \vert
$$

Counting the candidate values: $\vert V_C \vert = 3$ and $\vert V_\gamma \vert = 3$.

Substituting:

$$
N_{\text{comb}} = 3 \times 3 = 9
$$

So the grid contains **9 distinct hyperparameter combinations**.

### Step 3.1.2 — Compute the Total Number of Model Fits

Using $N_{\text{fit}} = k \cdot N_{\text{comb}}$ with $k = 5$:

$$
N_{\text{fit}} = 5 \times 9 = 45
$$

Therefore, **45 SVM models must be trained** in total. This is a direct consequence of the cross-validation requirement: each of the 9 grid points is evaluated across all 5 folds.

### Step 3.1.3 — Enumerate the 9 Combinations Explicitly

The full Cartesian product is:

$$
\mathcal{G} = \left\{
\begin{aligned}
&(0.1, 0.01),\ (0.1, 0.1),\ (0.1, 1), \\
&(1, 0.01),\ (1, 0.1),\ (1, 1), \\
&(10, 0.01),\ (10, 0.1),\ (10, 1)
\end{aligned}
\right\}
$$

### Step 3.1.4 — Hypothetical Cross-Validation Accuracy Matrix

The following matrix shows the accuracy of the SVM on each fold for every grid point. **Fold 5 accuracy** is given; the rest are abbreviated for clarity. (In an exam, the student will be given the full matrix.)

| $\gamma \backslash C$ | $C = 0.1$ | $C = 1$ | $C = 10$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\gamma = 0.01$ | 0.72 | 0.81 | 0.83 |
| $\gamma = 0.1$  | 0.78 | 0.88 | 0.85 |
| $\gamma = 1$    | 0.65 | 0.74 | 0.70 |

(For brevity, these are treated as the **mean CV accuracies** $\overline{S}(\theta_j)$ directly.)

### Step 3.1.5 — Find the Best Combination

We apply the selection rule $\theta^* = \arg\max \overline{S}(\theta)$:

Scanning the matrix, the maximum value is **0.88**, occurring at the cell $(C, \gamma) = (1, 0.1)$.

Therefore:

$$
\theta^* = (C^*, \gamma^*) = (1, 0.1)
$$

with a mean CV accuracy of $\overline{S}(\theta^*) = 0.88$.

### Step 3.1.6 — Total Wall-Clock Time Estimate (Optional Engineering Calculation)

Suppose each SVM fit takes $t_{\text{fit}} = 12$ seconds on the available hardware. Total tuning time:

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{fit}} \times t_{\text{fit}} = 45 \times 12 = 540 \text{ seconds} = 9 \text{ minutes}
$$

This is a small computational cost, but if the search space were expanded to, say, 5 values per hyperparameter and 10 hyperparameters with 5-fold CV:

$$
N_{\text{fit}} = 5 \times 5^{10} = 5 \times 9{,}765{,}625 = 48{,}828{,}125 \text{ fits}
$$

which becomes prohibitively expensive — illustrating the **Curse of Dimensionality** in Grid Search.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Stepwise Summary of the Procedure

| Step | Mathematical Operation | Result / Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Enumerate candidate values per hyperparameter | $V_1, V_2, \dots, V_n$ |
| 2 | Compute Cartesian product cardinality | $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_i \vert V_i \vert$ |
| 3 | Train + validate on $k$ folds for each combination | $k \times N_{\text{comb}}$ fits |
| 4 | Average fold scores | $\overline{S}(\theta_j) = \frac{1}{k}\sum_f S_f(\theta_j)$ |
| 5 | Select argmax of mean scores | $\theta^* = \arg\max \overline{S}(\theta)$ |
| 6 | Refit on full training set | Final model $M_{\text{final}}$ |

## 3.3 Key Mathematical Properties

### 3.3.1 Determinism

For a fixed search space and fixed data, Grid Search is **fully deterministic**. Two runs will always produce the same $\theta^*$. This property is critical for KTU exam questions emphasizing *reproducibility*.

### 3.3.2 Local vs Global Optimum

Grid Search guarantees the **global optimum within the discrete grid**, but **not** within the continuous hyperparameter space. If the true optimum lies between two grid points (e.g., $C^* = 3.7$), Grid Search will miss it. This is a frequent KTU question on limitations.

### 3.3.3 Computational Complexity

The time complexity of Grid Search is:

$$
T_{\text{Grid}} = \mathcal{O}\!\left( k \cdot \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert \cdot T_{\text{train}} \right)
$$

where $T_{\text{train}}$ is the per-fit training cost. This **exponential growth** in $n$ is why Grid Search is impractical for high-dimensional spaces (e.g., deep neural networks with 10+ hyperparameters).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Grid Search Functional Architecture

The diagram below illustrates the complete functional flow of the Grid Search procedure, from search-space definition to final model retraining.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Defines Hyperparameter Grid] --> B[Enumerate Cartesian Product of Values]
    B --> C{For Each Combination theta in G}
    C --> D[Split Data into K Folds]
    D --> E[Train Model on K-1 Folds]
    E --> F[Validate on Held-out Fold]
    F --> G[Store Fold Score S_f]
    G --> H{All K Folds Done?}
    H -- No --> E
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute Mean CV Score S_bar]
    I --> J[Record Score for Combination]
    J --> K{All Combinations Evaluated?}
    K -- No --> C
    K -- Yes --> L[Identify theta_star via argmax]
    L --> M[Retrain Final Model on Full Training Data]
    M --> N[Deploy / Report Best Hyperparameters]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Search Space Explosion Visualization

The diagram below shows how the grid size scales combinatorially with the number of hyperparameters — a key concept for KTU numerical questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Hyperparameter 1: 3 values] --> M1[Stage 1: 3 cells]
    P2[Hyperparameter 2: 4 values] --> M2[Stage 2: 12 cells]
    P3[Hyperparameter 3: 5 values] --> M3[Stage 3: 60 cells]
    P4[Hyperparameter 4: 2 values] --> M4[Stage 4: 120 cells]
    M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4
    M4 --> Out[Total Grid Size: 120 combinations]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Comparison Block — Grid Search vs Other Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Hyperparameter Tuning Methods]
    Root --> GS[Grid Search: Exhaustive, Deterministic, Slow for Large Spaces]
    Root --> RS[Random Search: Random Sampling, Faster, May Miss Optimum]
    Root --> BO[Bayesian Optimization: Probabilistic Model, Smart, Complex]
    Root --> HO[Heuristic Methods: GA, PSO, Population-based Search]
```

## 4.4 KTU Schematic — K-Fold Cross-Validation Coupled with Grid Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Data[Full Training Dataset] --> Split[Partition into K Folds F1 to FK]
    Split --> Fold1[Fold 1 Held Out]
    Split --> Fold2[Fold 2 Held Out]
    Split --> FoldK[Fold K Held Out]
    Fold1 --> Train1[Train on F2 to FK]
    Fold2 --> Train2[Train on F1, F3 to FK]
    FoldK --> TrainK[Train on F1 to F minus 1]
    Train1 --> Score1[Compute S1]
    Train2 --> Score2[Compute S2]
    TrainK --> ScoreK[Compute SK]
    Score1 --> Mean[Mean Score S_bar]
    Score2 --> Mean
    ScoreK --> Mean
    Mean --> Best[Select Best theta_star]
```
