<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between model parameters and hyperparameters with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Model Parameters | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|
| Who sets them? | Learned automatically by the algorithm during training | Set manually by the user before training |
| Optimization method | Gradient descent, MLE, closed-form solutions | Grid Search, Random Search, Bayesian Optimization |
| Dependence on data | Value depends on training data | Value is independent of training data |
| Example 1 | Weights $w$ in linear regression | Regularization constant $\lambda$ |
| Example 2 | Split thresholds in decision tree learning | Maximum tree depth |

*Mark allocation: 1 mark for the correct distinction table, 1 mark for examples of parameters, 1 mark for examples of hyperparameters.*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three disadvantages of the Grid Search method for hyperparameter tuning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Curse of Dimensionality:** The number of combinations grows multiplicatively with the number of hyperparameters. With $n$ hyperparameters each having $k$ values, the grid contains $k^n$ combinations — quickly becoming computationally infeasible.
2. **Discrete Resolution:** The search is restricted to a pre-defined grid. The true continuous optimum may lie between grid points and will be missed.
3. **Wasted Computation on Unimportant Dimensions:** Grid Search allocates equal budget to every hyperparameter, even those that have little effect on performance — leading to inefficient use of compute.
4. **No Parallel Information Sharing:** Each combination is evaluated independently; no information from previous trials guides the next trial (unlike Bayesian Optimization).

*Mark allocation: 1 mark per disadvantage, max 3.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B — Choice A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Past Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the Grid Search algorithm for hyperparameter tuning in detail. Clearly state the formula for the total number of combinations and describe the role of cross-validation in the procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an SVM classifier with hyperparameters $C \in \{0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100\}$ and $\gamma \in \{0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 1\}$. Using 10-fold cross-validation, compute:
- (i) The total number of grid combinations.
- (ii) The total number of SVM models to be trained.
- (iii) If the best mean CV accuracy is 0.94 at $(C, \gamma) = (1, 0.01)$, explain the next step to obtain the final production model. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Choice A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Detailed Answer

**Definition (2 marks):**
Grid Search is an exhaustive hyperparameter optimization technique that systematically evaluates model performance for **every combination** of hyperparameter values from a pre-defined discrete search space, and returns the combination that yields the best validation score.

**Combination Formula (2 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert
$$

where $n$ is the number of hyperparameters and $\vert V_i \vert$ is the number of candidate values for the $i$-th hyperparameter.

**Role of Cross-Validation (2 marks):**
Single train-test splits produce noisy performance estimates that depend heavily on the random split. $k$-fold cross-validation partitions the data into $k$ folds; for each combination, the model is trained $k$ times (each time leaving out one fold for validation), and the mean of the $k$ validation scores is used as the stable performance estimate. This reduces variance and produces a more reliable basis for hyperparameter selection.

**Steps of the Procedure (1 mark):**
Define grid → Enumerate combinations → $k$-fold CV for each → Select best → Retrain on full data.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution

**(i) Total Grid Combinations (2 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{comb}} = \vert V_C \vert \times \vert V_\gamma \vert = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ combinations}
$$

**(ii) Total Models Trained (2 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{fit}} = k \times N_{\text{comb}} = 10 \times 20 = 200 \text{ models}
$$

**(iii) Final Production Model (3 marks):**
The next step is to **retrain the SVM** using the winning hyperparameter combination $(C^*, \gamma^*) = (1, 0.01)$ on the **entire training dataset** (combining all 10 folds into one full set). No further cross-validation is performed. This final retrained model is then used for prediction on the held-out test set or deployed to production. The rationale is that training on more data (all of it) typically yields a slightly better-performing model than any of the 10 individual fold-trained models.

---

### Question B — Choice B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Past Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Discuss three advantages and three disadvantages of the Grid Search method. Compare it briefly with Random Search. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Random Forest classifier is tuned with three hyperparameters:
- $n\_estimators \in \{50, 100, 200\}$
- $max\_depth \in \{3, 5, 10, \text{None}\}$
- $min\_samples\_split \in \{2, 5, 10\}$

If 5-fold cross-validation is used, compute the total combinations, total fits, and total tuning time if each fit takes 4.5 seconds. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Choice B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Comparison

**Advantages (3 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Exhaustiveness:** Every combination in the search space is evaluated — no combination is skipped or sampled probabilistically.
2. **Determinism & Reproducibility:** Given the same grid and data, results are identical across runs — essential for academic and regulatory settings.
3. **Simplicity:** Conceptually straightforward; easy to implement, explain, and audit. No probabilistic surrogate models are required.

**Disadvantages (3 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Exponential Scaling:** Computational cost explodes with the number of hyperparameters. With 10 hyperparameters and 5 values each, the grid contains $5^{10} \approx 9.77$ million combinations.
2. **Discrete Resolution:** Cannot find optima between grid points.
3. **Inefficient Allocation:** Spends equal effort on important and unimportant hyperparameters.

**Comparison with Random Search (1 mark):**
Random Search samples combinations randomly from the search space (with a fixed budget $N$). It is more efficient in high-dimensional spaces because it explores a *broader range of values* per dimension in the same compute budget. However, unlike Grid Search, it provides **no guarantee** of finding the global optimum within the grid.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Total Grid Combinations (2 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{comb}} = 3 \times 4 \times 3 = 36 \text{ combinations}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Model Fits (2 marks):**

$$
N_{\text{fit}} = k \times N_{\text{comb}} = 5 \times 36 = 180 \text{ fits}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Tuning Time (3 marks):**

$$
T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{fit}} \times t_{\text{fit}} = 180 \times 4.5 \text{ seconds}
$$

$$
T_{\text{total}} = 810 \text{ seconds} = 13.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $k$ factor:** Many students compute only $N_{\text{comb}} = 36$ and forget that with 5-fold CV, the actual number of model trainings is $5 \times 36 = 180$. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss.
> 2. **Mixing up hyperparameters with parameters:** When the question says "model parameters", do **not** list $C$ or $K$ — these are hyperparameters. Parameters are *learned* (weights, splits, support vectors).
> 3. **Writing $5^{10}$ for complexity but misinterpreting it:** Make it explicit that the exponent is the number of hyperparameters, not the number of values.
> 4. **Ignoring the curse of dimensionality in disadvantages:** This is a *high-frequency* KTU answer point — always mention it.
> 5. **Concluding Grid Search is "bad":** It is not. It is the gold standard for low-dimensional classical ML. Frame it as a *trade-off*, not a flaw.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Hyperparameter Tuning: Grid Search**

- **Hyperparameter** = set manually before training. **Parameter** = learned from data.
- **Grid Search** = exhaustive enumeration of all combinations in a discrete search space.
- **Combination formula:** $N_{\text{comb}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \vert V_i \vert$.
- **With k-fold CV:** $N_{\text{fit}} = k \cdot N_{\text{comb}}$.
- **Selection rule:** $\theta^* = \arg\max_{\theta \in \mathcal{G}} \overline{S}(\theta)$.
- **Final step:** Retrain $\theta^*$ on the **full training set**.
- **Strengths:** exhaustive, deterministic, reproducible, simple.
- **Weaknesses:** exponential scaling (curse of dimensionality), discrete resolution, equal-budget allocation.
- **Determinism property:** same grid + same data → same result, every time.
- **Global vs. local optimum:** Grid Search finds the *global optimum within the discrete grid*, not within the continuous space.
- **Total time formula:** $T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{fit}} \times t_{\text{fit}}$.
- **Common KTU trick question:** "How many models are trained?" — always multiply by $k$ for cross-validation.
- **Engineering utility:** classical ML pipelines, regulated industries, small-to-medium search spaces, reproducibility-critical applications.
- **Sibling methods (for comparison only):** Random Search (sampling-based), Bayesian Optimization (probabilistic surrogate), Heuristic Search (GA, PSO).

> **End of Notes — Module 4: Hyperparameter Tuning (Grid Search) | PEPET413 — Machine Learning | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Search
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Random Search

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Random Search** is a baseline hyperparameter optimization (HPO) algorithm in which hyperparameter combinations are sampled **uniformly at random** from a pre-defined search space $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^{d}$ (or a mixed discrete-continuous space), and the best-performing configuration with respect to a chosen validation metric is returned.

In the context of **KTU Module 4 (Model Selection & Hyperparameter Tuning)**, Random Search is treated as a probabilistic alternative to *Grid Search*. It is described in the seminal work of Bergstra & Bengio (2012, *Journal of Machine Learning Research*) and is a default technique in libraries such as **Scikit-Learn** (`RandomizedSearchCV`) and **Keras Tuner**.

Mathematically, given a search space $\mathcal{X}$ and a budget of $n$ trials, Random Search selects:

$$
\mathbf{x}_{i} \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{X}), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n
$$

where $\mathcal{U}(\mathcal{X})$ denotes a uniform (or specified) probability distribution over the search space. Each $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_i^{(1)}, x_i^{(2)}, \dots, x_i^{(d)})$ is a $d$-dimensional hyperparameter vector.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Treasure Hunt":** Imagine you are searching a vast desert for a hidden treasure. The desert is the *hyperparameter space*, and every spot you dig is an *evaluation*.
>
> - **Grid Search** is like dividing the desert into equal squares and digging one hole in the center of each square. If the treasure is in the *same square* as another dug hole, you miss it.
> - **Random Search** is like randomly throwing $n$ darts at the desert and digging wherever each dart lands. Because the darts are spread out, you have a *better chance* of landing near the treasure — even though you have the same number of holes.

The key insight: when only a **few hyperparameters actually matter** (the *effective dimension* of the search space is small), random search finds good configurations **more efficiently** than grid search, which wastes evaluations on unimportant dimensions.

---

### 1.3 Why Random Search? — The "Curse of Dimensionality" Perspective

In Grid Search with $k$ values per dimension and $d$ dimensions, the number of evaluations grows as $k^d$ (exponential). Random Search decouples the *number of trials* $n$ from $d$, allowing **fixed-budget** optimization.

| Property | Grid Search | Random Search |
|---|---|---|
| Sampling Method | Deterministic (all combinations) | Stochastic (uniform / specified) |
| Budget Scaling | $k^d$ (exponential) | $n$ (linear) |
| Redundancy on Irrelevant Dimensions | **High** | **Low** |
| Parallelism | Limited (independent jobs) | **High** (trivially parallel) |
| Reproducibility | **Exact** | Seed-controlled |
| Best For | Low-dimensional, exhaustive coverage | High-dimensional, budget-constrained |

---

### 1.4 Key Terminology

- **Search Space $\mathcal{X}$:** The bounded domain from which hyperparameters are sampled (e.g., learning rate $\in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$).
- **Trial / Configuration:** A single sampled vector $\mathbf{x}_i$.
- **Objective Function $f(\mathbf{x})$:** The validation score (e.g., cross-validation accuracy or negative loss) to be maximized/minimized.
- **Budget $n$:** Total number of trials allowed.
- **Best Configuration $\mathbf{x}^*$:** $\arg\max_{i} f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ or $\arg\min_{i} f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ depending on objective.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Random Search can be broken down into **6 logical steps** as required for KTU 14-mark derivations.

1. **Define the Search Space $\mathcal{X}$:** Specify the domain (and distribution) for each hyperparameter. For example:
   - Learning rate: $\alpha \sim \text{LogUniform}(10^{-5}, 10^{-1})$
   - Number of layers: $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ (categorical)
   - Regularization: $\lambda \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 1)$

2. **Set the Budget $n$:** Determine the number of random configurations to evaluate. This is a *user-defined* parameter.

3. **Sample $n$ Configurations:** Draw $n$ independent samples $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \mathbf{x}_2, \dots, \mathbf{x}_n\}$ from $\mathcal{X}$. The most common distribution is **uniform**, but any samplable distribution is allowed (log-uniform is preferred for learning rates).

4. **Train and Evaluate:** For each $\mathbf{x}_i$, train the model and compute the validation score $f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ using $k$-fold cross-validation or a held-out validation set.

5. **Track the Best:** Maintain the running maximum (or minimum):
$$
\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{*} = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le n} f(\mathbf{x}_i)
$$

6. **Return the Best Configuration:** $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{*}$ becomes the chosen hyperparameter setting for the final model.

---

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Random Search — Effective Dimensionality

> [!NOTE]
> **Bergstra & Bengio (2012) — The Theoretical Justification:**

Suppose a model's performance depends strongly on **only 2 out of 5** hyperparameters. With Grid Search allocating 4 values per dimension, you get $4^5 = 1024$ trials, but **only $4^2 = 16$** distinct values are tried on the important dimensions — and the other 3 dimensions are merely repeated 16 times.

With Random Search, the $n$ samples are spread across **all 5 dimensions** independently, so each important dimension is explored by **all $n$ trials**. The redundant dimensions add noise but do not consume the budget.

Formally, if only $d_e \le d$ dimensions are *effective*, then Random Search explores $n$ distinct values per effective dimension, while Grid Search explores only $n^{d_e/d}$ — an exponential loss.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Equation / Expression | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathcal{X}$ | Search space | $\mathcal{X} = \bigotimes_{j=1}^{d} \mathcal{X}_j$ | Cartesian product of per-dim spaces |
| $n$ | Number of trials (budget) | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}$ | User-defined, e.g., $n = 50$ |
| $d$ | Total dimensionality | $d = \vert \mathcal{X} \vert$ dimensions | Hyperparameters count |
| $d_e$ | Effective dimensionality | $d_e \le d$ | Hyperparams that actually matter |
| $f(\mathbf{x})$ | Objective (validation score) | $f: \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ | Maximize accuracy / minimize loss |
| $\mathbf{x}_i$ | $i$-th sampled config | $\mathbf{x}_i \sim \mathcal{U}(\mathcal{X})$ | IID samples |
| $\mathbf{x}^*$ | True optimum | $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\max f(\mathbf{x})$ | Unknown in practice |
| $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^*$ | Estimated best | $\arg\max_{1 \le i \le n} f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ | Returned by algorithm |
| $P(\text{cover})$ | Prob. of covering $\varepsilon$-ball | $\left(1 - \frac{\vert B_\varepsilon \vert}{\vert \mathcal{X} \vert}\right)^{n}$ | Geometric decay |
| $E[T_{\varepsilon}]$ | Expected trials to $\varepsilon$-optimum | $\approx \frac{\log \delta}{\log(1 - p_\varepsilon)}$ | Prob. $1-\delta$ of success |

> **Notation Note:** We use `\vert` and `\mid` for absolute-value/cardinality bars to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Deep Learning:** Tuning learning rate, batch size, dropout, weight decay (e.g., in CNNs, Transformers, RNNs).
- **Support Vector Machines:** Searching over kernel type, $C$, and $\gamma$.
- **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost / LightGBM):** Tuning `max_depth`, `learning_rate`, `n_estimators`, `subsample`.
- **AutoML Pipelines:** Used as a *baseline* against Bayesian Optimization, Hyperband, and Population-Based Training.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Random Search is the **industry-standard baseline** for HPO. Any new HPO method (e.g., Bayesian Optimization) must *beat Random Search* to justify its computational overhead. This is a frequent interview/test question.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coverage Probability After $n$ Trials

> **Problem (KTU-style):** *In a 2D search space $\mathcal{X} = [0,1]^2$, we define the "target" as an $\varepsilon$-ball of radius $\varepsilon$ around the true optimum. Derive the probability that at least one of $n$ random samples falls inside this target.*

---

**Step 1 — Identify the Target Area**

The $\varepsilon$-ball of radius $\varepsilon$ in a 2D unit square has area:
$$
A_{\text{target}} = \pi \varepsilon^2
$$

(We assume $\varepsilon$ small enough that the ball is fully contained in $[0,1]^2$, i.e., $\varepsilon \le 0.5$.)

**Step 2 — Probability of a Single Sample Missing the Target**

Since the sample is drawn uniformly from $\mathcal{X} = [0,1]^2$ (total area = 1), the probability that *one* sample does **not** fall in the target is:
$$
p_{\text{miss}} = 1 - A_{\text{target}} = 1 - \pi \varepsilon^2
$$

**Step 3 — Probability That All $n$ Samples Miss**

Because samples are drawn **independently and identically distributed (IID)**, the joint probability is the product:
$$
P(\text{all miss}) = \left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^2\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 4 — Probability That At Least One Sample Covers the Target**

Using the complement rule:
$$
P(\text{at least one hit}) = 1 - \left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^2\right)^{n}
$$

**Final Result (KTU High-Yield Formula):**

$$
\boxed{P(\text{cover in } n \text{ trials}) \;=\; 1 - \left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^{2}\right)^{n}}
$$

**Interpretation:** As $n \to \infty$, $P \to 1$ (consistent estimator). For finite $n$, the coverage is sub-linear in $n$ and depends on the *target volume* $\pi \varepsilon^2$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expected Number of Trials to Reach $\varepsilon$-Ball

> **Problem:** *How many trials $n$ are required, on average, to guarantee a hit inside the $\varepsilon$-ball with probability at least $1 - \delta$?*

---

**Step 1 — Set Up the Inequation**

We require:
$$
1 - \left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^{2}\right)^{n} \;\ge\; 1 - \delta
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify Using Complement**

$$
\left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^{2}\right)^{n} \;\le\; \delta
$$

**Step 3 — Take Natural Logarithm on Both Sides**

Note: $\ln(1 - x) \le -x$ for $0 \le x < 1$, so:

$$
n \cdot \ln\!\left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^{2}\right) \;\le\; \ln \delta
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $n$**

Since $\ln(1 - \pi \varepsilon^2) < 0$, dividing flips the inequality:
$$
n \;\ge\; \frac{\ln \delta}{\ln\!\left(1 - \pi \varepsilon^{2}\right)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Lower Bound $\ln(1-x) \ge -x/(1-x)$ (or use the simpler $-x$ bound for small $\varepsilon$):**

$$
n \;\ge\; \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{\pi \varepsilon^{2}}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,n \;\ge\; \frac{\ln(1/\delta)}{\pi \varepsilon^{2}}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The required budget is *inverse-quadratic* in $\varepsilon$ and *logarithmic* in $1/\delta$. To halve $\varepsilon$, you need **4× more trials**; to improve confidence from 95% to 99%, you only need $\ln(100/95) / \ln(1/95) \approx 1.13 \times$ more trials.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Random Search vs. Grid Search on a Toy Problem

> **Setup:** $d = 3$ hyperparameters, $k = 4$ values per dimension. Only $d_e = 1$ dimension affects performance.

**Grid Search:**
- Total trials: $k^d = 4^3 = 64$.
- Distinct values on the effective dimension: $k = 4$.
- Trials per effective value: $k^{d-1} = 16$ (lots of redundancy).

**Random Search with $n = 64$ trials:**
- Samples are spread uniformly across all 3 dimensions.
- Each effective-dimension value appears with frequency $\approx 64/k = 16$ but the *sampling diversity* on the other 2 dimensions is far higher than grid search (16 unique combos per effective value instead of just 1).

This example is the **core argument** of Bergstra & Bengio (2012) and is a frequent KTU 14-mark question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Search Strategies

| Criterion | Grid Search | Random Search | Bayesian Opt. |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling** | Deterministic Lattice | Uniform / Specified | Posterior-driven (GP / TPE) |
| **Best Use Case** | Low-dim, exhaustive | High-dim, budget-limited | Expensive evaluations |
| **Tuning Knobs** | Grid resolution | Budget $n$, distributions | Surrogate + acquisition |
| **Wall-clock Cost** | $k^d$ model trains | $n$ model trains | $n$ + surrogate overhead |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow (wasteful) | Moderate | Fast (sample-efficient) |
| **Reproducibility** | Exact (no seed needed) | Seed-dependent | Seed-dependent |
| **Parallelism** | Trivial (embarrassing) | Trivial | Limited (sequential by design) |
| **Handles Irrelevant Dims** | Poorly | Well | Depends on prior |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Students often confuse *Random Search* with *Stochastic Gradient Descent*. They are unrelated. Random Search is for **hyperparameters** (outer loop), SGD is for **model parameters** (inner loop).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Process Flowchart of Random Search

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Search Space X] --> B[Set Budget n]
    B --> C[Initialize Best Score = -infinity]
    C --> D[Sample x_i from X]
    D --> E[Train Model with x_i]
    E --> F[Compute Validation Score f x_i]
    F --> G{Score better than Best?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Update Best Score and x_star]
    G -- No --> I[Discard x_i]
    H --> J{All n trials done?}
    I --> J
    J -- No --> D
    J -- Yes --> K[Return Best Config x_star]
    K --> L[End]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Random Search System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        IN1[User-defined Search Space X]
        IN2[Budget n]
        IN3[Objective Function f]
    end

    subgraph SAMPLER[Random Sampler Module]
        S1[Distribution Handler: Uniform, Log-Uniform, Categorical]
        S2[PRNG with Fixed Seed]
    end

    subgraph EVAL[Evaluation Engine]
        E1[Model Trainer: K-Fold CV]
        E2[Score Aggregator: Mean, Std]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        O1[Best Config Tracker]
        O2[Logging and Visualization]
    end

    IN1 --> S1
    IN2 --> S1
    S2 --> S1
    S1 --> E1
    IN3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> O1
    E2 --> O2
    O1 --> R[Final Recommendation x_star]
```

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Comparison: Grid vs. Random Coverage in 2D

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GRID[Grid Search 2D]
        G1[9 Lattice Points: 3x3 Grid]
        G2[Each Row Has Identical x2 value]
    end

    subgraph RAND[Random Search 2D]
        R1[9 Random Points Scattered]
        R2[Each Point has Unique x1 AND x2]
    end

    G1 --> G2
    R1 --> R2
    G2 --> CMP[Random Search covers more unique x1 and x2 combinations]
    R2 --> CMP
```

---

### 4.4 State Machine: Trial Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Pending
    Pending --> Sampling: Draw x from X
    Sampling --> Training: Begin K-Fold CV
    Training --> Evaluating: Compute Mean Score
    Evaluating --> BestUpdated: Score > Best
    Evaluating --> Logged: Score <= Best
    BestUpdated --> Logged
    Logged --> Pending: i less than n
    Logged --> [*]: i equals n
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed (`stepA`, `evalB`, `inputC`, etc.) to comply with the KTU Mermaid Safeguards. No reserved keywords are used as standalone node names.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define Random Search for hyperparameter optimization. How does it differ from Grid Search in terms of the number of evaluations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> Random Search is a hyperparameter optimization technique in which $n$ configurations are sampled **independently and uniformly at random** from a pre-defined search space $\mathcal{X}$, and the configuration that yields the best validation score is selected.
>
> **Difference in evaluation count:**
> - **Grid Search:** $k^d$ evaluations (exponential in dimensions $d$).
> - **Random Search:** $n$ evaluations (linear, user-controlled).
>
> This decoupling makes Random Search **budget-friendly** and well-suited for high-dimensional spaces. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for grid count, 1 mark for random count and significance.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is meant by the "effective dimensionality" of a search space? Why is it relevant to Random Search?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **effective dimensionality** $d_e \le d$ is the number of hyperparameters that *actually influence* the model's performance. The remaining $d - d_e$ dimensions are *irrelevant* (or weakly relevant).
>
> **Relevance to Random Search:** When $d_e \ll d$, Random Search is far more efficient than Grid Search because its $n$ samples are spread across *all* dimensions independently. Each important dimension is explored by **all $n$ trials**, whereas Grid Search wastes trials on the irrelevant dimensions. *(3 marks — 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for relevance to Random Search.)*

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Random Search algorithm with a clear step-by-step procedure. Discuss its advantages over Grid Search with a suitable example where only 1 out of 4 hyperparameters is influential. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the probability that at least one out of $n$ random samples from a 2D unit-square search space falls inside an $\varepsilon$-ball of radius $\varepsilon$ centered at the optimum. Using this, find the number of trials $n$ required to achieve coverage probability at least $0.95$. Take $\varepsilon = 0.1$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Algorithm & Comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Search Space:** Specify domains for each of the 4 hyperparameters: e.g., $\alpha \in [10^{-4}, 10^{-1}]$, $\beta \in \{1,2,3,4\}$, $\gamma \in [0, 1]$, $\delta \in [0, 1]$.

**Step 2 — Set Budget:** Let $n = 50$ trials.

**Step 3 — Sample:** Draw $\mathbf{x}_i = (\alpha_i, \beta_i, \gamma_i, \delta_i)$ for $i = 1, \dots, 50$ uniformly at random.

**Step 4 — Evaluate:** For each $\mathbf{x}_i$, train model and compute validation score $f(\mathbf{x}_i)$.

**Step 5 — Track Best:** Maintain $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^{*} = \arg\max f(\mathbf{x}_i)$.

**Step 6 — Return Best.**

**Illustrative Example (1 influential hyperparameter):**
Suppose only $\alpha$ affects accuracy, and $\beta, \gamma, \delta$ are irrelevant.
- **Grid Search** with 4 values per dim: $4^4 = 256$ trials, but only 4 distinct $\alpha$-values are tested. Each $\alpha$ repeats 64 times. **Wasteful.**
- **Random Search** with 256 trials: all 256 trials explore *different* $\alpha$-values (sampled independently). **Highly efficient.** *(2 marks for example analysis.)*

---

**(b) Probability Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Area of $\varepsilon$-ball in 2D unit square: $A = \pi \varepsilon^2 = \pi (0.1)^2 = 0.01\pi \approx 0.0314$.

**Step 2:** Probability that a single sample hits the target:
$$
p = \pi \varepsilon^2 = 0.01\pi
$$

**Step 3:** Probability that at least one of $n$ IID samples hits:
$$
P(\text{hit}) = 1 - (1 - \pi \varepsilon^2)^{n} = 1 - (1 - 0.01\pi)^{n}
$$

**Step 4:** Set $P(\text{hit}) \ge 0.95$:
$$
(1 - 0.01\pi)^{n} \le 0.05
$$

**Step 5:** Take logarithm:
$$
n \cdot \ln(1 - 0.01\pi) \le \ln(0.05)
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $n$:
$$
n \ge \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(1 - 0.01\pi)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-0.03175} \approx 94.4
$$

**Step 7:** Round up: $n = 95$ trials.

> **Final Answer:** $\boxed{n = 95 \text{ trials to achieve } P(\text{cover}) \ge 0.95 \text{ with } \varepsilon = 0.1.}$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** Compare Grid Search, Random Search, and Bayesian Optimization across **at least 5 criteria**. State one practical scenario where each is the preferred choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine learning engineer is tuning 6 hyperparameters. Each has 5 candidate values. The training cost per trial is 2 hours. Calculate the total time taken by (i) exhaustive Grid Search, and (ii) Random Search with budget $n = 50$. Comment on the practical implications. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 marks — 1.4 marks per criterion):**

| Criterion | Grid Search | Random Search | Bayesian Opt. |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling** | Deterministic lattice | Uniform random | Posterior-guided (e.g., GP) |
| **Trial Count** | $k^d$ exponential | $n$ user-defined | $n$ + surrogate cost |
| **Best Scenario** | Low-dim exhaustive | High-dim, budget-limited | Expensive evaluations |
| **Handles Irrelevant Dims** | Poorly | Well | Depends on prior |
| **Parallelism** | Trivial | Trivial | Sequential bottleneck |
| **Reproducibility** | Exact | Seed-controlled | Seed-controlled |

- **Grid Search** preferred for: low-dimensional SVM tuning in academic studies.
- **Random Search** preferred for: deep learning hyperparameter sweeps (learning rate, dropout).
- **Bayesian Opt.** preferred for: AutoML where each model training takes hours/days.

---

**(b) Time Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $d = 6$ hyperparameters, $k = 5$ values each, training time per trial = 2 hours.

**(i) Grid Search:**
- Number of trials: $k^d = 5^6 = 15{,}625$.
- Total time: $15{,}625 \times 2 = 31{,}250$ hours $\approx$ **3.57 years**.

**(ii) Random Search:**
- Number of trials: $n = 50$.
- Total time: $50 \times 2 = 100$ hours $\approx$ **4.17 days**.

**Practical Implication:**
- Grid Search is **computationally infeasible** in this regime.
- Random Search is **312× faster** and provides comparable (often superior) results due to effective dimensionality.
- For deeper efficiency, Bayesian Optimization or Hyperband would be recommended. *(2 marks for implications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Random Search Questions**
> 1. **Confusing Sampling Distributions:** Students often claim Random Search samples "from a normal distribution" — this is **wrong by default**. It samples from a user-defined (usually uniform) distribution. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Skipping the Independence Assumption:** The coverage probability formula $1 - (1-p)^n$ **requires IID samples**. If samples are dependent (e.g., quasi-random / Latin Hypercube), the formula changes.
> 3. **Forgetting the Logarithm Direction:** When taking $\ln$ of an inequality, remember that $\ln(1 - x) < 0$ for $0 < x < 1$, so the inequality **flips** when dividing.
> 4. **Mixing Hyperparameter vs. Parameter:** Random Search is for hyperparameters (model architecture, learning rate), **not** model parameters (weights, biases).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Random Search**

- ✅ Random Search samples **IID** configurations from a pre-defined search space $\mathcal{X}$.
- ✅ Sample count $n$ is **decoupled from dimensionality** $d$ — this is its biggest advantage.
- ✅ Most common distribution: **uniform**; for learning rate, use **log-uniform** (sampling in $\log_{10}$ space).
- ✅ Coverage probability: $P(\text{hit in } n) = 1 - (1 - p)^n$ where $p$ is the single-trial hit probability.
- ✅ Budget to achieve $1 - \delta$ confidence: $n \ge \dfrac{\ln \delta}{\ln(1 - p)} = \dfrac{\ln(1/\delta)}{p}$ for small $p$.
- ✅ Outperforms Grid Search when **effective dimensionality** $d_e \ll d$ (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012).
- ✅ Trivially **parallelizable** (no sequential dependency between trials).
- ✅ Returns $\hat{\mathbf{x}}^* = \arg\max_{1 \le i \le n} f(\mathbf{x}_i)$ — a **consistent estimator** of the true optimum.
- ✅ Industry-standard **baseline** for HPO; any new HPO method must beat it.
- ✅ For 2D unit square, $p = \pi \varepsilon^2$ for an $\varepsilon$-ball target.
- ✅ For $d$-dim unit cube, $p = V_d \varepsilon^d$ where $V_d$ is the volume of a unit $d$-ball.
- ✅ **NOT** the same as Stochastic Gradient Descent — different loop, different purpose.
- ✅ Scikit-Learn implementation: `RandomizedSearchCV` with `n_iter` controlling $n$.
- ✅ KTU keyword triggers: *hyperparameter optimization*, *effective dimensionality*, *budget*, *sampling distribution*, *coverage probability*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Machine Learning Algorithms: Support Vector Machines (SVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Machine Learning Algorithms: Support Vector Machines (SVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a **supervised, discriminative, non-probabilistic binary classifier** (extendable to multi-class via one-vs-one / one-vs-all strategies) that constructs an **optimal separating hyperplane** in a feature space of dimension $d$ by **maximizing the geometric margin** between the two classes. The decision boundary is defined exclusively by a small subset of critical training points called **Support Vectors**, which lie closest to the hyperplane and satisfy the **Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) optimality conditions**.

Mathematically, an SVM learns a hypothesis of the form:

$$h_{\mathbf{w},b}(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)$$

where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is the weight (normal) vector and $b \in \mathbb{R}$ is the bias term. The **goal of training** is to find the *unique* $(\mathbf{w}, b)$ that solves the margin-maximization problem subject to correct classification of all training points.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Road Builder" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine you are a **road engineer** tasked with building the **widest possible road** between two neighbouring villages (Class +1 and Class −1). The road is the hyperplane; its **width** is the margin. You are constrained that **no house (training point) is allowed to be built on top of the road**. The clever insight: the road width is dictated *only* by the houses sitting closest to its edges — these edge houses are the **Support Vectors**. If you move a house that is far from the road, the road does not shift. But if you nudge one of those edge houses even slightly, the optimal road shifts too. This dependency on a *few critical points* is what makes SVM a **sparse, geometrically elegant** classifier.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook for KTU viva:** "SVM = Maximum Margin Classifier + Support Vectors + Kernels."

### 1.3 Why SVM? — Engineering Motivation

- **Robustness to outliers far from the boundary** (only support vectors matter).
- **Strong theoretical guarantees** via Statistical Learning Theory (VC dimension, structural risk minimization).
- **Handles non-linearity** elegantly through the *kernel trick* — no explicit feature mapping required.
- Performs well in **high-dimensional spaces** (e.g., text classification, bioinformatics), often superior to deep nets when data is limited.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Glossary (Board-Exam Ready)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}+b=0$ | Decision boundary in $\mathbb{R}^d$ |
| Margin | $\gamma$ | Perpendicular distance from hyperplane to nearest point |
| Support Vector | $\mathbf{x}_i$ | Training point with $\alpha_i > 0$; lies on the margin |
| Slack variable | $\xi_i$ | Permits margin violations in soft-margin SVM |
| Kernel function | $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j)$ | Implicit inner product in higher-dimensional space |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric & Functional Margin

For a training point $(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$:

**Functional margin** (not scale-invariant):

$$\hat{\gamma}_i = y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)$$

**Geometric margin** (actual Euclidean distance):

$$\gamma_i = \frac{y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b)}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$$

The **margin of the dataset** is the smallest geometric margin over all training points:

$$\gamma = \min_{i=1,\ldots,m} \gamma_i$$

### 2.2 Hard-Margin SVM — Primal Formulation

Assuming the data is **linearly separable**, the canonical optimization problem is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $\frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ is mathematically equivalent to maximizing $\frac{1}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}$ but yields a **convex quadratic** objective — easy to optimize. The constraint $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$ is the canonical (rescaled) form: every correct point must lie at geometric distance at least $\frac{1}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}$ from the hyperplane.

### 2.3 Soft-Margin SVM — Slack Variables

When classes overlap (the realistic case), introduce **slack variables** $\xi_i \ge 0$ to allow margin violations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{m} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The hyperparameter $C > 0$ balances **margin width** vs. **classification error**:
> - Large $C$ → heavy penalty on violations → narrower margin, fewer misclassifications (low bias, high variance).
> - Small $C$ → wider margin, more tolerated violations (high bias, low variance).

### 2.4 The Dual Problem (Lagrangian Formulation)

Construct the Lagrangian by introducing multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$ (for the margin constraints) and $\mu_i \ge 0$ (for the non-negativity of $\xi_i$). Setting $\partial L / \partial \mathbf{w} = 0$, $\partial L / \partial b = 0$, $\partial L / \partial \xi_i = 0$ gives the **dual**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The dual is preferred for three KTU-relevant reasons:
> 1. **Kernelization** — the data appears only as inner products $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$, replaceable with $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$.
> 2. **Sparsity** — most $\alpha_i = 0$; only support vectors have $\alpha_i > 0$.
> 3. **Efficiency** in high dimensions.

### 2.5 Karush-Kuhn-Tucker (KKT) Complementarity Conditions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_i \bigl[ y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \bigr] &= 0 \\
\mu_i \, \xi_i &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Consequences:
- If $\alpha_i = 0 \Rightarrow$ point is **not** a support vector (lies beyond the margin, correctly classified with slack $0$).
- If $0 < \alpha_i < C \Rightarrow$ point lies **exactly on the margin** ($0 < \xi_i = 0$); used to compute bias $b$.
- If $\alpha_i = C \Rightarrow$ point is a **margin violator** ($\xi_i > 0$); misclassified if $\xi_i > 1$.

### 2.6 The Kernel Trick — Non-Linear SVM

Replace every inner product with a kernel:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

where $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathbb{R}^D$ is an (implicit) feature map into a higher-dimensional space. The decision function becomes:

$$h(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$$

### 2.7 Mercer's Condition

A function $K$ is a **valid (positive semi-definite) kernel** iff the Gram matrix $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite** for all finite datasets.

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Decision function | $h(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}+b)$ | Output $\in \{-1,+1\}$ |
| 2 | Geometric margin (single pt) | $\gamma_i = \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b)}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}$ | Scale-invariant |
| 3 | Hard-margin primal | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ s.t. $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b)\ge 1$ | Linearly separable |
| 4 | Soft-margin primal | $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ | $C>0$, $\xi_i \ge 0$ |
| 5 | Dual objective | $\sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum\sum \alpha_i\alpha_j y_i y_j \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$ | Maximize w.r.t. $\alpha$ |
| 6 | Dual constraints | $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$, $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ | Box + equality |
| 7 | $\mathbf{w}$ recovery | $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ | Sum over support vectors |
| 8 | Polynomial kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma\, \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | Degree $d \in \mathbb{N}$ |
| 9 | RBF (Gaussian) kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\Vert^2\right)$ | $\gamma > 0$ |
| 10 | Sigmoid kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j) = \tanh(\gamma\,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | Valid only for some $\gamma,r$ |
| 11 | Linear kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i,\mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$ | $d=1$ polynomial |
| 12 | VC dimension of hard-margin | $D_{VC} \le \min(m_0, d) + 1$ | $m_0$ = number of SVs |
| 13 | Hinge loss | $L(y, f) = \max(0, 1 - yf)$ | Surrogate for 0-1 loss |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Bioinformatics:** Cancer classification from gene expression microarrays (high $d$, low $m$).
- **Text & NLP:** Spam filtering, sentiment analysis (linear SVM with TF-IDF).
- **Image classification:** Handwritten digit recognition (with RBF kernel).
- **Anomaly detection:** One-Class SVM for industrial fault diagnosis.
- **Remote sensing:** Land-cover classification from satellite imagery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A: From Geometric Margin to Quadratic Program

**Step 1 — Start with margin definition.**
We want every training point to be classified correctly with a margin of at least $\gamma$. This gives the constraint:

$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge \gamma \cdot \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$$

**Step 2 — Rescale to canonical form.**
Recall that scaling $(\mathbf{w}, b) \to (k\mathbf{w}, kb)$ does **not change the hyperplane** nor the sign of $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b)$, but rescales the margin. We are therefore free to fix the *functional margin* of the closest point to be exactly $1$:

$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1$$

**Step 3 — Translate "maximize margin" into an objective.**
The geometric margin equals $\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$. Maximizing $\frac{1}{\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert$, and for convexity we minimize $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **convex quadratic program (QP)** with linear constraints — a well-posed problem with a unique global optimum.

---

### 3.2 Derivation B: Lagrangian Dual for Soft-Margin SVM

**Step 1 — Form the primal Lagrangian.**
Introduce Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i \ge 0$ for margin constraints and $\mu_i \ge 0$ for $\xi_i \ge 0$:

$$L(\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}, \boldsymbol{\alpha}, \boldsymbol{\mu}) = \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum_{i=1}^{m} \xi_i - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i\bigl[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\bigr] - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero (stationarity).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \mathbf{w} &= \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i &= 0 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C - \alpha_i - \mu_i &= 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 \le \alpha_i \le C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back to obtain the dual.**
Plug $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ into $L$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_D &= \frac{1}{2}\sum_i\sum_j \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \,\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j \;+\; C\sum_i \xi_i \\
&\quad - \sum_i \alpha_i\Bigl[y_i\Bigl(\sum_j \alpha_j y_j \mathbf{x}_j\Bigr)^\top\mathbf{x}_i + y_i b - 1 + \xi_i\Bigr] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the stationarity relations $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0$, the terms collapse to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_D = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final dual problem.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation C: Kernelization

**Step 1 — Recognize the inner product.**
In the dual objective, the training data enters **only** through the inner product $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$.

**Step 2 — Define a feature map.**
Let $\phi: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{F}$ be a mapping into a (possibly infinite-dimensional) Hilbert space $\mathcal{F}$. Replace $\mathbf{x}$ with $\phi(\mathbf{x})$:

$$\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \;\longrightarrow\; \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

**Step 3 — Define the kernel function.**
A **kernel** is a function $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ such that:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_i), \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \rangle_{\mathcal{F}}$$

**Step 4 — Replace inner products by kernels in the dual.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Kernelized decision function.**

$$h(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}) + b \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Toy Example (2D → 3D with polynomial kernel of degree 2):**
> Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2)^\top$ and $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^2, \sqrt{2}\,x_1 x_2, x_2^2)^\top$. Then
> $$\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) = x_{i1}^2 x_{j1}^2 + 2 x_{i1} x_{i2} x_{j1} x_{j2} + x_{i2}^2 x_{j2}^2 = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)^2 = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$
> We computed an inner product in $\mathbb{R}^3$ using only operations in $\mathbb{R}^2$ — the kernel trick in action.

---

### 3.4 Derivation D: Recovery of the Bias $b$

Pick any support vector with $0 < \alpha_k < C$. By KKT complementarity, $y_k(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_k + b) = 1$. Therefore:

$$b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_k = y_k - \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_k$$

For numerical stability in practice, average $b$ over **all** such support vectors.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Computational Complexity

| Step | Time Complexity | Space Complexity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| QP solver (general) | $\mathcal{O}(m^3)$ | $\mathcal{O}(m^2)$ | Standard interior-point / SMO |
| SMO algorithm | $\mathcal{O}(m^{2.3})$ empirically | $\mathcal{O}(m)$ | Sequential Minimal Optimization — sklearn default |
| Kernel evaluation | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ per call | — | Pre-compute Gram matrix for linear |
| Prediction cost | $\mathcal{O}(\#SV \cdot d)$ | — | Sparse — only SVs are touched |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — SVM Training Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Training Data X y] --> B0[Preprocess and Scale Features]
    B0 --> C0[Choose Kernel K and Hyperparameters C gamma]
    C0 --> D0[Construct Primal QP Problem]
    D0 --> E0[Form Lagrangian with Multipliers alpha i]
    E0 --> F0[Stationarity Conditions on w b xi]
    F0 --> G0[Derive Dual Maximization Problem]
    G0 --> H0[QP Solver or SMO Loop]
    H0 --> I0[Recover Support Vectors with alpha i greater than 0]
    I0 --> J0[Compute Bias b from margin SVs]
    J0 --> K0[Construct Decision Function h x]
    K0 --> L0[Predict Labels for Test Points]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Decision Boundary with Margin Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Class plus 1 region] --- M1[Margin boundary wT x plus b equals plus 1]
    M1 --- H0[Hyperplane wT x plus b equals 0]
    H0 --- M2[Margin boundary wT x plus b equals minus 1]
    M2 --- P2[Class minus 1 region]
    SV1[Support Vector plus 1 on plus 1 boundary] -. lies on .-> M1
    SV2[Support Vector minus 1 on minus 1 boundary] -. lies on .-> M2
    SV3[Margin violator xi greater than 0] -. inside margin .-> H0
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Kernel Trick Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Input space X subset of R d] --> R0[Implicit feature map phi]
    R0 --> S0[High dimensional space F]
    S0 --> T0[Inner product phi xi T phi xj]
    T0 --> U0[Kernel function K xi xj]
    U0 --> V0[Dual objective and decision function]
    V0 --> W0[Non linear decision boundary in X]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block — KKT Region Classification of Training Points

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0[Training point xi yi] --> N1{alpha i equals 0}
    N1 -- yes --> N2[Correctly classified beyond margin]
    N1 -- no --> N3{alpha i less than C}
    N3 -- yes --> N4[On margin boundary support vector]
    N3 -- no --> N5[alpha i equals C]
    N5 --> N6[xi equals 0 and inside margin]
    N5 --> N7[0 less than xi less than 1 and inside margin]
    N5 --> N8[xi greater than 1 and misclassified]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
*Define the terms "Support Vector," "Margin," and "Maximum Margin Hyperplane" as used in a Support Vector Machine. Why are support vectors sufficient to define the optimal hyperplane?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Support Vector** is a training data point $\mathbf{x}_i$ whose corresponding Lagrange multiplier satisfies $\alpha_i > 0$ in the dual solution. Geometrically, it is a point lying *on* the margin boundary ($0 < \alpha_i < C$) or *inside* the margin / misclassified ($\alpha_i = C$).

The **margin** is the perpendicular distance from the hyperplane to the nearest training point: $\gamma = \min_i \frac{y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b)}{\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert}$.

The **Maximum Margin Hyperplane** is the unique hyperplane that maximizes this margin, equivalently solving $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b) \ge 1$.

Support vectors are sufficient because, from stationarity, $\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ — the weight vector is a *linear combination only of support vectors*. All other points have $\alpha_i = 0$ and contribute nothing. Hence, the entire classifier (both the hyperplane and the bias) is determined by these few critical points. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the concept of the "kernel trick" in SVM. State Mercer's condition for a valid kernel function.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **kernel trick** is a computational shortcut: rather than explicitly mapping data into a high-dimensional feature space via $\phi(\cdot)$ (which may be infinite-dimensional and infeasible), we replace every inner product $\phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ with a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ computed in the *original* input space. This enables learning non-linear decision boundaries without ever leaving the original feature space.

**Mercer's Condition:** A symmetric function $K: \mathcal{X}\times\mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a valid (positive semi-definite) kernel iff for every finite dataset $\{\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_m\}$, the corresponding **Gram matrix** $\mathbf{K}$ with entries $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ is **positive semi-definite**, i.e., $\mathbf{v}^\top \mathbf{K} \mathbf{v} \ge 0$ for all $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^m$. **(3 marks)**

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) norms: each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Students must answer *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a) Formulate the optimization problem for a soft-margin Support Vector Machine. Explain the role of the regularization parameter $C$ and the slack variables $\xi_i$. Distinguish between hard-margin and soft-margin SVM with their applicability. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **soft-margin SVM** optimization problem is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \boldsymbol{\xi}} \quad & \frac{1}{2} \Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{m} \xi_i \\
\text{subject to} \quad & y_i (\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x}_i + b) \ge 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \ge 0, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role of slack variables $\xi_i$:** Each $\xi_i$ measures the *extent to which* the $i$-th training point violates the margin:
- $\xi_i = 0$ : point is correctly classified and outside (or on) the margin.
- $0 < \xi_i \le 1$ : point lies *inside* the margin but is still correctly classified.
- $\xi_i > 1$ : point is **misclassified** (lies on the wrong side of the hyperplane).

**Role of the regularization parameter $C > 0$:** It controls the trade-off between **margin maximization** ($\frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$) and **training error** ($\sum \xi_i$).
- **Large $C$** → aggressive penalty on slack → narrower margin, fewer violations, **lower bias, higher variance** (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$** → tolerant of violations → wider margin, **higher bias, lower variance** (risk of underfitting).

**Hard-margin vs Soft-margin SVM:**

| Aspect | Hard-Margin | Soft-Margin |
|---|---|---|
| Constraints | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b) \ge 1$ | $y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i+b) \ge 1-\xi_i$, $\xi_i \ge 0$ |
| Slack variables | None | $\xi_i$ for each point |
| Objective | $\frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ | $\frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ |
| Data requirement | Strictly linearly separable | Allows overlapping / non-separable |
| Robustness | None (single outlier changes hyperplane) | Controlled via $C$ |
| Applicability | Toy/ideal datasets | Real-world (always used in practice) |

**(7 marks — full breakdown: 3 marks for formulation, 2 marks for role of $C$ and $\xi_i$, 2 marks for the comparison table)**

---

**(b) For a 2-class classification problem, derive the dual optimization problem of a soft-margin SVM using Lagrange multipliers. Clearly state the KKT complementarity conditions and interpret the three cases for $\alpha_i$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Primal Lagrangian.** Introduce $\alpha_i \ge 0$ for margin constraints and $\mu_i \ge 0$ for $\xi_i \ge 0$:

$$L = \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2 + C\sum_i \xi_i - \sum_i \alpha_i\bigl[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\bigr] - \sum_i \mu_i \xi_i$$

**Step 2 — Stationarity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} L = 0 &\Rightarrow \mathbf{w} = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = 0 &\Rightarrow \sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0 \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 &\Rightarrow C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \Rightarrow 0 \le \alpha_i \le C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into $L$** to eliminate $\mathbf{w}, b, \xi_i, \mu_i$. The dual becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\boldsymbol{\alpha}} \quad & W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{m} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{subject to} \quad & 0 \le \alpha_i \le C, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — KKT Complementarity Conditions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_i \bigl[y_i(\mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i\bigr] &= 0 \\
\mu_i \, \xi_i &= 0, \quad \mu_i = C - \alpha_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation of the three cases for $\alpha_i$:**

| Case | Value | Geometric Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| (i) | $\alpha_i = 0$ | Point is *not* a support vector; correctly classified, **outside** the margin. |
| (ii) | $0 < \alpha_i < C$ | Point lies **exactly on the margin boundary** ($\xi_i = 0$); used to compute $b$. |
| (iii) | $\alpha_i = C$ | Point is a **margin violator** ($\xi_i > 0$); inside the margin or misclassified. |

**Recovery of the model:**

$$\mathbf{w} = \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i, \qquad b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_k \text{ for any } 0 < \alpha_k < C$$

$$h(\mathbf{x}) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x} + b \right)$$

**(7 marks — full breakdown: 2 marks for Lagrangian setup, 2 marks for dual derivation, 2 marks for KKT conditions, 1 mark for case interpretation)**

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024]*

**(a) What is the kernel trick? Explain with examples how Polynomial and Radial Basis Function (RBF) kernels enable non-linear classification in SVM. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition.** The **kernel trick** is the replacement of every inner product $\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$ in the SVM dual (and decision function) by a kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^\top \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$, where $\phi$ is an *implicit* mapping to a high (possibly infinite) dimensional feature space. This allows SVMs to learn non-linear boundaries *without* explicitly computing $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.

**Polynomial Kernel:**

$$K_{\text{poly}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\gamma\, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$$

where $d \in \mathbb{N}$ is the polynomial degree, $\gamma > 0$ is a scaling factor, and $r$ is a constant. For $d=2$, this is equivalent to mapping $\mathbf{x}$ into the space of all monomials of degree up to 2 (e.g., $x_1^2, x_2^2, x_1 x_2$). Higher $d$ yields more flexible boundaries but also higher overfitting risk.

**Radial Basis Function (RBF) / Gaussian Kernel:**

$$K_{\text{RBF}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp\!\left(-\gamma \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j \Vert^2\right)$$

This corresponds to a feature map into an **infinite-dimensional** space. Intuitively, the kernel measures the *similarity* between $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$: it equals $1$ when the two points coincide and decays to $0$ as they move apart. The parameter $\gamma$ controls the locality:
- **Large $\gamma$** → narrow Gaussian → local influence → complex, wiggly boundary (overfit risk).
- **Small $\gamma$** → wide Gaussian → global influence → smoother boundary.

**Comparative Insight:**
- Polynomial kernels are useful when prior knowledge suggests the boundary has a specific polynomial shape.
- RBF kernels are a **safe default** for unknown non-linearities; they can approximate any continuous kernel on a compact domain (universal kernel property).

**(7 marks — full breakdown: 1 mark for definition, 3 marks for polynomial kernel, 3 marks for RBF kernel)**

---

**(b) Consider the dataset with points: $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,1)^\top, y_1 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1,-1)^\top, y_2 = -1$; $\mathbf{x}_3 = (-1,-1)^\top, y_3 = +1$; $\mathbf{x}_4 = (-1,1)^\top, y_4 = -1$. Solve the hard-margin SVM dual and identify the support vectors. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the dual.** For hard-margin SVM with linear kernel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\max_{\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3,\alpha_4} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{4}\sum_{j=1}^{4} \alpha_i \alpha_j \, y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j \\
\text{subject to} \quad & \alpha_i \ge 0, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{4} \alpha_i y_i = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Gram matrix $G_{ij} = y_i y_j \, \mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j$:**

Since $y_i y_j \in \{+1, -1\}$ and $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$ is just the dot product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G &= \begin{bmatrix}
+1(2) & y_1y_2(0) & y_1y_3(-2) & y_1y_4(0) \\
y_2y_1(0) & +1(2) & y_2y_3(0) & y_2y_4(-2) \\
y_3y_1(-2) & y_3y_2(0) & +1(2) & y_3y_4(0) \\
y_4y_1(0) & y_4y_2(-2) & y_4y_3(0) & +1(2)
\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}
2 & 0 & -2 & 0 \\
0 & 2 & 0 & -2 \\
-2 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\
0 & -2 & 0 & 2
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the equality constraint.** The data is symmetric: the natural solution has $\alpha_1 = \alpha_3 = a$ (positive class) and $\alpha_2 = \alpha_4 = b$ (negative class). The equality constraint $\sum \alpha_i y_i = a - b - a + b = 0$ is automatically satisfied.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the dual.** Using $G$ and the symmetry:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\alpha}^\top G \boldsymbol{\alpha} &= \frac{1}{2}\bigl[2a^2 + 2b^2 - 4ab + 2a^2 + 2b^2 - 4ab\bigr] \\
&= 2a^2 + 2b^2 - 4ab
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the dual objective is:

$$W(\boldsymbol{\alpha}) = 2a + 2b - 2a^2 - 2b^2 + 4ab = 2(a+b) - 2(a-b)^2$$

**Step 5 — Maximize.** By symmetry, $a = b$. Let $a = b = t \ge 0$. Then:

$$W(t) = 4t - 0 = 4t$$

The objective is **unbounded above** without an upper bound on $\alpha_i$ — this happens because the **data is not linearly separable in the original 2D space** (points $\mathbf{x}_1 = (1,1)$ and $\mathbf{x}_3 = (-1,-1)$ belong to class $+1$ but are *opposite* to $\mathbf{x}_2 = (1,-1)$ and $\mathbf{x}_4 = (-1,1)$ which are class $-1$). The label pattern is the **XOR** problem.

**Step 6 — Resolve via kernelization.** Apply a **polynomial kernel of degree 2**:

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = (\mathbf{x}_i^\top \mathbf{x}_j)^2$$

The XOR problem is solvable in the space $\phi(\mathbf{x}) = (x_1^2, \sqrt{2}\,x_1 x_2, x_2^2)^\top$. The class $\pm 1$ regions are separable by the hyperplane $x_1 x_2 = 0$ in the original space (the two coordinate axes). All four points become **support vectors** with $\alpha_i = \frac{1}{2}$ and the decision boundary is the union of the two axes — a non-linear boundary in $\mathbb{R}^2$ obtained via the kernel trick.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a classic KTU favourite: the **XOR problem** demonstrates that **linear SVMs in input space are insufficient for non-linearly-separable data** and motivates the kernel trick. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes reported by KTU board examiners:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$** in the objective $\frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ — losing 1 mark consistently.
> 2. **Writing $\alpha_i \ge 0$ for soft-margin SVM** — incorrect! It must be $0 \le \alpha_i \le C$ (box constraint). Hard-margin has only $\alpha_i \ge 0$.
> 3. **Confusing functional and geometric margin** — functional is not scale-invariant; geometric is.
> 4. **Saying "kernel maps data to higher dimensions"** without mentioning the **inner-product form** and the fact that we *never compute* $\phi$ explicitly.
> 5. **Treating $\gamma$ in RBF and $\gamma$ in the polynomial kernel as the same parameter** — they have different roles and ranges.
> 6. **Skipping the equality constraint** $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$ in the dual — a complete dual must include all constraints.
> 7. **In derivation Q's, jumping from Lagrangian directly to the dual** without showing the stationarity step.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ SVM is a **maximum-margin classifier**; the primal is $\min \frac{1}{2}\Vert\mathbf{w}\Vert^2$ subject to margin constraints.
- ✅ **Soft-margin SVM** introduces slack variables $\xi_i \ge 0$ and the trade-off parameter $C > 0$.
- ✅ The **dual** uses Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i$ and exposes data only as inner products — this is what enables kernels.
- ✅ **KKT conditions** classify points into: non-support ($\alpha_i = 0$), margin ($\alpha_i \in (0,C)$), violators ($\alpha_i = C$).
- ✅ **Support vectors** are the *only* training points that define the hyperplane; sparsity of $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ is SVM's defining feature.
- ✅ **Kernel trick:** replace $\mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j$ by $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ to learn non-linear boundaries efficiently.
- ✅ **Common kernels:** linear, polynomial $(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^\top\mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$, RBF $\exp(-\gamma\Vert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\Vert^2)$, sigmoid.
- ✅ **Mercer's condition** (positive semi-definite Gram matrix) is the validity test for any kernel.
- ✅ **Recovery:** $\mathbf{w} = \sum \alpha_i y_i \mathbf{x}_i$ and $b = y_k - \mathbf{w}^\top\mathbf{x}_k$ for any $k$ with $0 < \alpha_k < C$.
- ✅ The **XOR problem** is a textbook example showing the necessity of kernels for non-linear separability.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 Scheme: focus on **derivation of the dual**, **role of $C$ and $\xi_i$**, and **kernel function properties**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods: Bagging
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET413 - MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods: Bagging

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)** is an ensemble meta-algorithm designed to improve the stability and accuracy of machine learning algorithms by combining (aggregating) the predictions of multiple base learners, each trained independently on a different **bootstrap sample** (a sample drawn with replacement) of the original training dataset. It was formally introduced by **Leo Breiman in 1996**.

The term **Bagging** is a portmanteau of **Bootstrap** + **Aggregating**. Formally, given a standard training set $D$ of size $n$, bagging generates $B$ new training sets $D_b$ (for $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$), each of size $n'$, by sampling from $D$ uniformly and with replacement. By default, $n' = n$. These samples are called **bootstrap samples**. A base model $h_b(x)$ is then trained on each $D_b$. The final ensemble prediction is obtained by:

- **Majority Voting** (for classification tasks)
- **Averaging** (for regression tasks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Bagging is a parallel ensemble technique. Unlike boosting, the base learners in bagging are trained **independently and in parallel** — there is no sequential dependency between them. This is the single most important distinguishing feature for examination purposes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Jury of Doctors"**

Imagine you have a strange medical symptom, and you want an accurate diagnosis. Instead of visiting one doctor, you visit **100 different doctors** independently. Each doctor, however, sees a **slightly different set of patient case histories** (because each one is randomly given a stack of old cases — some cases appear multiple times, some never appear). This is the **bootstrap sampling** part.

After all 100 doctors give their independent diagnoses, the **majority opinion wins** (for a yes/no disease). This collective wisdom is much more reliable than a single doctor who might overfit to quirks in the cases they happened to see.

- **Bootstrap** = giving each "doctor" (base model) a randomly shuffled, slightly different set of past cases.
- **Aggregating** = taking the majority vote / average of all doctors' opinions.
- **Independence** = doctors do not share notes; they work in parallel.

This is exactly what bagging does with decision trees (or any other "unstable" base learner). The magic is that the **randomness in training data cancels out the noise in any one tree's prediction**, reducing variance without increasing bias.

## 1.3 Why Bagging Works — The Core Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bias-Variance Decomposition Framework**

A model's generalization error can be decomposed as:
$$\text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Noise}$$

**Bagging primarily targets the Variance component.** It is most effective on **unstable learners** — models whose predictions change significantly when the training data is slightly perturbed. Examples include:

- **Decision Trees** (especially deep, unpruned ones)
- **Neural Networks**
- **Regression Trees**

Stable learners (e.g., $k$-Nearest Neighbors, Naive Bayes, Linear Regression) gain very little from bagging, because their predictions don't fluctuate much with resampling.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Vocabulary for KTU Viva & Exams**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Base Learner** | The individual model (e.g., a decision tree) trained on one bootstrap sample |
| **Bootstrap Sample** | A sample drawn with replacement, same size as original dataset |
| **Ensemble Size ($B$)** | The number of base learners (typically 50–500) |
| **Aggregation** | Combining predictions via voting or averaging |
| **OOB (Out-of-Bag)** | The ~37% of original samples NOT selected in a particular bootstrap sample |
| **OOB Error** | A free validation estimate using OOB samples — no need for a separate test set |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bagging Algorithm (Breiman, 1996) — Logical Workflow**

**Input:**
- Training set $D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}$
- Base learning algorithm $\mathcal{L}$ (e.g., CART decision tree)
- Number of bootstrap samples / ensemble size: $B$

**For** $b = 1, 2, \ldots, B$:

1. **Bootstrap Sampling:** Draw a sample $D_b$ of size $n$ from $D$ **with replacement**.
   - Each sample $(x_i, y_i)$ has probability $1/n$ of being selected in each draw.
   - On average, ~63.2% of unique samples appear in $D_b$; the remaining ~36.8% form the **OOB set** for this iteration.
2. **Train Base Model:** Build a model $h_b(x) = \mathcal{L}(D_b)$.
3. **Store** $h_b$ in the ensemble pool.

**Output (Final Prediction):**
- For **classification**:
$$H(x) = \arg\max_{y \in Y} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{1}\big(h_b(x) = y\big)$$
- For **regression**:
$$H(x) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)$$

---

## 2.2 Why Bootstrap Sampling? The 63.2% Mathematical Property

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistical Property — Exam Favorite**

For a dataset of size $n$, the probability that a specific sample is **NOT** selected in a single draw is:
$$P(\text{not selected in 1 draw}) = 1 - \frac{1}{n}$$

For $n$ independent draws (since we sample with replacement, draws are independent):
$$P(\text{not selected in any of } n \text{ draws}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$$

Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n} = \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.3679$$

So, the probability that a sample **IS** included in a bootstrap sample is:
$$P(\text{selected at least once}) = 1 - \frac{1}{e} \approx 0.6321 = 63.2\%$$

And the probability it ends up in the **OOB set** is $\approx 36.8\%$. This is a foundational number — **memorize it for KTU exams**.

---

## 2.3 Mathematical Justification — Variance Reduction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Averaging Reduces Variance**

Assume we have $B$ independent base models, each with variance $\sigma^2$. If we average their predictions:
$$\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)\right) = \frac{\sigma^2}{B}$$

So variance shrinks as $1/B$ — a clean linear reduction.

**However**, bootstrap samples are NOT truly independent (they share ~63% of their data), so the **actual** reduction is somewhat less. The empirical result is still a substantial drop in variance, with bias virtually unchanged (because each tree has the same expected prediction as a single tree trained on the full data).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — All Formulas You Must Know**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bootstrap inclusion probability | $1 - (1 - 1/n)^n \to 1 - 1/e \approx 0.632$ | $n$ → large |
| 2 | OOB fraction | $(1 - 1/n)^n \to 1/e \approx 0.368$ | Unused in each tree |
| 3 | Classification aggregation (voting) | $H(x) = \arg\max_y \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{1}(h_b(x) = y)$ | Plurality vote |
| 4 | Regression aggregation (averaging) | $H(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} h_b(x)$ | Simple mean |
| 5 | Ideal variance reduction | $\text{Var}(\bar{h}) = \sigma^2 / B$ | Assumes independence |
| 6 | Realistic variance reduction | $\text{Var}(\bar{h}) = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}$ | $\rho$ = pairwise correlation |
| 7 | OOB error estimate | $\text{Err}_{OOB} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}(H_{OOB}(x_i) \neq y_i)$ | Free validation |
| 8 | Bias-Variance decomposition | $\text{E}[(y - \hat{f}(x))^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma_\varepsilon^2$ | Fundamental |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where Bagging is Deployed in Industry**

- **Medical Diagnosis:** Aggregating predictions from multiple diagnostic models for cancer detection (e.g., histopathology image classification).
- **Financial Credit Scoring:** Combining hundreds of decision trees trained on bootstrapped loan default data — banks like Capital One and JPMorgan use tree-based ensemble models.
- **Remote Sensing:** Land-cover classification from satellite imagery (where training labels are noisy).
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression classification (high-dimensional, small-sample data).
- **Cybersecurity:** Intrusion detection systems where adversarial noise is a concern.
- **Fraud Detection:** Aggregating weak detectors to robustly flag fraudulent transactions.

The most famous bagging-based algorithm is **Random Forest** (Breiman, 2001), which adds an extra layer of randomness — at each split, only a random subset of features is considered. Random Forest is, in fact, the **default go-to algorithm** in many production ML pipelines.

---

## 2.6 Bagging vs. Boosting — The Critical Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Exam Comparison Table**

| Aspect | Bagging | Boosting |
|---|---|---|
| **Training** | Parallel (independent) | Sequential (each model corrects previous) |
| **Main Goal** | Reduce **Variance** | Reduce **Bias** |
| **Sampling** | Bootstrap (with replacement) | Reweighting (no bootstrap) |
| **Base Learners** | Strong, complex (e.g., deep trees) | Weak, simple (e.g., shallow trees / stumps) |
| **Weight on Models** | Equal | Performance-weighted |
| **Sensitivity to Noise** | Robust (equal votes) | Sensitive (misclassified points are amplified) |
| **Overfitting Tendency** | Lower | Higher (if not regularized) |
| **Example Algorithms** | Random Forest, Bagged Trees | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost |

---
